title: Fun Filter; Use Sparingly
text: I have a gold streak filter that I use occasionally with my main lens. This is the first blue streak filter I have used and I use it with my most versatile (and newer) lens, and so far I am pleased with it. It's a specialty filter that is useful in certain situations. The idea behind this filter is that it has grooves in the glass that trap light and the result is a long, blue streak in the image when a light source is bright enough or is in the frame. This kind of filter is very similar to star filters that create 4-, 6- and 8-point stars, though this one creates just straight lines. When used properly this can cause some very dramatic effects in images, such as when photographing street lights or approaching cars at night. I do need to say, though, that this kind of filter can easily be overused if not thoughtful. When used a lot, the effect can get dull and be more of an annoyance; however, when it is sparingly used it can evoke that "oh wow!" type of response for the unexpected effect. This is a screw-on filter that will fit most standard lenses with threads. Make sure you select a filter size that is matched to your lens. I like this lens filter and the effect it brings to the table. It is a niche product that should be used sparingly, but when used at the right time it can create an eye-popping streak effect.

title: Beautiful smartphone and tablet / Good for first-time smartphone users
text: First, let me say that I purchased the Lucid 2 through Verizon, a fact that may be important to readers here who want to read reviews only of products purchased through Amazon. I am a very active Amazon customer but have a Verizon landline account; it was easy to move forward using my existing account. The Lucid 2 looks like a beautiful tablet. It offers two modes: a mode for the first-time smartphone user and a standard mode for the experienced user. I chose the starter mode since I had not previously owned a smartphone. The hardest part of the learning process was inserting the SIM card. You have to pop-open the back cover (also called the battery cover) using the same opening as the charger port. A new user does not want to damage the charger port, and it takes a little patience to be sure that you are handling the delicate SIM card and the removal of the back cover with proper care. After inserting the SIM card and battery, one is instructed to charge the battery. A blue light shows that the battery is charged. At a later time, you can add a battery charging screen (three are available and are stored within the smartphone) so that you can view the progress of the charge. There are video tutorials stored within the smartphone and a very fine series of help screens located at Verizon.com under Support. The smartphone feels very much like a tablet. I was able to watch movie previews by accessing the App (icon) to an online movie site. One of the main reasons that I wanted a smartphone was for weather-watching during hurricane season. There are various ways to use Apps (icons) that are basically shortcuts to features that you wish to use. The weather App also functons like a pop-up screen to give you instantaneous viewing of local time and temperature. My primary interest is the tropics, however, and I am able to access [...] easily. I will download the App from that site at a future time. By the way, you can use Voice to access sites on Google. You can also use Voice to display the name of a person or a telephone number (that you had previously entered); you can then touch the name or number and then send the call. Voice can be used for text-messaging as well. As a young senior (age 63), I really like these Voice features. I own a Canon digital camera and did not buy the Lucid 2 for photography. Those people who do use a smartphone for photography may wish to read reviews about this feature on Lucid 2; my understanding is that three or four other smartphones might be a better choice if photography is one of your priorities when buying a smartphone. I have read some online, professional reviews that criticize the Lucid 2 as being plain from a design perspective. I disagree. One reviewer -- whose opinion matches mine -- said that the Lucid 2 is gorgeous. It is. It is elegant and fun to use. UPDATE: I am having a lot of problems with freezing and lack of responsiveness. A LOT of problems.

title: UPDATED- (Kissed, Made Up) I LOVE This.. And I HATE This..  Let Me Tell You About It..
text: WARNING- This is a somewhat in depth review. If you prefer a summary, see section marked THE BOTTOM LINE ------------------------------------- EDITED Well, sure enough as soon as I posted this review, I made a discovery. The touchpad was driving me crazy but I found a work around. By using two fingers instead of the other settings, I have made peace with my touchpad. I am revising this rating to FIVE STARS because honestly, I love this little Chromebook. The touchpad was my only big frustration with it. Now that I have the work around with two finger scrolling and moving around, I am much happier. It is not what I am used to but it works great now. I just wanted to let you all know BEFORE reading my original review. ---------------------------- HERE IS THE ORIGINAL REVIEW ------------------------ I have always wanted to have a Chromebook. I have laptops, tablets, a screaming desktop for my high powered applications, but sometimes I just want a small laptop touchscreen hybrid just to surf the net now and then without all of the other bells and whistles. Let's take a look.. WHAT IS THIS LIKE? I am going to review this in sections. SIZE The size of this is fantastic. I LOVE it. It is sleek, easy to use, and handy. WEIGHT This is the perfect weight for what it is. I find it to be just right for what I want. KEYBOARD This is a bit hard to see during the day in certain light, but I find that I am a touch typist for the most part, so as long as my hands are in the correct places, I am not too overwhelmed with it. The click and pressure of the keys is surprisingly good with this. I have an easier time typing with this than I do with my laptop which is larger. I typiecally work best on a split keyboard (ergonomic) so the fact that I can type on this so easily surprises me- well SHOCK actually. TOUCHSCREEN This is the part that I expected to be frustrated with, but it works BEAUTIFULLY. I have had no problems with it. In fact, if it were not for the touchscreen, this little Chromebook would probably have been thrown right out the window - probably without opening the window first. That leads me to my next section.. TOUCHPAD OMG. If there was ever a touch pad that would drive a person to insanity, this is it. The touchpad itself is all sealed with the two mouse buttons encased under the covering. That means there is not a separate button, but you have to press on the lower corners of the touchpad to get then to work. That is not the problem. The touchpad can be set and reset, but OMG, it is so annoying that I have literally almost thrown the thing across the room on several occasions until I remembered it had touchscreen so I could override my choices by pressing right on the screen,, even though the keyboard was still engaged. If it were not for THAT feature, forget it. The side scroll bars are my biggest annoyance. If you try to get them to surface, then press on them, it takes so long that when you finally get it to engage, you think it will work. It doesn't. The pointer will not seem to engage with the scrolling button and it takes forever. Touching it onscreen works better. CHARGING This takes a few hours to charge. BATTERY LIFE I get about 4 or 5 hours out of this depending on what I am doing. POWER BUTTON You can simply shut the Chromebook to turn it off in pause mode, or you can physically press the power button to turn it OFF. The thing is, however, when you open the book, it turns itself right on with no power button necessary. I rather like that feature. USB AND JACK The USB and Jack ports work great. No problems there. RECHARGE CORD The cord is plenty long enough and the transformer at the end of the cord is a slim one with a non-polarized plug so you can turn it either way in an outlet. VOLUME The volume buttons are wonderful- right on keyboard and easy to use. Volume is decent and no vibrating until it is way too loud anyway. SCREEN QUALITY Other than being in love with the touchscreen, I love the quality of the screen images too. The colors are good, and the clarity is nice as well. Is it as nice as the high level Samsung tablet? Not quite, but it is a decent runner up to it. EASE OF USE I find that the set up of this Chromebook is really nice. I have had no problems with lag and the set up is intuitive for those who are not great at finding and reading instructions. I have had no problems running basic apps on here, as well as using other browsers. I have not experimented with the Linux side of things at this point. PURPOSE? This makes a great notebook to bring to classes, coffee shops. for quick browsing of the web, and in my case to help manage online websites and a small business when I can not access my desktop or laptop and my tablet is not big enough for what I want to do. This is perfect for that. THE BOTTOM LINE I have to say that I REALLY love this thing Despite my fury at the ever frustrating touch pad, I love so much more about this Chromebook that I am willing to overlook the touch pad idiocy- well until it acts up again. I love the touchscreen overriding ability and that saves the whole thing for me. This is great to travel with me when I need basic access to internet packed in a perfect sized "laptop". MY RATING I want to give this five stars, but honestly, the touchpad is so ridiculous that I had to knock a star off for that. The touchscreen overrides it easily without having to flip the keyboard back and I have to say that placates me every time I get frustrated. This is a great little unit to have and I really like how fast it loads and performs. It is way nicer than I expected and is perfect for what I want to use it for.

title: FINALLY!  A Faraday Bag The WORKS!
text: I have been wanting a Faraday bag for a while, but every time I try one out, it does not work- I still get calls or texts. Let's take a look at this one. WHAT IS THIS LIKE? This has an accurate photo with the listing. It zips across the top, has a lightweight nylon divider if you wanted to put two tablets in it, or even a couple of phones, and then it folds over with a tight velcro grip. HOW DID IT TEST? We used this while traveling and tried to put all sorts of texts and calls into phones while in the bags and every time they did not ring or receive anything at all until they were taken out. IS IT STICKY? This is not sticky, but it does have a slightly tacky feel from the fireproofing. THE BOTTOM LINE For a great Faraday bag that is also fireproof (we did not test that for obvious reasons) this is a good choice. It kept a tablet and two smart phones inside without a problem. Calls were blocked and it held while the entire time they were inside, no matter how close the phone was to them that sent to the ones in the bag. MY RATING FIVE STARS. I like this and will use it. I recommend.

title: Good concept- having difficulty with the application!
text: GorillaPods are generally respected in the photography world, but I can't bring myself to give a high rating based on reputation alone. I bought this product expecting to be wow-ed, but I find myself with an adequate product at best. Overall, this product will get the job done, but it may be a pain in the butt getting there. The magnets in the feet are a great idea, but if sometimes, if the weight of the video camera is too much, it will slide. What I hate is the head- the turning function doesn't work as well as it should. There is a button on the side that is supposed to release the adjustment arm (the arm you turn and tilt to move the head of the tripod), but when you push it, it doesn't re-engage to lock the arm like it should, so trying to tilt and pan is difficult. I find that I have to move the arm really slowly or else it will slip and the arm will move but the head of the tripod won't pivot. It is frustrating. Trying to fix my tilt and pan problem, I tried adjusting the tension- if I go tight, it is really hard to move the head, and makes video jerky. If I loosen, the arm doesn't lock in like it should and then it doesn't work. I will keep trying to get it right, because I really want to love this product, but for now, it is 3 stars at best. Also note- this will work great if you don't want to move the camera around!

title: worth the try, gorgeous clear display, ease of use, fits my 7" wrist,
text: It's pretty good, especially if you dont need a name brand. It has a beautifully clear display. Easy to install app and pairs well with no hassle. Has all the bells and whistles of name brand products. While I prefer fitbit due to familiarity. I havent put it entirely through its paces. I noticed that it was registering nearly same heart rate as my fitbit sense. I havent it worn it long enough to determine blood oxygen. There is still a lot to learn. The learning curve feels a little clunky compared to my fitbit. Has ability to connect to Alexa, take and make calls, music. Can make changes through app or scroll through the screen face. The watch band fits my 7" wrist and feels comfortable enough. Typical silicone band. It is attached via pins, so I imagine it is possible to swap it out but not sure. I may try some old bands to see if it is doable. I'd also prefer for it to have a soft protective cover (bumper guard) to protect screen and edges. I still prefer the ease of using a name brand, but this has a good looking interface. Main thing is to see how accurate it is. I think it has been fine for heart rate, steps, but will need more sleep time for seeing how well it tracks sleep and oxygen. I dont rate on price, but I comment. This is pretty reasonable, but it looks like there are many similar products for less. So compared to a name brand of similar features this is a bargain. Compared to other no name generics, this is competitive. Some are easily $20 less. I noticed the price on this vacillates. I think I got it for $70, and have seen it as low as 35-40.

title: As a backpack organizer, it fits my needs (currently).
text: I don't use this as a laptop sleeve. Instead, I got this because I thought I could use it as a backpack organizer. I carry various notebooks, writing, and drawing supplies with me. I'm not an artist by any means, but I like to journal using different media. Having them loose in my backpack didn't make it easy to find things or have everything in one organized location. This sleeve drastically reduced clutter and makes it faster and easier for me to journal. Currently, it holds my journal, a soft writing mat, my Sonnenleder pencil case, a few fountain pens, a whiteboard notebook, and some markers (dry-erase, Sakura, permanent). It's a tight squeeze, with the front pocket, but overall I have no worries of anything being damaged. In fact, I feel everything is more secure in this. I also love that there are pockets to tuck the handles; I use one only just to pull the sleeve out of my backpack. The front pocket sometimes gets caught in my backpack when I take it out. There's a bit of fabric on the cover of the front pocket so as to conceal the zipper; I wish there wasn't as much excess fabric, or at least some way to stick it closer to the sleeve (like some Velcro or something). But now, instead of looking for and taking out a bunch of things from my backpack, I just pull this out and everything is one, easy-to-locate space.

title: get those action shots and videos easily
text: When I got this waterproof hi-res 8mp/1080p photo action camera, I wasted no time removing it from its case and charging it up. It still had a bit of charge when it arrived, so I was able to view the LCD screen before I charged. When I inserted the charging cord, the camera automatically turned on, so I turned it off to get a quicker charge. I didn't have any difficulty removing the waterproof case (look closely and you will find detail markings for the different parts of the case and the camera). As soon as you turn on the camera, you are going to get a message on the LCD screen that there is not a micro SD card. The capacity of the micro SD that it takes is 32GB and you will need one to use the camera or video features. The LD screen gives you menus to personalize your picture or video taking experience. The casing is waterproof up to 200ft and if you are in the water and lose it, it does float. I am pleased with this and will be able to attach it to my full-face snorkel mask easily.

text: Got these for my daughter to use when she has screen time with the iPad or one of our phones. They come in a very nice box with a window display inside the front flap. Included inside were the micro-USB charging cable and the 3.5mm-3.5mm headphone jack--both made of threaded fabric. Very nice. The headphones didn't come pre-charged so we charged them right away. The sole LED on the 'phones turned red during the charge. Once they were done, we paired them first with my Galaxy S5--it was detected right away. Next we paired with our ancient iPad 2, which also detected the headphones immediately. My wife carried them to a doctor's visit and it paired flawlessly with her iPhone 7. They are very attractive as headphones; the sound is decent. About right for this price range. They fold for storage but ours didn't come with a carrying case of any kind (not sure if one is supposed to be included). The volume doesn't reach very high, but I guess since this is for our daughter anyway that's a non-issue for us, but still something to point out.

title: EXCELLENT PRODUCTS!!
text: SUCH A GOOD PRODUCT! I read the reviews on past models and it seems like this company really listens to their customers. (Some annoyances included only 1 charging cable and wistful dreams of these being solar so they didn’t have to be continually recharged, as well as a clock that offered both a 12 hour format and 24 hour format) This new model took those things into consideration and they produced an even better product. Now these DO come with 2 chargers and a plug so you can charge both sensors at once. This also means you can keep main unit plugged in all the time now, even if you need to charge a sensor or doorbell. It took more than 2 hours to initially charge sensors. They come 20% charged. Their light changes from red to green when ready. After charging, I had to adjust the sensitivity levels. You’ll need to unscrew the back plate (a screwdriver is included!!) and simply move the switch from low to high or in between, whatever your needs are. After putting back plate on again they are ready to mount. The initial set is already “paired” to main unit so you can change the chime thru the main unit easily. I chose 3 different sounds so I know what zone the movement’s in. Mounting screws are included. Though 3M outdoor strips would be heavy duty enough to hold these too if you didn’t want to drill holes. My sensors are about 300+ feet from my house. There’s no problems with reception. I used the “doorbell” as more of a panic/assist button. We live on acreage and sometimes yelling for help when you’re far past the home is impossible. So I mounted the doorbell on the barn and programmed a panic-inducing chime for it. I’m sure my kids will really like that. :/ Overall, this took about 20 minutes to install. The directions are very well laid out and the product comes with a two yr warranty. This combined with what appears to be excellent customer service means these sensors are totally worth it!

title: Watch Out for their Terms of Service
text: The device worked well for me, but after 4 months, they shut off my Magicjack for violation of terms of service. It took a bit of effort to find out what was violated and they claimed excessive use. There is no support number; you have to get support online only with live chat or from their knowledge base. There is a billing questions number and you can call that. That's the only way to get a live person, but they only want you to call it for billing questions. The problem is you have no idea what constitutes excessive use or when you might be exceeding their limits. The terms of service says if your usage exceeds 20 times more than the average customer or more than 50 calls a day you have violated the terms. The 50 calls is easy to quantify, but what about the 20 times more than the average? The terms also say it is not limited to just this criteria, so you have no clue what other criteria they might use to decide that you have had excessive use. There is no way to know what that is. Instead of just suspending your service for a while, they cut you off permanently with no recourse. You lose any remaining time you may have paid for. If you are a light phone user, MagicJack may be okay for you, but don't expect to be on the phone for a lot of time or they may cut you off for excessive use, with no recourse, since you must agree to the terms of service. I would never get this device again. Read those terms of service before you agree to use this device.

title: Amazing Device, But With Fierce Learning Curve
text: This SEAGATE WIRELESS PLUS 1TB MOBILE DEVICE WITH BUILT-IN WI-FI STREAMING is an amazing device, but it has a fierce learning curve. Usually I'm pretty good at figuring out new hardware and software, but I needed a lot of help from some geeky online friends in order to get this device up and running. Now that I've gotten it to work, I like it a lot. No matter where I am, the device communicates wirelessly with my mobile devices; it also allows me to connect to an available local network to check email or surf the web (with the usual risks that go along with connecting to a public or unsecured network). It also allows more than one mobile device to access it at the same time. It has a full terabyte of storage space, so it will hold a huge amount of music and videos for on-the-road entertainment. However, the device has a lot of warts. The worst is that it's extremely difficult to transfer purchased video content in formats that will be recognized by different mobile devices. The various problems I encountered are described in some detail below. ***BASIC SETUP. First, it's not a simple plug-and-play device. To set it up, you must (1) charge and turn on the drive (a charger that plugs into an electrical outlet is included); (2) download and install the Seagate Media app from iTunes (for Apple devices), the Amazon App Store (for Kindle Fires), or Google Play (for Android devices); (3) launch the Seagate Media app (which will enable you to connect to the device's Wi-Fi network signal); and (4) connect to the Seagate Media Wi-Fi network (which will drop your existing network connection--and give you a "disconnected" warning--and then connect immediately to the Seagate network). Once you're connected to the device's network, you can proceed with the tutorial; if you go through the tutorial before you're connected to the Seagate Media network, you'll be scratching your head, wondering why nothing seems to work. When you're properly connected, the sample videos run perfectly. ***TRANSFERRING CONTENT. That was the easy part. Now you face the truly daunting task of transferring your own content to the device. Some things, like your personal documents and photos, can be copied directly from your computer with no problems. Although it's possible to connect your computer to the Seagate Media Wi-Fi network and copy files by wireless, it's much easier and faster to connect the device physically via USB cable to your PC or Mac and then copy the files. The included USB cable has a wide specialized plug that connects to one end of the device--there's a little "fingernail-slit" on the back, near the bottom, that allows you to remove the end cover and get at the port. Once you've copied some files, you can disconnect the device from your computer, and access the copied files wirelessly from the device, using the Seagate Media network and app. The app is sometimes glitchy, and navigation isn't particularly intuitive, but it works. The app lets you access files, stream video and music, and connect to any nearby wireless network to check your email or surf the web (without disconnecting from the Seagate Media network). ***PLAYING PURCHASED VIDEOS. However, there's a big problem when it comes to playing commercial movies or DVDs. The ads for this device suggest that you can copy your video files from your PC or Mac, and then use the Seagate Media app to stream them to your mobile devices (e.g., iPad, Kindle Fire). This may be true of your home movies, but it's certainly not true for purchased videos from Amazon, iTunes, or DVD downloads. In order to play these purchased videos, you must have the appropriate player available on the device you're using to access the video. (In fairness, it's the Digital Rights Management add-ons for the videos that is making things difficult, but the Seagate ads are misleading.) It's my understanding that you can use specialized software to convert .mp4 files (probably illegally) to file formats that can be streamed, but .mp4 files won't play if the ones that work with computer media players are simply copied to the Seagate device. As for movies purchased from Amazon, so far I've been unable to stream mine, because Amazon movies require the "Unbox" app. However, I was able to copy movies purchased from iTunes from my PC and stream them from the device to my iPad. I did have to download one movie to the iPad first, which apparently installed the necessary player software. ***IF YOU BUY THIS DEVICE. When you're ready to set it up, DO go to the Seagate web site, and download the complete User's Manual (available as a .pdf). A search for "Wireless Plus" should get you to the product's rather extensive support pages. Most end-users will want the additional information about using the device, even if they're able to get set up with only the brief "quick start" sheet that comes in the box, plus the tutorial that introduces the Seagate Media app. DO use the Seagate Media app to set up a password for your device. If you don't use a password, then anyone who detects your Seagate Media signal and also has the Seagate Media app on his/her own device will be able to access your device and content. DON'T expect to be able to "wirelessly stream all your videos, music, photos and documents to your tablet and smartphone", as the box claims--at least not right away and not without a lot of work. Some of your videos may not be streamable, and some may not be streamable to all of your mobile devices. (If it's possible to copy a purchased Amazon video for streaming to anything, I certainly don't know how it's done.) ***CONCLUSION. Because I'm a gadget person who loves fooling around with computer hardware, I like this device a lot--particularly the compact size (3-1/2" x 5") and sleek metallic-gray case. However, because the SEAGATE WIRELESS PLUS 1TB MOBILE DEVICE WITH BUILT-IN WI-FI STREAMING is so challenging to set up and use, I rate it at only 4 stars ("I like it" on the official Amazon scale).

text: This recent purchase is a backup keyboard and I'm not using it yet because the coffee I spilled did not affect the performance of the old keyboard. Pros: the ergonomic layout is better than most split keyboards; it is super quiet--I interview people on the telephone and type while they are speaking--with my former keyboard, people would ask what all the noise was and could I hear them okay--this keyboard eliminates that distraction completely; battery life is long. Overall, I recommend the keyboard if you are looking for a quiet and ergonomic keyboard.

title: Its just OK, there are better
text: I understand the need to have a dedicated media player at times. However, much like others I have used, this uses a very barebones UI that is laggy and feels very outdated compared to a smart device. The font has hard lines that are sometimes hard to read, and occasionally you'll even meet elements of the UI that have parts written in Chinese characters. And the video playback option is a joke- you can only play videos in a specific codec that is archaic and unreliable (and needs a bizarre app to encode to). It has an Fm tuner which is nice, and the size is super portable and cute. And honestly, this would be a decent device if I didn't know that for around the same price you can get much more polished media players powered by a modified smartphone OS. For example, another media player that I recently tried actually had a linux/Android kernel running under the hood, but stripped of all the smartphone parts. It supported video playback of modern formats, had more more pleasing fonts and a UI that was fast and responsive. I mean, in this day and age, unless this were half the price, there's no excuse for a UI to feel so far behind.

title: Good choice for a first tablet
text: Although I've been mucking around on a computer for many years now, this was my first venture into the tablet venue. I wasn't really sure what to buy. Do I go all out, buy a larger one with all the bells & whistles for the price of a base model laptop? Or do I stick with something simple & inexpensive? I decided to go with the Samsung Galaxy Tab 2. The price was a definite consideration, I liked that it was under $200. I preferred to stick with name brand, as I've had too many friends who went with an inexpensive brand that broke within a few months. The size seemed sufficient for me to play some of the fun apps & keep notes for school. It's been around 6 months since I first bought the tablet, and I'm overall pleased with it. I do wish it had two things. More storage, because the OS takes up a great deal of space. In hindsight, I would have paid more to have 16GB rather than 8. Also, I wish the charging port was a micro USB instead of 30 pin data port. My husband's Samsung Note has the micro USB and so does my daughter's Nexus 7. It would be really nice to be able to use other charging cords like they do (my husband can use his phone charger) than having to make sure I don't EVER misplace this one. But for a simple tablet from a trusted brand, I do think the Samsung Galaxy Tab 2 is a good choice.

title: The solution I needed
text: I have gone through a few mounts for my Samsung Tab 3 10.1, only to be disappointed each time. My main use was to mount to a microphone stand, but none - including the [[ASIN:B00BMUARHI Gator GFW-UTL-TBLTMNT Microphone Stand Tablet Mount]] - would hold it securely when in a case. In fact, the Gator did not fit it at all, which was the catalyst for me to give up and seek another solution. This mount works perfectly. True, I had to settle for a camera tripod, but even that turned out for the better because tripods are inherently more stable than microphone stands, and also with pan heads allow me a lot more flexibility in placement and viewing angle. What I really love is this mount will work with the two cases I use with my Tab 3: a [[ASIN:B00LIQJ1DW Samsung Galaxy Tab3 10.1-Inch Case Cover]] (my favorite and one the Gator case absolutely would not accommodate) and a [[ASIN:B00E8C8NCI Fintie Folio Slim Leather Case]], that did allow me to use with the Gator mount, but the fit was so tenuous that I never trusted it. Much to my surprise this mount also securely holds my [[ASIN:B00GJ3MF9Q Verizon Ellipsis 7 4G LTE Tablet, Black 7-Inch]] tablet, so if I need to use that too, this mount will fit it. I am so happy with this mount that I am purchasing a spare. As an FYI, it also holds my iPad 2, so it's truly versatile. Plus it beats any microphone stand mount that I have tried, and I have checked out a half dozen of those.

title: solar charge actually works, good assortment of features,
text: [[VIDEOID:17a58332b933800fe99b76e58398768d]] We have a few of these emergency radios. I am the main prepper in the household, but even my husband bought one at random recently. He showed me his, then I showed him mine. Then I got this one to add to the collection. Different brands, all very similar. The one feature that STANDS OUT to me on this FORTECLEAR is that there is a light that indicates that it is being charged. I can see it glow brighter when using the hand crank, as well as when I have it sitting in a ray of sunshine. If in the sun with the light glowing, once I cover the solar panel with my hand, the indicator turns off. Really nice to see that working. Like others, there is also an indicator that shows about what percent of power is has (25%, 50%, 75%, 100%) which will light up when it is being actively used. Really nice to be able to see this important info. For that I recommend this as an emergency item (or even casual use, why not?) Has typical features such as flashlight (low beam, high beam, and flashing), has a flip up "reading" light, and radio bands such as alert, WB, FM, and AM. Nice long antennae, knob tuning, knob volume control. USB port, USB C port, comes with a cord & instructions, wrist strap, has SOS alarm- which requires pressing for a couple of seconds to activate, SO much better than another where I have accidentally triggered it by a simple short press. Scared myself. LOL. SO LOUD. When looking for weather band, you may need to walk around to get a signal. I find this true of all emergency radios, or any radio with WB/NOLA option. As with most all other radios, FM comes in the best, the easiest. I am a big fan of having multiple options for lighting- whether it be solar, battery operated, internal battery that can be charged, etc. This has the ability to be charged, to be hand cranked, and to be solar charged. Nice to have a few options in one device. I also keep regular flashlights, rechargeable flashlights, battery powered radios, etc on hand. Multiple options for when it is needed. But this will be one I will rely on. I would get it again, and I would get this for someone else. I tend to give functional gifts, stuff that most folks dont want to buy for themselves (preps, fire extinguishers, smoke alarms, car care kits, etc), and this would be a good one to give. Really pleased that it works so well because it happens to be a few bucks more than most options out there. I will continue to play around with this. If I think of anything else, or experience anything else (good or bad) I will be sure to come back to update. So far so good!

title: Downgrade from 5 stars to one star.
text: Update. April 12, 2014. 4 months after purchase. I have unplugged the TIVO, will pay the monthly fees for the next 8 months. At least I'll save the $ 3 month cable card fees. I got another tuner. The tuner works. I have 2 TIVOs and the new tuner works in the old TIVO, but not the new Roamio. It is a Roamio problem. I now only get 4 standard def stations with the Roamio. TIVO offered to replace the Roamio for a flat $ 49, plus shipping. I figure the total cost will be $ 75. I'm sure I will get a refurbished TIVO. Decided it wasn't worth spending $ 75 to get a refurbished TIVO, which may or may not work. Since my ROKU 3 gives me Time Warner Demand channels, it is much less compelling to have a TIVO. I'll keep the TIVO Premiere connected as it has a lifetime and is trouble free, The Time Warner demand capability of the ROKU 3 is a major threat to TIVO's existence. I did purchase the TIVO on Amazon with the AMEX credit card which doubles the warranty. I called AMEX. Was told it would not cover the cost of the replacement ($ 49). Only would cover the labor cost, since parts were still under the TIVO warranty (90 days labor, 1 year parts). Since labor isn't separately charged, I'm not covered. Think that stinks. Update. 105 days later. Well warranty is up by 15 days. TIVO doesn't recognize tuning adapter. My old premiere works perfectly. Time Warner spent 2 hours at my home today, swapped out cable cards and tried 3 different tuning adapters. It's a TIVO issue. I bypassed tuning adapter for a direct hookup. Gives me half the channels. When my one year committment is up, I'll scrap it. Only saving grace. When I called to cancel my 13 month old Premier XL, they offered me a lifetime for $ 199. I took it. Do not recommend it Roamio. I was one of the original TIVO users. A 45 hour standard definition TIVO. Very disappointed. Original review. Integrated Wi Fi. It's about time. This is my 4th TIVO. Currently paying for 2 monthly subscriptions. Have been satisfied with all my TIVOs. Getting rid of the $ 90 Wi Fi accessory is a big plus. Our other TIVO is a 2 tuner Premiere XL that holds 300 hours of HD. We've never had it over 25% , so 75 hours is enough for us. Our other TIVO has been moved to the bedroom. We really needed at least a 4 tuner set. Two tuners may seem enough, but the stations are now playing it cute by ending programs at 9:01 instead of 9. This causes a conflict and forces you to cancel a show, or not watch a live station at the same time. It effectively reduces the two tuner to a one tuner. Before your TIVO arrives, go to your cable source (if you use cable), and get the appropriate cable card for a 4 tuner set, and tuning adapter. TW charges me $ 2.80 each for the cards. One for each TV. You need to call your cable company to activate and pair your cable card. TW has a great system, and it took only minutes to do. You also need to have an HDMI cable on hand, and a USB to HDMI cable. The kind that connects from your wired printer to your computer. I also pay monthly, not lifetime. On my original TIVO, an old 45 hour SD job, I took a $ 199 lifetime plan after 2 months. Three weeks later, a nearby lightning strike wiped out the modem on my TIVO, and my computer. Yes, I did have surge protectors, as well as a $ 300 whole house surge protector sold and installed by my electric company. End of my TIVO, end of my $ 199 lifetime plan. TIVO offered no sympathy. So TIVO yes. Lifetime plan NO. You cannot stream to your mobile devices with the 4 tuner TIVO, without adding accessories. Added. You also need a short coaxial cable. My prior TIVO packages included every possible cable you may need. No longer. The power supply, remote, remote batteries, and the TIVO were all that were included. An added explanation on the usb needed. The standard side of the USB connection connects to the TIVO. The printer type side connects to the tuning adapter. I went to my junk box, and pulled an old printer cable out. It's not the older type with the large ends. It is a very small odd sided type that plugs in. The cable company should be providing these. Ask them for one.

title: Simply in LOVE
text: My first kindle was the Kindle Keyboard and I only used it as a pure reading device: no text to speech, no browser, very little highlighting to dictionary search (latter two options were not thatneasynon the keyboard) and unlike K1 and K2 reviews my key board did not wear out as I rarely used it other than create collection. Now, after playing with my new 3G-WiFi. With ads - I am simply in love with this pure reading device. It is everything I wanted and here is why I am in love: 1. The touch aspect to create new collection folders was so easy and then moving the books into them was intuitively easy (much easier than the KK,which was frustratingly slow); 2. Set up was real easy as the step by step guide was really helpful; 3. Moving some of my previously purchased books from the cloud to the new kindle was so easy, simple tap multiple books and when you switch back to the home page, they were downloaded really fast! Wow. I was worried this transfer was going to be a painful process, woohoo - so not the case. 4. Light adjustment was easy and reading in a dark room was easy and no eye strain!!! Super whoop whoop. (update: I am in double of getting sleepless nights! The reading in the dark was sooo comfortable, and I couldn't stop reading! Wow. Love it). 5. The touch screen was so easy to use and very intuitive and I do like the fonts selection! 6. Now has a little clock on top right wephen you are on the home page so I can check when to start cooking dinner! 7. There is finally a password (numbers only) at last to protect access to your kindle!!! 8. Great size and easy to hold in one hand and easy to touch and move the page, which was nice and responsive. 9. Shop kindle is the same as is the location of the ads, which I do not find intrusive as it only appears when your screen is asleep and not when you are reading! Had no issue with it with my KK, and no issue with the ads now! 10. I upgraded to the 3G- i had to goto the lobby that would prompt the 3G- nice!!! My KK only had wifi. 11. Extra pixel, you certainly notice it against the KK! But your brain and yes adjust very quickly so it may seem like nothing, but relatively, the improvement is noticeable! Yes, I understand why some people are not happy with the light projection, as there are four lights projected from the bottom of the screen, which does create a little teeny weeny shadow. It is not so annoying at all, not for me and it Does not interrupt the reading experience since the wee shadow do not affect the text. Just my experience. All round for me, this kindle is everything I want in a kindle! I am soooooooo happy and yes, the upgrade, for me, from a KK was worth it. I am in love!

title: Good concept, not so good device
text: I bought and installed the Pogoplug on my LAN for the purpose of sharing photos over the Internet. Right out of the box I had misgivings. There is no documentation or technical specifications. That immediately raised serious questions in my mind about it being vulnerable to hacking or opening access to my computer and personal information. You have to attach it to your modem/router and allow it to go out on the Internet without knowing what it is doing. I decided to take it on faith that the company was legitimate and not going to install a backdoor into my system. I attached a Seagate USB external hard drive as my main memory device. I have also used USB memory sticks for temporary storage. All are USB 2.0. Initially I had issues with people accessing my files. The Web access used by outsiders does not show any indication of activity. If you have a couple of hundred photos like me, it can take a couple of minutes for anything to appear. I have also had people report 404 and other errors fairly frequently. My workaround was to create more folders to compartmentalize everything. They load quickly enough for people to see something happening and know that they are connected. Once you get inside the folders, people see a filmstrip and a large image like they would see in Windows. It is a serviceable but not glamorous interface. It is also not customizable. Once I established that people could access my photos over the Internet, the next step was to fill up the hard drive. To do so, my plan was to push the files over my 10/100 MBps LAN. The Pogoplug shows up as a drive, so all I have to do is drag-and-drop. The problem is that it is incredibly slow. I normally get about 30 kBps. That is dial-up modem slow. This exposed the second big problem. Remember the total lack of documentation? I had no FAQ, troubleshooting guide, technical specifications or other information to go on. I had to contact technical support via the Pogoplug web site. The Web site is slow and not always working, but I did get to someone, and they gave me the obvious suggestions like turning everything off and back on, dismounting and remounting the drive, trying a different drive, etc. After several iterations, I still had a slow connection that was now unreliable, a bunch of tech talk gibberish on ports and processes to open and close, and a closed out support ticket. Doing more research, this appears to be a known and frequent problem. It is a deal breaker for me. I am going to be looking for a replacement. As an interim solution, I will attach the USB HD directly and transfer my files on an as-needed basis. However, I am looking for an alternative now. So, to summarize, the concept is great, the implementation is okay, but the tech support and the LAN connection are not good. If you are serious about file sharing, this is not the device for you.

text: I was hoping for better. I have gigabit fiber internet, and wanted to upgrade from the Actiontec router that I bought from the ISP. Unfortunately, this ER4 doesn't cut it as an upgrade. It is a pain to configure: the web gui is clunky at best, and advanced options require using a command-line interface through SSH. Even updating the firmware is more pain than it should be (you have to download from web and upload from computer). But all that would have been acceptable if I'd seen any significant advantage over the decent-but-not-stellar router I've been using. There isn't any. For my home office & home streaming/voip/web needs the Actiontec is a tad unsatisfactory but just as good as this one, at less than half the price. And it doesn't seem to be all that great for the next level up either: if I wanted a true small-business router I'd look for something more capable (and easier to manage!). Ubiquiti seems to be letting the USG/ER product line languish, at least at the lower end. Maybe they've decided that it's not a profitable segment for them.

title: Guess I got a bad one...

Being the first prime lens I got for my T2i, I didn't suspect anything at first. After about 3 months, the AF stopped working entirely. I sent it off to Canon to fix under a warranty claim and they had it back to me in about 3 weeks. From that point, it worked for about another 8 months before reverting back to the same state of disrepair. I resolved to just use MF on it, however, just having this lens attached to my camera nearly instantly drains even a full-battery....must be some kind of short in it. Regardless of the failed mechanics and weird behavior, the glass was good and I really liked the way it handled indoor light....great colors and warm tone, nice FOV. Nonetheless, this lens is basically worthless to me now and just sits in the top of my closet. Because of this experience, I am very weary to invest in more Canon lenses and have since only purchased Sigma lenses, with mixed emotions there as well. One day....I'll get a good one...hopefully.

title: A big improvement over the Smokin' Buds
text: When my iPod buds finally died on me, I decided I wanted in-ear 'phones. However, I was short on cash, so I decided against the Shures, Etymotics, and the like. I saw the Smokin' Buds in a store for $[...], and gave them a shot. First off, the sound wasn't terrible. It was at least on par with the Apple buds. I had problems with the fact that the sleeves wouldn't stay put in my ears for any reason, slipping out almost as soon as I'd inserted them. In addition, the cord was rather thin, and anytime it rubbed against my clothing, or heaven forfend, my jacket's zipper, it would create enough noise to drown out my music. The nail in the coffin was the small chrome ring on the left bud. I had foolishly thought it was structural, but when it popped loose it turned out to be merely ornamental. I took them back for a refund, and sent off a letter to the company expressing my dissatisfaction with their product. Much to my surprise, I was invited to try another pair, with the promise that improvements had been made and I was sure to find them more to my liking. The buds that arrived in the mail were the Full Metal Jackets. The difference in construction is night and day. Where the bulk of the Smokin' Buds is plastic, these are aluminum. The sleeves never slip from my ears. The cord is far more durable, and thicker as well. The sound was quite boomy when I first tried them, but I let them run at high volume for 24 hours on static to loosen them up. This helped quite a bit, and the sound continues to improve. It's still bottom-heavy, but turning off the EQ function on my nano helped balance it out. I really like that I can run my nano at half volume and still hear everything clearly. In fact, I would caution against running them at full volume while they're in, as I'm certain one would suffer hearing damage. My battery life has improved tremendously. I'm very happy with the sound and the comfort of these buds. I can't think of a better set of earphones for the price... and the warranty is first-rate. 9 February 2008: I sent my FMJ's back for a warranty repair, which turned out to be a warranty replacement. The jack on my 9mm FMJ's had developed a short, and was causing the left 'bud to cut in and out. I received an RMA number within moments of contacting them at their website, [...], and while I paid for shipping the 'buds back, they replaced them immediately, free of charge. Not only did they replace them with a new set, they sent the 11mm FMJ's. The jack is a bit longer, in order to provide better strain relief, I suppose. Otherwise, the build quality is identical. More importantly, the sound is far better balanced top to bottom, and while the bass is still great, it no longer dominates the sound spectrum as it did on my 9mm's. In short, the 9's are really good, and you can't get anything better (in my opinion) at the price, but the 11's are worth the extra money. All the things that made my experience such a positive one with the 9's is improved with the 11's. Definitely a 5-star rating.

title: Great feel, easy to connect and decent battery life
text: I received the universal model as a review sample and tested on a few devices. Those were a Samsung Galaxy Note 3 phone, a Samsung Tab 3 10.1 and iPad 2 tablets. It paired and connected to each effortlessly and without going through a bunch of technical steps like the earlier generation of bluetooth keyboards. I pressed the connect button on the keyboard, scanned for it in my devices and - viola! - they paired and connected at a touch of my finger. I love it when stuff is that simple. The key feel has the tactile feedback that sets this apart from cheaper keyboards, In fact, I would say that the feel is equal to the one on my ThinkPad T61 laptop. It's about 9 3/8 inches wide, which gives a lot of typing space. A point of comparison is my laptop's keyboard is about two inches wider. Because this has an aluminum casing it is not going to creep away while you are typing, and it also keeps the keyboard from flexing when you are handling it. Again, some other keyboards that I have used were flimsy and did not last very long. One final thing I love is the micro USB connector for the power cable is sturdy. I actually had a keyboard from another brand that failed because when I pulled out the charging cable the internals of the port came out with it. I have been using my keyboard for over eight straight hours, untethered from a charging cable, and it's still going strong. In fact I am writing this review with it to test it. This is a five star product in my opinion because of construction, feel, and the fact that it does everything the specs claim for less than twenty bucks (at the time of this review.)

title: Excellent sound, easy to set up, and a great price!
text: The Onkyo HT-S3910 5.1-Channel Home Theater Receiver & Speaker Package is a nice home surround system at an affordable price point. The set up is very easy, literally, just plug and play. I like that it's a complete system with 2 front, 2 rear speakers, 1 center speaker and a subwoofer. It also comes with the speaker wire. The speaker wires are thin, but this is a home theater surround sound system, not a techno night club sound system, so the wire size is just fine. The wire is also colored white so it is easily concealed along the baseboards, if your walls are white that is, but the wires being thin also helps conceal them. The ends of the wires are soldered, which keeps the wire from fraying and it makes them easy to plug in to the speakers and receiver ports. Another thing I like is that you can plug (HDMI) your cable box, video game console, DVD player, and Fire T! V/Box etc... into the Onkyo receiver and you just have to run one HDMI cord from your Onkyo receiver to your TV to play any of them. You don't have to run a lot of individual wires to the back of your TV to hook up all your electronics. You might have to run one more cord from your receiver to the TV for sound, it depends on if your TV is an ARC enabled set. That means the TV has an ARC HDMI plug which receives sound and video through the HDMI cable, not just video. If not, you can run a sounds cable, it not hard to do. I also love that it has bluetooth capability, so I can connect to the receiver with my cellphone and play my music through the sound system. Set up is really quick and easy, I would recommend the Onkyo HT-S3910, it great for small rooms, dorm/bedroom, in a living room or in a home theater room. This isn't the top of the line theater systems, but it is very good quality surround theater system.

title: You really need this remote
text: Like so many others with multiple remotes, I view this unit as a very attractive alternative to a sixpack of remotes. A single button turns on (in sequence) my Sony HDTV, Yamaha receiver, and FIOS DVR/receiver. Or, alternatively, the Sony, receiver, and Sony DVD - depending on the selection. It even switches the input on the receiver appropriately. A single button turns off whatever units are "on". And one of the best features is the ability to re-program the remote via the Logitech website if/when something is upgraded or replaced. The only downside is that (obviously) a "universal" remote can't select EVERY option that may be available with the manufacturer's OEM unit. But those options tend to be little used ones. Another peeve is that sometimes I must shake the unit to get the display to light up. Otherwise, I don't see how most folks can get along without one. I really like mine. UPDATE (4/3/2013) I don't know if anyone will be interested due to the original date of this review, BUT just to show the utility of this remote, I thought I'd post this update. I recently replaced the previously mentioned Yamaha receiver with a new Denon unit and the Sony DVD was replaced by a Sony Blu-ray AND I added a Roku streaming box. I went to the Logitech site and created new profiles using the new equipment and now I control all of the new units just as well as it had controlled the original units. So, the point is - the remote can grow with your system when you change or add new equipment. Pretty great, huh?

title: Amazing sound, high quality & easy controls
text: The 1st thing I noticed about these headphones when I received them (other than the amazing box they came in) are that they are noticeably heavier than my other headphones. I take that as an indication of higher quality speakers. They do sound amazing. I listened to an album I have heard dozens of times before in the car & on earbuds & could hear elements I have never picked up on before. I actually thought I was listening to a different version of the song. I expected an exaggerated bass like I get with some earbuds but the sound is straightforward. The noise cancelling seems about the same as my much cheaper headphone set but both are very good & make a world of difference on a plane or other noisy situations. Pairing is practically automatic on Apple devices but it is also easy for my Android phone. I think we have 3 or 4 devices currently paired to this set. The headphones turn on as soon as they are unfolded. This is a cool feature but it means they are always on if they are worn around the neck. The battery lasts a long, long time. One charge played movies & noise cancelling for 7 hours of flying, probably another hour of music plus at least 4 more hours of just noise cancelling because someone forgot to close them until they were down to 2% The volume & play /pause are easily controlled by tapping the right ear cuff. The charge port is the robust Apple lighting cable type which was a surprise to me until my daughter told me that Apple owns the Beats brand. The carry case is much bigger than the folded headset. At first glance it doesn't look thick enough to provide good protection but pinching the material reveals that it has a thin, soft layer of foam or something inside. The zipper is the waterproof type which is a nice. The case comes with a hang tag & carabiner. The headphones were too tight for my XL head. My ears were sore after long days on airplanes. I extended the cuffs all the way to reduce the tension which helped a little but made them harder to wear. The ear cuffs are a little smaller than another pair I have & I like the larger cuff because I can move them around a little on the ear for comfort while still getting a good 'seal' on my ear. I ended up giving these to my daughter & she absolutely loves them. She is very is proud to have the Beats brand which isn't so important to me.

title: Clear Sound, But Fumbly Controls, Hard-to-Read Instructions
text: At the price (about $40 on Amazon), I expected more from these PIONEER ACTIVE IN-EAR WIRELESS HEADPHONES WITH INTEGRATED CLIP (Red SE-CL5BT(R)). The sound is very clear, which is obviously the most important thing. However, I find the controls (3 tiny, flat buttons) to be fumbly and difficult to use. The fact that the buttons have dual functions (e.g., one button increases the volume when pressed once, and advances music to the next track when pressed and held) is partly to blame. However, the buttons seem slow to respond, so I am constantly pressing and holding when all I want is the press once function. The headphones paired with both my iPad Pro and my Galaxy S7 smartphone, but NOT particularly easily. To place the headphones in pairing mode, so that your Bluetooth device can see the Pioneer broadcast in the device Settings, you must press and hold the headphones’ center button until a miniscule LED light on the side of the clip blinks alternately red and blue. (Normally, the light is red when the headphones are charging, and slowly-blinking blue when the headphones are turned on and connected.) What’s hard about that pairing, you may ask? It’s in the instructions, except that the instructions are printed in type so tiny that even the best eyes will need a magnifying glass to read them. Worse yet, the key instructions are printed in pale gray (like grayed-out PC menu selections) on soft, recycled paper that is also gray. Purchasers deserve instructions that are printed in black ink, at the very least. The clip is nice to have. The battery life isn’t great (about 8 hours), and the battery takes about 2 hours to charge via the included USB cable. The “carrying case” for the extra ear tips (you get size S, M, and L sets) is a small resealable plastic bag. The headphones don’t have “over ear” fit (as stated at least once on the product page), but are strictly “in ear” fit (i.e., battery-powered earbuds). I didn't try using these earphones to answer telephone calls. However, considering how many mistakes I make with the tiny buttons when playing music, I suspect that I'd be hanging up a lot when I was trying to take calls.

title: Versatile Use, Easy to Store
text: I ordered the 43 Inch/110 Centimeter Light Reflector. It comes in it's own little zippered bag. The fabric appears to mostly be nylon, so it should wash easily with a damp cloth. The stitching is solid. I didn't know what to expect, but the Light Reflectors are exactly as pictured. My only little gripe is I had to refer to the listing to remember how to fold it to fit back into the pouch. Once you remove it, you have a large disk, silver on one side, black on the other. This has another zipper on the side. Inside the reflector's zipper is a diffuser which you will need to remove from the larger pouch to use. This is where the mounting tab is located. You will need the mounted clip that is available as part of the kit, or you may purchase it separately. That's not all. The large, outer pouch is reversible, so you then reverse the large pouch and you have a gold reflector one one side or a solid white on the opposite side. This is how you have black, white, gold, silver and a diffuser all in one. The diffuser has it's own tap to clip on your tripod, which pokes through from a button hole to mount the entire piece. You will need to make sure it's pulled through if you remove the outer layer of the reflector. This is a good purchase for the value. I'm a hobbyist, and sometimes take photos of my projects. My problem comes from being a night-shift worker. I live and work at night. I must make my own lighting setup if I want to photograph anything at night, even in my home. It's light weight, and folds to a small circle, so this is also portable. It weighs less than a pair of jeans. Here's a free tip: Only use the gold if your subject is brown. I don't find the gold reflector so useful. I'm glad it's available, but I doubt it will get much use for me. Also, place the reflector in a way that would mimic the sun's light, and your subject will look more natural. This is where the clips and extension arms from your tripod can come in handy if you are working solo. Of course, you can always enlist the assistance of another for outdoor shoots. Sometimes this is a best option if it's available. In the movies, they tend to show someone holding a reflector with it facing up towards the subject, but that's only because it does look silly holding a big disc over your head. If you hold it, you will need to hold it up over your head, and it will look silly but your photos will not. It's a good tool to have on hand for any level of photography. I mostly photograph my doll collection. Once or twice a year, our doll collectors group will gather outdoors for shoots. We've not had a reflector available to us before, so this will help us immensely finding good spots to make photographs, even if the clouds are moving or we want to setup under trees. I also ordered the 2-pack clips. These are nice because they come with an adapter on them. If you remove the threaded black female cylinder and threaded ring, you are left with a standard sized male mount that will fit a tripod base. You do not need to make a separate purchase for an adapter, it's already there. The ball joint swivels all the way around and there is a groove in the base to tilt the clip in towards the base, so you can use these clips for holding the reflectors, diffuser or even a backdrop on a side or top mount adapter of your backdrop frame. Thank you for reading my review. I hope you found it helpful.

title: Cute and Functional with Adjustable Volume and Radio
text: This is the best little alarm clock I've ever owned. It is cute and functional and has a radio and adjustable volume controls. Two alarms can be set at the same time. The FM radio comes in great and I have it set on a favorite station. You can program in stations and then you can wake with the radio or listen for a specific time before going to sleep. There is a nap, sleep button which is very useful. I press the radio button, then the sleep button and the radio turns off automatically after a preset time ( 15/30/60/90 minutes). This also has a nice big snooze button on the top so it is easy to turn off the alarm when you want to sleep a little longer. What I like is that you can adjust the brightness of the display so it doesn't light up your whole room at night. However if you want it to act as a night light, you can set it on the brightest setting. Also, I find this useful when charging my little speaker I use with my CD player. This alarm clock has two USB charging ports which is very convenient. I can charge my kindle and the speaker at the same time! If you have a cell phone or iPad you keep by the bed, this is also a good feature as there is also a line-in jack so you can play music from your phone on the alarm clock speaker. The only negative I'd say is the AM radio which does not seem to work very well. The instruction book is however great and makes setting this clock up easy. It seems the company who makes this alarm clock really did think of everything. My favorite thing to do is climb into my warm bed, put on the radio with one touch of a button and read to my heart's content. I think you will love this alarm clock and it doesn't take up a lot of room on your nightstand. ~The Rebecca Review

Anyway, I like headphones that have wide tonal range and have enough bass without sounding stupidly thumpa-thumpa (like Bose or Beats products), and these Sennheiser Momentums fit the bill perfectly! I use this with my tablet (MP3s), phone (MP3 or streaming from Spotify), and computer (FLAC, CD, and high def audio through external DAC). Sounds absolutely wonderful all the time. The fact that they look incredible, and are incredibly comfortable, are awesome bonuses. At under $200, I think this hits the price/performance sweet spot. One thing though... people with big ears might not like to wear these for long periods, because the ear cup seems a bit smaller than other over-the-ear headphones.

title: Exactly what I expected--maybe a little better, even.
text: I spent a long time hmmming and hawing over this purchase. I really wanted the OEM Panasonic case for my LX7, but at $65 here on Amazon, it was just too expensive. I compared the aftermarket cases for this camera and examined photographs closely. This was the winner for apparent quality and design. And it lived up to expectations. I was worried that it would block some of the screen or controls in the back, but this is not an issue at all. This case fits almost perfectly (screw mount is slightly out of alignment with the hole on the bottom of the camera--but it works), and is of as high-quality materials as one can imagine, given that this is *not* a leather case. The prospective purchaser should be aware that the vast majority of the cases on Amazon for the LX7 are not real leather. You can tell a case is real leather on here if it costs a fortune. The real leather cases on Amazon are all >$50, and some are $100+. For $15, this case represents an excellent value, as it fits pretty well, is well-made (sturdier than I expected!), and looks very nice. (I have the white LX7 and I bought the light brown case--the color of a well-worn baseball glove). One thing: as expected, the strap is made of vinyl, and looks it. I will likely be upgrading to a proper, padded camera strap though, so this is of little import, at least to me. If you are on the fence, take my advice and go for it. This is a good value, and a decent case--especially for this price. I did, and I have no regrets. Note: you will have to remove the case to access the battery/SD card compartment and the ports, as is the norm with all cases of this type for this camera (I was unable to find one that did not require this). --- UPDATE: After several months of hard use, this case has held up remarkably well. No evidence of wear. I get compliments on it, as well. Note: Do not attach the camera to a tripod with the bottom of the case on. The knob on the case is threaded to allow you to do this, but getting it off the tripod mount afterwards is a nightmare. Just take it out of the case--trust me.

title: top load, front display, comes w/ remote (INC batteries!), HDMI, USB power, inc Earbuds
text: Compact, super lightweight, easy set up. There is an HDMI cord, and a USB power cord. Depending on how you plan to power up, you can use the USB side, or you will need to supply your own wall adapter, or your own USB-C adapter. I wanted a DVD player for our spare room tv. I was looking at plain DVD players, and wanted something on the small side as well as something I could use with another tv or computer. This came up as an option, and I decided to take my chance on it (initially thought it pricey for a mini DVD player--but if it worked, then it would have been a good purchase). It meets all my needs. I wanted it to have a display, and a remote. It has both. It is super easy to set up-- just plug in the two cords --HDMI from DVD player to TV/monitor and power cord (plug mini USB into DVD player, then plug USB into a USB port or wall adapter). DONE! There is a small front display to show track time. There is a power button, volume dial, pause/play, forward, reverse. So remote can be used or can make adjustments directly on unit. Headphone port. Comes with ear buds! Top load. Included a sturdy remote that comes with batteries! Picture quality and response is spot on. No issues with sounds. No issues at all! Everything works. I tried everything on it. I tried it on two tvs and a monitor (it is that easy to use). I kept a DVD going for a few hours (season 1 the office) and no issues. I have tried both a store bought DVD and a rewritable. Both worked fine. I noticed a slight pause when I was messing around with the cords (working on cord management when putting in the main spot I plan to keep it). So I am not sure how it would be for "active" use. I dont plan to move around with it. So far, I am quite pleased with this unit. Meets all my needs. If I learn anything else, or experience anything else worth sharing, I will be back to do so.

title: It works- sorta
text: I got this specifically to use with my Amazon Fire Stick 4k. I have a wireless media remote keyboard that I used to use with an HTPC that needs a USB dongle, and wanted to also take advantage of the wired Ethernet jack I had previously installed by my TV (wired is always more reliable for streaming in our experience). This device seemed perfect! It connects to the power socket of the Fire stick and offers a USB hub with ethernet jack. The problem is, the ethernet jack doesn't seem to be recognized by the Firestick at all! I know what it is supposed to do when it recognizes a network jack, but the option to use the wired internet simply doesn't show up- it doesn't know or acknowledge it is there! On the upside, at least the USB sockets work, so I was able to use my media remote keyboard. In fact, this device comes in two parts- one is a USB host plug that allows a single USB connection while passing power, and a standard USB hub + ethernet that attaches to that. In all honestly, if I just want to use the USB media remote, I don't need the hub... I can plug it directly into the host port without the hub attached. Its a shame because I really want ethernet. So it only half works.

title: Cannot live without mine
text: Range limitations notwithstanding, this receiver has dramatically expanded the usefulness of my Droid X by allowing me to play my music through my home stereo system, as well as through my friend's [[ASIN:B001DLTDQC Bose SoundDock Series II Digital Music System for iPod (Black)]] (using an [[ASIN:B000068O51 Hosa YRA154 1/8 Male To Dual RCA Female]] to adapt the Belkin to the Sounddock since my Droid will not fit the iPod cradle.) One reason I do not have a problem with the range is I am usually in the room where the stereo is, and sit fairly close to where I have the Belkin receiver located. And, with the Bose rig, that is for taking out to the patio or in the kitchen, so I am in line of sight and pretty close as well. The signal is consistently strong between my Droid X and the receiver, and the sound quality is more than adequate for my old ears. The only thing I would want is for the Belkin receiver to automatically connect with my Droid X in the same manner that the [[ASIN:B003PPGOC0 GOgroove FlexSMART X2 ADVANCED Wireless In-Car Bluetooth FM Transmitter with Charging, Music Control and Hands-Free Calling for ANDROID, iPhone, Blackberry and Windows Smartphones]] I have in my car does. As it stands, I always have to go into my Droid's settings and manually connect the paired Belkin before I can transmit my music stream to it. title: Big, but does extend the range
text: This antenna is about 16" long, but it does seem to do what it is supposed to. It looks a bit weird attached to the camera because it is long, But as far as I can tell it works pretty good. I have some Foscam FI8910 W cameras. I set one up in a steel pole barn with a fiberglass garage door. I couldn't get it to couple with my router in the house with the standard antenna. I put this antenna on and bingo it connected right away. I don't think it can punch through steel and the fiberglass door helps I'm sure, but still, the standard antenna wouldn't get there and this one does. I can't speak to how far it will extend the range and there are a lot of factors that can impact that, but I am convinced that it will get you farther than the standard antenna. I plan on trying some experimenting this coming Summer with an outdoor camera to see just how far it will extend the range line of sight. I will just add that among the various factors that can impact wireless range, other wireless traffic in the area can be the biggest one to cause problems. Sometimes different channels work better than others. This antenna will not solve all problems by any means, but it should help. Whether or not it is enough can not be predicted reliably. Your specific set of conditions means a lot. Sometimes you can also get high gain antennas for your router too. These might even fit depending on the connector. Keep in mind that increasing the range of your router also makes your router more vulnerable to being intercepted. Use security.

text: -This camera is definitely for beginners and I am an amateur at photography so this is perfect for me -Portrait shots are beautiful on this camera and I find they provide the best quality of them all -Other images seem to be closer to the quality of a newer smartphone -Sports shots are pretty good at capturing movement. I took some pictures of my son on a trampoline and got some good shots of him in the air while he was just playing around and not trying to pose -A sports video, however, was not that good. The camera didnt capture the movement too well (this was also for movement on the trampoline) -I also did a video of toy train going along a track and that looked much better than the trampoline video, so I guess it depends on the type and speed of the movement -The battery life is probably my least favorite part. It seems the battery allows about 30 minutes of use because no matter how long I charge it, it always says 29 minutes remaining and nothing more. Although, I dont plan on using it too long, if I happen to then it will be a con for me -I like the use of the filters as you are shooting or even after right on the camera -You can use the large view or the small view, I happen to like the small view best, it allows me to focus and see what I'm shooting more clearly -The flash provides good lighting. I used it for portraits and taking pictures of my art -The video sound isnt the best from the camera itself. It sounds a little better viewing it from another device, but still sounds a bit muffled. I wouldnt suggest it for vlogs or anything like that, but instead, to use a mic for something like that -The screen is touch screen, but when going into the full menu, it can be hard to press exactly what you want because of the size, although it is a great feature to have -You can take a picture by pressing the button or pressing the area on the screen that you want the camera to focus on. This has actually caused me to take quite a few accidental shots, but I still like that feature -the camera is not lightweight, it is more mid weight

title: So Far, So Good (**Updated 08/11/2017**)
text: I've bought expensive USB cable. I've bought cheap USB cables. I've had braided nylon USB cables that felt like they were taking your skin off you hand when they rubbed against you. Other than the braided cable every single cable I use to keep my bedroom tablet plugged in with has split, torn or ripped at the point just above the micro-USB plug end where the cable meets the plug (no, I don't, as I've been accused in the past, yank on the cord. I always use a firm grip on the plug.) Some last a month or two. My old white Kindle charging cable started flaking apart the same day I put it in use. I'd probably still be using the braided cable which hasn't failed but won't stay plugged in. They advertise that this cable lasts 10x longer and has a 5,000 bend lifespan. I've only had it a short while. There just under seventeen months left on the warranty. I'd honestly be ecstatic if it lasts that long. I'm tired of worrying about shorts or what might happen to the tablet if it goes from plugged to unplugged over and over. So, here's to hoping that this cable lasts as advertised. Update May 2nd 2017 - Well, here we are a little over three months since I purchased this cable and although it has developed a permanent bend just past where it plugs into the tablet it show no cracking, no lightening of the covering which would show stressing. This has literally at just three months has doubled the average lifespan of my USB cables. This device which is plugged in 24/7 (unless I need to charge my eReader) gets used numerous times a day, it just doesn't sit there. I'm extremely happy with the durability of this cable. Update August 10th 2017 - I am amazed. No other cable has ever lasted as long as this Anker. I've had cables fail the same day, the same week and as I've mentioned it has developed a distinct bend from the way it hangs from my tablet, kept in the pocket of the grab bar on my bed, the cable shows almost imperceptible whitening just above the connector from strain but no cracks, splits or crumbling. It also stays firmly plugged into my Fire tablet and on occasion it's fine with my PaperWhite as well. Will certainly buy this brand again. Very impressive quality.
