title: Bright and Colorful Images
text: Some of the best computer monitors I have ever used were ViewSonic monitors so I was very interested in trying out the ViewSonic PJD5232L projector and seeing if the company is also capable of making good projection equipment. I have an excellent proving ground – the sanctuary of a small church with a retractable screen as well as access to the computer there, which is the kind of environment where one might use this kind of projector. So far I have been very pleased though I have not used this projector very long at all. First things first – I was a little surprised at how light this projector is. I expected to be a bit heavier and therefore harder to manage when moving it or mounting it somewhere. The lighter weight means that mounting it from a ceiling-suspended rack or on a wall shelf requires less anchoring and reinforcement right out of the box. This is a very good thing. This particular projector supports inputs from two computers as well as S-Video and composite plugs. What is missing is newer input standards like HDMI. This means that this is the wrong projector if you are using it with a system that does not support the older inputs unless you use an adapter. This may or may not be a big deal depending on the specific setup but it does mean that with the most modern computers it probably will not connect right out of the box. Keep this in mind. The resolution is 1024x768 which is not considered “high-definition “ by today’s measure but it is fine for putting presentations up or for watching a movie. To move up to a higher resolution will cost a lot more. The color and clarity of images this projector puts out are excellent. I have used other projectors where the colors diffuse into drab or washed-out hues but this projector seems to suffer less from that than some of the others. One of the great aggravations that has bothered me about projectors is the short range and uselessness of the remote that come with them. A remote control is not helpful if it requires being a foot away from the projector to work. This remote is better, giving better range than some that I have used in the past, and it is also fairly self-explanatory. Now with that said, it would have been nice to have better documentation with it. Overall, this is a very nice projector and ViewSonic has done a nice job with it. It projects bright, colorful images and it is very lightweight which makes mounting easy. My main complaints are the lack of modern input options (and no adapters) and sub-par documentation; otherwise, this is a 5-star projector worth its asking price.

title: Decent After Some Initial Disappointment
text: Philips is a household name when it comes to electronics and this was my first opportunity to play with their camcorder/camera line. This camera was initially very disappointing but after giving it a second chance I have come to appreciate it more. I am completely revising this review. This is an attractive little camera, wine red and compact. It is a little smaller than an ice cream sandwich, which makes it ideal for on-the-go imaging and also for people who dislike toting a heavier camera around. Because this camera is waterproof it feels ruggedized and solid, and the camera is able to withstand being dropped from low heights. Overall, it is a well-built product. I plugged in the camera and I let it charge, and it took about an hour to fully charge. This was a pleasant surprise - other brands of cameras I have used take 2-4 hours so Philips has the charging mechanism down fairly well. My first big disappointment happened when I set up for recording. By default, the camcorder is set for HD recording and it reported that I had about 15 seconds of recording. When I started recording, it stopped shortly thereafter. Sure enough, it ate up all the space available. It was at this point I realized that there was no MicroSD card in the package - not even a small one. I had to dig online to find the internal capacity of the camera - 128MB. A camcorder that cannot record even 1 minute of video is all but useless. This was evidence of simply cutting corners. Granted, I could set recording to standard resolution and I could get more video time but still not enough to be considered useful. Why Philips does not include more internal storage in this camera is completely beyond me. My initial video tests were abyssmal. I tried several things to get the video quality up but I was unable to get things to look very good. Videos were grainy, pixelated and otherwise looked bad. I tried setting the recording mode (scene) manually and while that did help it did not improve the video quality enough to make a real difference. I moved the video to the computer and it looked even worse. It occurred to me that all of my videos were shot in the evening with lower light. I decided to take a video of my cat in the middle of the day instead and the results were vastly better, to the point of actually looking quite good! So, I set the mode to "Auto" and recorded about 30 seconds worth of footage, copied the video to the computer and everything looked great. Apparently, the low-light capabilities of this camcorder are not great but it's daylight recording is very good. The anti-shake capabilities on this camcorder are excellent. There seems to be a slight filming delay which allows the camcorder to compensate for slight motion - this is good for when you're outside and you have a shiver. Still image quality is good when using good lighting but is average at best when in low-light conditions. With the small internal storage there is room for pictures but again this will fill up fairly quickly, especially at full resolution. I had high hopes for this camcorder/camera and though I was initially very disappointed with it I have come to a point of satisfaction with it. It does not perform well in low light, even with the flash on, but in daylight it is very good. The storage capacity (or, rather, the lack thereof) really is inexcusable but for an on-the-go camera/camcorder it's worth a look. As long as this is not going to be a night-life camera, it's good.

title: Great Dome Camera Junction Box
text: In order to properly install some cameras a junction box is required. Depending on the manufacturer of your camera the junction box will differ. This particular one will work with the vast number of dome cameras manufactured by Hikvision - all those that use a 3 hole mounting system. The box is solidly made and will provide a secure waterproof seal protecting the critical components of your camera. I have had this box installed for a few months with absolutely no issues. Installation is straight forward and easy. When ordering double check the mounting system of your dome camera to verify the number of mounting screws - this will save you great frustration caused when trying to mount with the wrong junction box. This is a great product - one I will purchase again.

The handles seem well secured, which is good because this bag is quite big and I image one could really load it full of heavy books and files. There are only two interior pockets both the full size of the bag. One with a velro enclosure that is has a padded feel to contain your laptop and one that is also nylon but not padded. My wife uses a surface, so this bag is a bit too big for her purpose. The Surface slides around in the bag since it is not a snug fit. This bag also needs more pockets. And some smaller pockets. The overall look of the bag is nice, although it is pretty basic. The quality meets the price point. And it definitely is huge. I believe instead of my wife using this bag for travel, we will use it for my daughter to use when she has a sleepover. It would nicely fit her clothing, small blanket and toiletries. title: Good for the Price
text: UPDATE (03.20.14): The original review (Nov. 20,2012) is below. A couple of times, I tried to use the radio with the so called back up battery that had been more than charged, but the radio doesn't operate with it. I therefore dropped the rating from 5 stars to 3 because what's the use of having a back-up battery if it isn't going to work? The radio works fine with the AC/DC adapter that costs extra. I haven't tried the crank and have told myself that I will resort to such labor only in an emergency, which I haven't yet had. Also: You get static on the radio if your cordless phone activation is too close to the radio, but any microwave or radio wave (including flourescent lights) affect all radios, not just this one (that's the nature of transmission). ORIGINAL REVIEW: Yes, you do have to buy the AC/DC adapter separately, but if you have a Radio Shack universal adapter, you can use that. I paid $10 for the Kaito one, which is a fair price. I bought six of these little radios for myself, a relative, and friends. We get a good signal in both NYC and Alexandria, VA. I bought the green model from Amazon, which is fine for me because I'm not fashion conscious about most things. After purchasing them, I discovered that Acorn sells them in different colors: red, yellow, etc. I prefer analog to digital because digital electronics has to be reset every time the power drops to zero, so the analog tuner on this radio is perfect for me. I wish the little front knobs were a little larger, but the fact that they are not totally round compensates for the lack of size, especially for people like me, who have arthritis. If you have vision problems though, you might need a magnifying glass or good glasses to read the markings along those front buttons. In summation, I like this radio and all the little connections that come with it for the cellphone or computer tablet.

title: Great video quality!
text: Purchased two of these Eufy 2K cams to test out and determine if they're good enough to replace the Wyze cams. These Eufy cams definitely capture higher resolution, more detailed videos compared to the Wyze cam v2. The pan and tilt feature is great when I want to remotely check different parts of an area and the pan/tilt action is very silent. You can set the cam to automatically track/follow motion when it's detected. The amount of time it takes to go to live view on a cam is within a couple of seconds and the alerts for motion is definitely faster than the Wyze cam v2. The time from me waving my hand in front of the cam and getting an alert on my phone is about 3 seconds or less. I did experience issues with one Eufy cam where it would randomly start panning by itself and would do a 360 degree pan. The Eufy app works overall, but I did notice several times I had connection issues when trying to live view, but this issue also happens on Wyze cams, too. You can set the Eufy cam to record only when motion or sound is detected or set the cam to continuous recording. If you choose continuous recording the video resolution will be reduced to 1080p resolution. For motion recording you can set up activity zones (specific areas you want to detect motion), adjust motion detection sensitivity, detect people, detect pets, and detect all other motion. Eufy's app is still missing the ability to view multiple live views of cams at the same time, which is a shame and hopefully they have a software update for that. Overall, the Eufy 2k cam has great video quality, the pan/tilt motion is useful and silent, has a lot of features, and the price is competitive. They go on sale from time to time, so if you're not in a hurry then I'd wait for a sale.

title: Great gimbal with strong motors
text: Feiyutech is a brand that I've come to know for their camera accessories. Generally, their products have great build quality and is well-designed. I've had problems with their app working with LG phones but once I upgrade, that wouldn't be a problem anymore. The FeiyuTech Official SCORP-C Camera Stabilizer is a 3-axis gimbal made for DSLR and mirrorless cameras. All of the axis arms are metal with high quality plastic in the body. The motors are quiet and there are plenty of buttons that control most aspects of the gimbal. There's even a video made by Feiyutech that explains the buttons very well. This SCORP-C model does not have an LCD screen. The accompanying smartphone app is used to upgrade the gimbal firmware and for finer adjustments of the gimbal such as the follow speed and horizontal calibration. Control of the gimbal itself is also possible, but it is easier and faster to use the buttons on the device. It really is a good thing that the gimbal is designed to be operated without the need to fuss with a phone and app. A few USB cables are included with this gimbal, which is designed to control the shutter of the camera that you own. Unfortunately, my Lumix GX8 is an odd model that just isn't well supported, even by Panasonic themselves. None of the included cables worked for me so this gimbal isn't able to control my camera. Not a big problem, though. If I really need wireless control of my camera, I can probably work out some sort of phone mount and use the Panasonic app. Just be aware that Feiyutech's list of compatible camera might be a little limited. They publish a compatiblity list. Just search for the term "Feiyu SCORP-C Lens Compatibility List" With that said, the gimbal stabilization is great. Once your camera is balanced (search youtube for "Scorp-C Balancing" to see a video demonstrating this process), the motors are very, very quiet and the various follow modes help to create smooth motion in your videos. Recently, I'm helping my daughter record videos and this gimbal has really come in handy. My camera doesn't support in-body stabilization for video and the lenses I have do not have in-lens stabilization. Gimbals are the best answers for my camera's limitations.

title: Quick and responsive - premium grade laptop
text: This Surface Laptop 4 model from Microsoft is an excellent premium grade laptop. My prior laptop is an older 17" Lenovo. This one is smaller (obviously), but much, much quicker. The aluminum chassis is thin, lightweight and solid. The display is looks great- colors are detailed rich and vibrant, and the touchscreen is effective and appropriately responsive. I find myself simply reaching for the button on a given sites screen, rather than maneuvering the cursor and tapping. (Although, the TouchPad is certainly responsive, too). The keyboard is comfortable and responsive - the surface around the keys and track pad has a texture that makes it seem sort of "soft" (even though it's not). Sound is rich and full. Very nice! Very few ports - only what you need and not much else. A USB Type-A and Type-C, a headphone jack, and the chargers connector. (Or you can charge using the USB Type-C port). Set up is straightforward, I loaded in some software (Wordperfect suite) and everything just fell right in place and all worked as it should without issue) 5.) The keyboard is good. It’s not the best, a little spongier than the MacBook Pro 16’s, with a slower rebound, but still among the “good” keyboards on the market, - reasonably tactile. It has physical F-keys, something I wish I had on the MacBook Pro 16. I think the TouchBar is one of the worst things to happen to MacBooks (inadvertent touches are the worst!), so I hope Apple reverts to physical keys this coming year on their flagship system. Rumor has it they will. 6.) Ergonomics are decent, though it’s actually a bit tricky to open the laptop as there’s no indent where you’re meant to flip the screen up, - a peculiar oversight. Bezels are a little large for 2021, but far from offensive. 7.) Sound quality is not bad, but a far cry from the bass that the MacBook Pro 16 is able to generate. You could certainly listen to music here, but headphones would be preferred. 8.) Battery life and thermals are absolutely fantastic, decimating the MacBook Pro for general usage, which is just a portable toaster. The laptop just runs remarkably cool with this AMD setup. That said, when the fans kick in, they kick in with a WHOOSH, which can be a bit disconcerting. Battery life is superb, easily giving me 8-11 hours for general web usage, a good 40% longer than my MacBook Pro doing the exact same things at a comparable brightness. 9.) The SSD is nothing too special. While yes, any SSD is faster than not-an-SSD, this is on the lower end of the spectrum in terms of NVMe performance. Overall, I really enjoy the Surface Laptop 4, but it’s not hard to see where Microsoft could make some massive improvements and solidify the Surface line as THE Windows 10 systems to buy. 4 out of 5. A worthy alternative to high-end laptops, but not yet at the top of its game.

title: Good product, but go for the bus-powered Nikon ME-1!
text: I used this for a large video project this summer. It did the job well enough, but I wouldn't recommend it to anyone who is looking for decent DSLR HD video audio capture. Why? Because it is not bus-powered. You need to be sure this is powered on and the 9V battery is always good to go. I used this in 2 different situations: 1) in camera audio capture, connected directly to D7000, 7D & 5DII bodies. It produced great audio, but many times I forgot to turn the mic on, which resulted in zero sound. 2) I used this in conjunction with an external audio device, such as the Zoom H1, to get more specifically located audio. Again, it did the job good enough. But compared to the Nikon ME-1, you're missing a lot of handy features from the VideoMic. For example: Nikon ME-1 is stereo. VideoMic is mono. Nikon ME-1 is bus-powered. VideoMic requires on switch and 9V battery. Nikon ME-1 is compact and small. VideoMic is large and bulky. Nikon ME-1 is cheaper than the VideoMic. After using both of them I sold my VideMic promptly. The Nikon ME-1 does the job much better. Finally the included Fuzzy Windjammer on this kit doesn't do much more than the already included foam on the VideoMic. I compared the two in light wind situations and found the sound to be nearly the same. Save some money and trouble, get the Nikon ME-1. Ben

title: Works perfectly on some devices, not so well with others
text: Note: I would love to love this speaker set, but it has inconsistent performance with various devices. I received a review sample and was pleased by the sound when I plugged this into the phone port on my ThinkPad T61. The sound was clear and I could hear every note on the album that I use to test speakers (Miles Davis' [[ASIN:B000002ADT Kind of Blue]]). I use that album because the instruments in the ensemble cover low, mid and high range frequencies. The bass was a little thin, but with the speakers less than two feet away the sound was rich and I was happy. Where I started to experience problems was when I plugged this into the 3.5mm port on my Samsung Galaxy Note 3 phone. There was static in the mix. The same with my [[ASIN:B00D029NNA Samsung Galaxy Tab 3 10.1 inch]] - static interfered with the music. My final test was to plug the 3.5mm cable end into an [[ASIN:B00AEJJRBK Avantree Cara Bluetooth 4.0 Handsfree stereo Car Kit with mic]] and connect via bluetooth. The sound was crystal clear. I am not sure what conclusions to draw. It could be something wrong with the Samsung phone and tablet, although both work perfectly with headphones which I have directly plugged into their 3.5mm ports. It may be that the 3.5mm cable and interface on the input of this speaker is overly sensitive to impedance and the slightest deviation will induce static. I am not sure, but because I was unable to get the same quality consistently across all devices with which I tested these speakers I subtracted two stars. Construction is exceptional, although I wish the design employed removable USB and 3.5mm cables instead of the fixed ones that provide power and signal inputs, and connect the two units. As it stands if a cable breaks it will take some skill to repair it. With removable, off-the-shelf cables a broken one can be rectified with a trip to an electronics store. Overall, on the two devices that I tested with which these speakers worked the sound was spectacular. On the other two it was unlistenable. If you are connecting this to a bluetooth receiver it's probably a safe choice, but if you are connecting this to anything else I would proceed with caution.

title: Wow! I'm impressed
text: I'm impressed. This little guy is strong and bright! I've tried several lights, including handheld (like this), panel lights, ring lights, etc., and I have been very impressed with this one. Before I dive into the good, let me discuss the bad: the iPhone app. I'm all for having an app to control the light and I totally understand why it's necessary. HOWEVER, this app REQUIRES that you sign up with an email and password. While there might be benefits to this (like accessing presets/settings across devices); I didn't check. Registering an account should 100% be optional. There is no reason to require me to register with my email + password so I can control this light via Bluetooth. As an aside, according to the Apple App Store, the developer does not collect any data from your phone (see pic); this is the reason I did not deduct a star for this. My second gripe with the app, is that the it is a little rudimentary. Not particularly polished; and a little buggy at times (e.g. I couldn't get effects to kick in after selecting them on the app (I'd have to do it multiple times before the light would react), the light wouldn't always connect on the first try, etc. This was not the norm, but it happened a few times during my testing. Now onto the good: First and foremost, it is BRIGHT. Brighter than similarly-sized lights. Physically, it feels well-designed. It has a 2000 mAh battery (I expected 1000 mAh). It is intuitive to operate and you can use it without the app if necessary (setting color temp, intensity, etc. No effects). There is a USB-C port for charging and a small display on the top, which shows battery %, time remaining (!), and color settings. The soft cover on the front is attached magnetically, and can be attached to the back when you take it off (!); a wonderful design touch that is much appreciated. The light can also be affixed to metal surfaces via the magnet in the back. The app, while barebones, provides a lot of functionality. There are many effects to choose from, and even a "music" mode in which the light will pulse to the beat of music picked up by your iPhone (or whatever device you are running the app on). Overall, I have been really pleased with this light.

title: Very Pleased with this Headset
text: A good headset is a fun and very useful accessory when playing online games with friends and can really add to the gaming experience. Such is also useful when interacting with software that uses voice recognition and interpretation (a good example being Rosetta Stone). Creative Labs’ Tactic3D Rage wireless headset is a very good midrange headset and boasts some really nice features, but it suffers from one issue that otherwise would make it perfect. My teenage son is a frequent online gamer and he assisted me in evaluating this headset. This is a lightweight and comfortable headset. It can be worn for extended periods of time and not cause ear pain like some other headsets can. I was a little apprehensive when I first put them on, thinking that they would be uncomfortable after a while but it turns out I was mistaken. Some other reviewers have reported discomfort when wearing this headset but so far this has not been the case for us. Being wireless this headset does not require a wired connection to the computer to work. It does come with a USB cable that allows for charging, but the headset needs instead a USB receiver in order to work properly. Without the USB connection the headset stays charged for a good while. The packaging claims that the headset can be used for 16 hours on a single charge, though so far we have not tried to go that long. Sound quality is excellent. We loaded up a few music tracks that cover a wide range of the spectrum and the overall sound was balanced, not too heavy on the bass and not muddled on the highs. When communicating with someone online with this headset, he told us we were coming through loud and clear. The way the headset works can be customized through the use of a Control Panel application. This Control Panel allows one to tweak the voice properties (such as adding robotic effects or greatly twiddling the pitch/volume), change the color of the LED glow on the cups, volume settings and more. My son and I had fun playing with the settings, though we agreed the headset was must useful with the default voice settings. I have only one complaint with this headset - the control panel is not included on a CD in the box and tracking it down online proved to be a lot more challenging than it should have been. It took a bit of searching before I finally was able to find the correct download package - and then the download kept timing out. I think this is the one area where Creative dropped the ball with this headset - if there is no CD in the box, it should not require a lot of effort for customers to find the application on the Creative website, especially when the application is touted on the packaging. Overall, my son and I are very pleased with this headset. It delivers good sound on both ends of the connection, it is comfortable and it is not absurdly expensive. It would have been nice to have the software on a CD in the box, but otherwise we have no complaints. This is a good mid-range headset that is worth consideration if you’re looking for a headset you can customize.

title: Excellent for the price
text: Ordered one from Amazon, $35. Like that camera can sit inside on window sill, flush to the glass of the window, and perfect video picture, night vision is spectacular (have read some ambient light outside is required). No need to worry about batteries or wiring, it plugs into the wall. You also do not need a subscription for just basic usage, but it will only film an event and notify you once every 5 minutes if you opt for no subscription. For $15/year, it is unlimited with no 5 minute pause between even detections. Has a micro SD slot (micro SD card not included), we just used one we had, but not necessary for camera operation. It can also be paired with other appliances through an IFTT app like a garage door. The MAJOR ISSUE is that even though you may set your detection area, the detection is via a change in pixels (where Ring, Blink, etc. are infrared and detect a heat source) and we receive an EXCESSIVE DETECTION NOTIFICATIONS when the sun sets and shines through the leaves or at night when car headlights flash across the detection area. We have also read where people receive excessive detection notifications when it rains or bugs attracted to light, but we have not had an opportunity to put it to the test with these. The only solution to minimize these notifications for these non-events is to move the camera or to set up rules to silence events during certain times of the day. For the cost, we decided we can live with this. Our walk around for now, is to just have one family member's phone receive alerts. And the other family members must block the alerts for this specific app in the settings of their phone (not within the app, or it blocks everyone's notifications). Only the initial person who sets up the camera on the app, can make a lot of the changes to the camera within the app. And other family members are just shared access to the camera and can set up their own rules. The initial person who did the set up will receive notifications a split second before the people it is shared with. Ordered a 2nd one that was supposed to be new, but WAS OPENED and still had the UPS stickers on it with the customer's name and address who had returned it. So returning the 2nd camera and ordering a replacement, hopefully this one will be new. Realize with any electronics that receive updates (like with cell phones, fitness trackers, etc), they do not have an infinite life due to technology restrictions so another reason to go for this budget friendly option. I believe Wyze retired support for their 1st model after 5 years. Price on Wyze website was $29.99 ($57.98 for 2) plus $5.99 shipping, but decided buying it from amazon would make sure we had free returns with Prime. Wyze is a company started by former Amazon employees and it is based in Seattle. Update: very sensitive to raindrops on the window, birds, neighbors' lights turning on and creates an event and notification.

title: These are fantastic!!!
text: I’ll start off saying that I have received zero compensation to write this review. We just like these so well at my home that I had to write a review. My teen wanted bluetooth earbuds for Christmas. He’s terrible at keeping up with items, so I want buying the pricey big-name ones. My husband had a wireless set of earbuds that have the connector cord made by this company that he really likes, so we read the reviews on these and decided to give them a go. Kiddo loves them. They stay in his ears while lying in bed and he often wakes up in the morning with one still in his ear! They’re just bulky enough to see when mixed in the sheets, but not bulky enough to be intrusive. I have a great pair of connected Bluetooth earbuds by a big name company, but was tired of the cord getting in my way. I frequently only wear one bud so I can hear what’s happening around me, and the wire on that pair are in the way when worn like that. I jumped on board and ordered a pair of these for myself. I love them. They are incredibly comfortable. I can’t wear apple’s buds because they hurt my ears. These are so comfy you have no clue they’re in your ear! Good noise cancelling, good sound. The best part? Super easy pairing with your device! Literally just hold the button down and they connect. AND some genius made them actually read as “sound peat” in the bluetooth menu on the device. Not a single other bluetooth device we own actually says what it is in that menu- they’re all a string of letters and numbers. My Sony buds are like wic2000, my car is U2, my bluetooth speaker is “bts” but we have 2 so I have to guess. These are a no brainer! They hold a good charge, the case is sturdy and they clip in there well with the magnet. I marked my headphones with a dot of nail polish and my case with a sticker to differentiate them from my son’s. I wish they came in fun colors, but that’s not a fault. (I didn’t want to do white because of grunge potential.) So far these have been a great product. I’d def not hesitate to recommend them!

title: Long Cables, Work Well
text: Most of the USB cables I have are either 3' cables or are really short ones that come with electronic devices. Sometimes a longer cable is really handy, especially when the immediate area surrounding a work space is cramped. I looked to these 10' cables to help alleviate one such issue and I learned a few things. One of those things is that a cable can be too long in some cases, though this is a failure in organization, not in the cable. These are flat USB cables. Flat cables are less likely to tangle than their round counterparts, but I will confess to preferring round cables anyway (it’s a personal preference, not a technical preference). These are shielded in woven nylon which is great; the nylon is stronger than soft plastic shielding and gives the cable a little more stiffness but does not make the cable so stiff that it’s awkward to run. The connectors are interesting - two-tone plastic that is different than anything else on my desk which makes it a lot easier to determine where both ends of the cable are plugged in. I tried stressing the cable ends at the connectors by trying to pull out the shielding from the plugs and they held firm. I tried one of these with my phone and it worked just fine, I was able to charge the phone and move data between it and my laptop. I did not attempt a speed test. There is a big difference between 3' cables, 6' cables and 10' cables. If you need a 6' cable, a 10' cable will leave you trying to figure out what to do with the extra 4' of cable. In my case this meant having to use a twist tie to bunch the cable up in the middle. This is not a criticism but rather a suggestion - get a cable that’s the right length for the job. If you need a 10' cable, these have worked great for me. These are nice USB cables. They seem to be made well and they work as expected. If you need a really long cable, this is a good choice. Disclaimer: I was provided a sample of this product in exchange for a fair, honest and thorough review. Even though I received this product at no cost I have rated and reviewed this product without bias, based solely on my experience with it.

title: Great buy if you can catch them on sale!
text: Disclaimer - I've only had these for about a week so far and these are the very first Truly Wireless Earbuds that I have ever owned. I purchased these earbuds on a great sale while waiting for another more expensive brand to get restocked, and honestly didn't expect to much from these. First impressions were that they were quite cheap however after using them for a little while now, I've come to appreciate them even more. Even though these are my very first Truly Wireless Earbuds, I feel that after tons of research and watching reviews, I know what to look for in sound and quality. There are so many budget wireless earbuds on the market right now with so many more on Indigogo (which from comments and reviews I've seen and read, I will NEVER buy from). This makes it quite hard to know what to buy. For me, I have been waiting for that perfect very small and discreet Truly Wireless Earbud with amazing sound. The Iyesku YK-T02 TWS Sport Earbuds didn't hit that mark for me but at the price I paid, I honestly can't complain. Pros: 1. Bluetooth 5.0 - This was one of my main requirements when searching and the YK-T02 has it. 2. Easy to pair (I am using the Samsung Note 8) and pairing was very easy. Once the buds are initially paired, they connect super fast once you pull them out of the charging case. 3. No loss of connectivity to either earbud. I've seen a bunch of reviews where this is an issue with MANY other wireless earbuds on the market. Even some of the more spendy ones. 4. Fit is very comfortable and seem to be very secure though I have not put them through the test of running nor exercise. However I don't think this would be an issue at all. 5. Sound quality is decent. More about this in the Cons. 6. Tons of ear tip options. Most earbuds come with a few extra sizes but these come with a plethora, for that perfect custom fit. 7. Quite small and discreet. 8. Small pocketable charging case. Cons: 1. Sound. As I stated in the Pros these sound "good" (for the money) but the Highs are quite loud (Mids and Bass are not) and almost hurt when the volume is turned all the way up. Mids are not to bad but the bass is nearly non existent which was a huge let down. HOWEVER let me say, once I played with the ear tips and found the right fit, Highs, Mids, and Bass are much better, but still not what I would have hoped for but again these aren't "High end" earbuds right? 2. Because the volume of these buds are quite low, Audio books are hard to hear when in a loud environment. 3. No satisfying magnet to hold the buds in the charging case like many others on the market. (The case does click shut to ensure the buds wont be falling out.) 4. Speaking of the case, my first impression was that the case felt very nice and lightweight, but it does feel cheap. Final thoughts: Like I said earlier in this review, if you can get these from Amazon on a lightning deal or with a coupon, I would say grab you a pair, but just don't expect these to compete with higher end buds. These would be great for the kids and anyone that just wants a good pair of Truly Wireless Earbuds that wont break the bank.

title: Excellent keyboard case for your iPad!
text: I don't need my personal laptop at the office, just my iPad, but typing on the iPad screen was a pain. I did NOT get any compensation from anyone for this review. This is a great product and my review reflects it: If I could give 10 stars, I would! This keyboard case is genius! It does a great job protecting your iPad, the keyboard feel is great and the Bluetooth connection is reliable and makes sense! The special function keys - like screen dim and brighten, cut, copy and paste - actually work! What is so clever about this is that it uses the iPad's built-in magnetic-sensing strip (the one that holds the original cover to the iPad) to turn on the Bluetooth keyboard: When you flip this case open and put your iPad in place on the clearly marked strip, you can FEEL the magnetic "click" and then the keyboard wakes up and turns on. Instant keyboard and very stable when sitting on your desk. If you leave it alone for a while, it shuts down the Bluetooth connection, to save battery life. But press any key for two seconds, and it's back up. If you don't need the keyboard, it's very easy to open the case and flip the IPad over and use it without the keyboard: The Bluetooth doesn't connect unless you "Click" the iPad into the magnetic strip. I've had to recharge the keyboard once, in two months of use. But the indicator lights warned me well in advance and it recharged in about two hours. Great product! The keyboard feel is great, though the keys are necessarily small I bought another case that had a rotating pivot for portrait and landscape use, but it had bad keyboard reliability (what you typed didn't always appear) and the battery failed after one charge. title: Excellent Quality Tiny Ear Buds
text: I’ve tested a few similar sets of ear buds with mixed results but I was really impressed with this set. I read the instruction manual - removed the tiny stickers on each ear bud and plugged in the case. It achieved a full charge in just over an hour. I removed the ear buds from the case, the right one first and then the left and they paired with each other immediately. I went to my Bluetooth settings in my iPhone and found Haylou-GT1_R and my phone paired with the set with no issue. I was surprised at the sound quality these tiny ear buds deliver. They can definitely be turned up as loud as you’d want to listen. The bass, midtones and treble is on par for ear buds - even those that are much larger. I did figure out that the initial way I had them in my ears was upside down when I went to test the calling features. The microphone needs to be pointed towards your mouth for best audio reception and sound quality improves a bit in this position. Call quality was excellent for both hearing the other person and them hearing you. In my testing the earbuds had no problem receiving within the 33’ stated range (actually making it to around 40’) and even received clearly through multiple walls - 2 rooms away with the doors closed and many obstacles in between. The only issue I struggled with was tapping the ear bud to stop / start the music and answer / end calls / activate voice assistant and skip tracks. There is no button, you just tap an area of the exterior of the ear bud but you have to tap a very exact area which isn’t necessary easy to locate as it is not differentiated from the rest. It would have been helpful if the manufacturer put a different texture in that area so you could feel it with your finger to locate it. While the manufacturers claims these last for 12 hours I found it was closer to 8 under normal use - still excellent. And the case will charge them without it having to be plugged back in which is always a nice feature. These earbuds excelled in all of my tests showing that they are a high quality, dependable product. This is a set I wouldn’t hesitate to recommend. ⭐️As with all Drew’s Reviews, if I discover something new about this product - good or bad - I will UPDATE my review. I believe in creating detailed, complete reviews because, like you, I depend on them when I am shopping. If you found my review HELPFUL - please click ⬇️ below!⭐️

title: It's a mouse, it works.
text: When purchasing a bluetooth mouse, or any bluetooth device, an important factor is how well it connects and how well it STAYS connected. After all this time, the industry still can’t make up its mind about how bluetooth works: - Microsoft with their mouse and Sony with their controller that pair without the need of a code (which Android’s bluetooth stack doesn’t support). - Mice and keyboards that will not reconnect after it wakes from sleep mode. - To this day, bluetooth devices still can’t wake a computer from suspend state. So when I get a simple bluetooth mouse for a good price, and it actually works as expected, I was happy. For my review, I’m comparing the Bornd mouse to the [[ASIN:B006428K7I Motorola 89519N Bluetooth Mouse]] which I got in a set that included the [[ASIN:B004L9MBH6 Motorola Wireless Keyboard for Motorola ATRIX and XOOM (Motorola Retail Packaging)]]. With the Motorola mouse, its frequency somehow conflicts with the Motorola keyboard, resulting in sticky keys, as in “I’m having a good daaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaay”. Conversely, the keyboard interferes with the mouse where the computer would actually lose connection with the mouse for a few seconds after typing, or lose connection completely. What I liked about the Bornd: - Pairing was simple using the button at the bottom of the mouse. - Operating distance is about 5 feet, but this may be because of my computer’s bluetooth antenna - Takes a single AA battery which lasts over a month of moderate use (2 hours per day) - Mouse movement is good. No need to adjust mouse speed in the control panel. - Accuracy is good. Editing images using Pixlr.com, I’m able to select precisely the parts of the image I need. What gave me trouble with the Bornd: - When you first put your hand on this mouse, you will not feel that it is very well built. The plastic is completely smooth and the mouse wheel seems to rattle when you shake it. I got used to it after a few minutes, though. - The mouse reconnects with the computer most of the time, but sometimes it was necessary to turn the mouse off and on again after the computer reboots or if the computer was out of range for an extended period of time. - Lately, I’ve been experiencing double-clicks when I click on the left mouse button once. I’m usually able to fix this by getting angry and slamming the mouse down a couple of times. This may indicate a build quality issue with this mouse. In my specific case, the Bournd did not interfere with my Motorola keyboard at all and it seems the sticky key occurs only when the batteries on the keyboard runs low.

title: No keystone, 720p native, bright.
text: The Salange 7500 LUX Mini Home Projector is a barebones projector that provides good brightness, good focus, full-sized HDMI input, but not much else. Connect a FireTV or AndroidTV device to this projector and you have all of the "smart" features that you'll need. So the good of this projector is that it is somewhat compact, not as small as the pico projectors, but small enough... it's about the size of a bread box (as the saying goes). The focus ring is fairly tight, meaning that once you find your focus, it won't move. The USB outputs enough amps to power a FireTV and overall, it is very straightforward to use. The bad is that there is no keystone adjustment, not even the analogue kind that tilts the lens. The resolution is only 720p and I found that projecting at a shorter range of around 9 feet, I'm able to see every pixel on the projected image. At a longer distance, the pixels may meld together better. In summary, this is a low-priced projector that will project an image through an HDMI input. If that's all you're looking for, this will work, but if you're looking for something that you may set up more permanently, this isn't it.

title: Great router, I purchased 2 of these and I ...

I already owned the RT-AC5300 which is serving as the router and these 2 will serve as Nodes. I live in a giant Faraday cage, a 60+ year old house with plaster walls and chicken wire mesh behind those walls which severely cuts down on wifi traveling through them. Add metal siding too, no much WiFi gets out or even through the walls. The router is small, in size but seems to have a big attitude. I was able to get great speeds throughout the house. With one of them on each end and the 5300 in the middle. I will likely be buying another one of these to extend the range from the house to my detached garage and the backyard. I was hesitant to spend the money but the better performance was well worth it. If I had purchased it here on Amazon I could have saved myself a lot of money. But that's the price you pay the you want something and don't necessarily want to wait for delivery. I would recommend it to anyone and also suggest you look at setting up the AiMesh. I know I am a happy camper and can go all over the house with no dropped connections. The only con I had was I was never able to get the router into rescue mode. Its pretty straight forward on the 5300, not so much on this one. Though that's something not everyone would need. I had to reset the router with e WPS button held until it reset. title: First Time MP3 User - Having Difficulty Here
text: Because I am a First Time MP3 User, please ignore this review (if you are an expert.) I am having difficulty with this MP3 player because the manual does not have references in each paragraph as to "more readings." For example, Sandisk does not offer its own MP3 burner and/or Music Management Software. This would have been a great advantage, even if the software was at cost, say $10. It would mean the equipment would be integrated with mp3 music burning. I had difficulty transfering mp3 music to the sansa clip +8. First, it seems that the PROPERTIES data in each song was important. With 100 of songs having a song title as "track 1" and "track 2" the music had some chaos creating a playlist. So, a warning about this on the manual, for novice mp3 users would have been nice. (Do you agree?) Maybe I should have looked harder on the sandisk website. (Maybe?) At least a reference to help beginners in the manual would have been appropriate. Ok, second, the internal memory saves music more true to original names than the extended memory. Some of my files says "DN30000101 Journey - Love is Everything" where the DN330000101 tag was added by Sandisk. Not sure why, but this just adds to the chaos. (OK, stick a pencil in my ear.) I am a beginner, so music management means everything to me. Chaos with this Sandisk is life right now. If my collection was just two or three CDs, then I could say ok. (Life is beautiful.) But so many readers, I assume, have a collection of 100 CDs that have been existing on their library for a few years. So, the SANSA CLIP must meet integrating with old files in mid stream. (Old, old file systems, maybe?) It appears not enough testing my Sandisk has been done to make the product user friendly for first time MP3 users. If they did enough testing, they would have built their own music management software I am tired. I called Sandisk, and they suggest to change ALL of my ID3 tags on the PROPERTIES of each song. Well, there goes my summer vacation. If I can manually change 100 songs each weekend, in about 10 weekends I will have make sense out of chaos. Right now, I have so many songs saying "track 1." Yeah, etc, track 2, track 3. Bottom line is that Sandisk is not creating a product like the iPOD which takes responsibility for all experiences the user has: managing albums, burning mp3, transfering data, creating playlist, and teaching novice people about the equipment. Instead, Sandisk just makes the equipment, and assumes "sink or swim" to the users. So, all the good reviews, I assume, come from experienced users, and not first time idiots, like me. Maybe I should go back to my old music player? (Tell me.) I must be tired, because I am having a hard time with this Sansa Clip. Ok, give me about two years, and I'll catch up. So, I give it a 4-star.

text: I had a big clunky WD My Book Essential 1Tb USB 3.0 external drive and it was getting old and failing. I received this Ultra Metal 2Tb model today. I actually took the old My Book apart by prying the case off with a large screwdriver and removed the bare HDD from inside. There is a WD Green drive in there and I actually mounted it in my computer, wiped and formatted it and now it gives me 1Tb of extra storage, not bad. So, the Ultra Metal external drive is so classy. The only complaint I have is the included USB 3.0 cable, it is very short, maybe 12 inches long, not good, however, I had the other 3.0 cable from the old drive, it's about 1 meter long, and it of course works fine with the new drive, I have it plugged into a 1 meter USB 3.0 extension cable so now have about 6 feet of wiggle room from the computer to the external drive. I like this Blue- Black Ultra Metal drive's aesthetics, it is, in short, gorgeous. That big boxy My Book I had before was so clunky, big and ran hot all the time. This device has no unnecessary power cable, just one USB port and nothing else. I opened the file for this drive and saw about 5Mb of preinstalled apps installers including a PDF Users Manual and quite a few other apps in about 20 different languages. WD has gone to a lot of effort to make this drive world class. Except for that little 12 inch USB 3.0 cable. Get yourself an extension cable and this drive is the Cadillac of external drives. The included software is icing on the cake for you to choose how sweet you will make your drive.

title: Great little tablet
text: First and foremost- What every prospective tablet user needs to consider: What am I planning on doing with my tablet? For most users, tablets are for entertainment and are considered a luxury item. They aren't good for working on school work, or anything that requires a lot of typing, so for most people, these devices are for consuming media (news, videos, social networks...). Why pick the Wifi only: When I was looking at Xooms at the local electronics establishment, I was confronted with the decision of getting the $599 Xoom Wifi or the $799 Xoom 3G/Wifi (upgradeable to 4G at a later date at no cost). For me, while 3G was tempting for convenience, it was still a pretty easy decision to buy the Wifi only device. With the 3G, you are tied into a service contract. If you go with Verizon's service (which is advertized with the device), you only pay $599 for the device, but you have to pay $20 a month for 24 months. On top of that, you can only use 1GB of data. For a device that is going to be used for the consumption of data (especially videos), I don't want to be limited to only 1GB. Another decision point was the fact that I knew my Xoom would be used mostly at home, where I have a Wifi network, or at local eatting establishments that provide free Wifi. The real nail in the coffin on this decision was that my 4G phone is capable of wireless teathering and my data plan is unlimited- taking that into consideration- there really is no need to pay for an additional data plan. The Device- an out of the box look: Out of the box, you find the approximately 1.5 lbs device, power cable, and a couple booklets. I would have been nice to have a sleeve for the Xoom, but Motorola doesn't provide any type of protection for the tablet. I made sure to pick up a [[ASIN:B004S748UY ZAGG invisibleSHIELD for Motorola Xoom (Screen) (MOTXOOMS)]] to make sure my screen stayed pristine. The device is a solid little tablet. The back id 4/5 aluminum and the top part of the back case is rubberized plastic. The hardware feels solid. Additionally, the layout of the device generally makes sense. The power button was a little confusing, but once I found it on the back, the idea really grew on me. It will be a little wierd when it comes to accessories, but I have no complaint about the device itself. Next to the power button, there is the HD camera lense with LED flash. Having an HD camera is nice, even if shooting pictures on a tablet isn't really practicle. The back of the device also includes stero speakers that are surprisingly loud for a device of this size. All of the connectors (power, usb, dock...) are all located on the bottom of the device, except the headphone jack. Not sure I am a fan of this oddity, but I have yet to use headphones, so it is not really an issue to date. Also, the volme buttons are located on the left side of the device and are sufficiently easy to reach and use. On the face of the device, there is a forward facing camera- this is great for video chatting. While GTalk works, Skype currently does not support video chat on this device (app issue, not hardware). There is also a light senseor- I hate the light sensor- I sometimes find that the brightness of the screen changes abruptly by a huge amount because I accidently put my thumb over the sensor. The best fix for this is to just turn off the auto adjustment. Orientation: Deviating from the norm, the Xoom is built to be oriented in landscape mode rather than portrait mode. With the 10 inch screen, landscape works great. I find that I am able to thumb type on the on-screen keyboard without straining much to reach the keys in the middle. The size is really what makes landscape work well with this tablet. In portrait, I (like many others) find that the device is a little bit hard to balance- feeling a little top heavy. It isn't so bad that the device is difficult to use- it just takes a little getting used to. Android: The android 3.0, Honeycomb load on this device is great. Many who don't use android or are iPad users will probably find that the device will take a little bit of learning to get used to. I am an android user (though my phone has an older version of android), and found that I was able to fully use and understand the device in 30 minutes of playing with it. The learning period may take up to a day, but once you fiure it out, you will notice that the controls really make sense and are easy to find when you need them. Overall, I love this little device. There are some minor little design dislikes, but nothing that impacts my 5 star rating of this device!

title: A vast improvement over standard Corsair PSU cables
text: I purchased these for the RM650x in my new build. to clean up the appearance of my case, as the default Corsair cables are serviceable but neither flexible nor visually appealing. Do I have a fancy case? It's a Fractal Define Mini C, so, sorta. Do I have a side window? No. Will anyone ever see the interior of this case aside from me and randos and I'm flexing on? Certainly not. Why get cables like these? Call it an eccentricity. I don't like chaos hiding right out of sight. Sure, the Mini C would have the outward appearance of a stocky monolith of unalloyed order, but inside there would be a twisting madness of PVC and copper, heaving silently against the surface. There would be no evidence of it, sure, but I would know. I would know. So anyway, I purchased these to extend the relentless teleology of the IC into the broader world, and to this end I was successful. The cables are much more flexible than the default ones and the paracord is an interesting contrast against the typical circuit boards and enameled aluminum panels. They are a bit pricey for what you get, that's definitely the case - you might be better served by getting a different brand or shorter extensions rather than the full PSU set. I just happened to have the money for the CableMods. Caveats: if you are using velcro ties inside the case, then you have to be careful with the ModFlex, as the hook-side can pull threads from it. I presume ModMesh, being nylon, would not have that issue. to get around it, I used rubber/silicon bands, since unlike zipties they can be removed and adjusted relatively easily. Would I recommend them? If you have a side-window or transparent case, they are a definitely aesthetically on point. ModFlex or ModMesh, either should be suitable. If not, unless you too are eccentric, it probably isn't worth it. The improvement in airflow and the reduction in strain on the components are minimal so long as you are implementing reasonable cable management techniques. I'm still happy I purchased them though, since I'm pretty sure I'll be able to use them in the next two-three builds and they did make it a bit easier to get everything where I wanted it.

title: Nice smart watch with a lot of great features
text: If you cringe at the $$$ price of the brand name smart watches, I think this one by Eurans is a pretty good value. It still have quite a few features that are really nice. It tracks fitness, sleep, heart rate & O2 monitoring like a champ. The step counter is good too, fairly accurate compared to the Fitbit I previously wore. Pairing the watch to the app was a breeze. I like how there's a good variety of fitness options to choose from, both indoor and outdoor. That's great when like me you like to do different types of exercise through the week to keep from getting bored or losing motivation. I'm very happy with the Fitdock app. No weird translation issues like with other apps I've tried. It's easy to understand, and it works well. It's fun to be able to use a picture from my phone as the background screen on my watch. The silent alarm works well too, it wakes me without waking my spouse, a great feature when couples are on different work schedules. A couple of features I wish the smart watch had- the ability to monitor blood pressure (though I know it's not really a medically approved method), and a reminder to drink water. But these don't really detract from how nice this watch is, so I didn't remove any stars for it. I think this smart watch would make a great gift for someone who is interested in smart watches, but concerned about paying the high price for the brand name models.

title: First one broke, but replacement has been good!
text: UPDATE 11/30/22: I received a warranty replacement for the one that stopped working, and I'm much happier now. I still don't understand what happened to the first one or why it stopped working after 2 days, but so far so good. I just used this on a 14 hour road trip for a weekend, and it broadcast wifi to the whole car without bogging down my phone. It's still working now everytime we drive. I might have just gotten a lemon the first time. Original review follows: I wanted to give this 5 stars because I love the functionality, and it worked pretty great at first! I put a T-mobile SIM card in, and it just worked out of the box like that. When plugged into a Windows PC, it detected it as a modem, and on some Android tablets I was able to select USB tethering and it would work like that as well! And if not, it broadcasts its own wifi hotspot so on devices like my car's Android head unit, I could just find a free USB port and I could connect over Wifi instead. I even managed to get a Verizon sim (Visible wireless) working by manually entering the APN in the web interface. Sure, it didn't have all the bands so the speed was limited (max I got was 25Mbps down and 30 up), but it worked! ...Until it didn't. I noticed that sometimes the speed would suddenly crawl. It would be reduced to 0.5 or less mbps. I was experimenting with it today, my 2nd day of owning it, to try and figure out if some devices perhaps didn't give it enough power or something, or find some common reason it would sometimes drop. And then it happened... it stopped showing up on anything. No Wifi network broadcast, no USB device detected, the unit just suddenly up and stopped. The blue light still turns on (which it did when starting up), but it now never turns green (how you know the connection is live). I left it for an hour to make sure it wasn't just slow, and it just refuses to do anything beyond show me a blue light. No network to connect to, so I can't even log in and see what's going on. There is a reset button pin, I tried jabbing that with a sim tool to reset and that doesn't seem to have fixed it either. title: Exceeds Expectations
text: I have worked with a lot of active noise canceling (ANC) headphones, from a large variety of manufacturers. Every set I have owned, from the top-of-the-line to the weakest and cheapest, has had its strengths and weaknesses. Until recently, Bose was the one to beat when it specifically came to the ANC function, though many others have a better sound profile. My favorite ANC phones are a set of Sony in-ear buds that rival Bose in ANC and trounce them in sound quality. When I found out that Microsoft had made an ANC headphone as well I did not have the highest of expectations – I expected mediocrity with the ANC and a reasonably good sound profile. I was dead wrong in both presuppositions – these are incredible headphones and are worth every penny of the asking price. They’re not absolutely perfect, but they’re better than one could reasonably expect. I’ll start by saying that I am sorry in advance for the lengthy review. I have a lot to say on these, and I would much rather give far too much information than not enough. The present asking price as I write this is $349 and that’s a lot of money to drop, so more information is better. Right out of the box these are relatively nondescript and don’t jump out as either flashy or gaudy. The controls that are visible and obvious are limited – a power button and a mute button, as well as the USB Type C power jack and a 3.5mm audio input jack. There are more controls, though, and it takes a quick glance at the documentation to get a feel for what’s there. There are two dials, one on each can, for controlling the level of ANC and for controlling the volume, L and R respectively. The flat portion of the cans facing outward are multi-function touch controls that allow you to play/pause, skip forward and backward, handle calls and also interact with your digital assistant. I will come back to that last point a little farther down. I powered these on and they immediately announced that they were ready to pair. I paired them to my iPhone and they connected and were usable quickly, faster than most Bluetooth devices I have used. I put them on, tapped once on the right ear and Pandora immediately started up. That was easy! The sound quality is very good though I will stop short of calling it audiophile level. The bass is good and not overdriven. I don’t care for deep, pounding bass and that is a problem with a lot of ANC phones I have used (Dre Beats being among the most aggressive in that regard). It softens as the volume level is dropped and that is very normal. The midrange is actually quite good with the proper amount of depth, so music sounds full and warm and not like it’s being piped through a tunnel. The highs are bright and clear though not piercing. Overall, these are among the most balanced ANC headphones when it comes to the sound profile. I cranked the volume and they don’t distort. The ability to adjust the ANC is not new; however, the way these headphones do it is very innovative and unlike anything else I have seen. The dial that controls it gradually lessens the amount of ANC that is applied when listening, in small but perceivable steps, until the ANC is turned off. With the central air on in the house, I gradually went from no ANC to maximum and it very gradually wiped out all of that sound. When at the maximum level the ANC is almost as aggressive as Bose, with a lot less of the the weird pressure sensation on the ears. A unique feature of these phones is integration with Cortana. When paired to a device running Cortana, simply saying “Hey, Cortana” gets its attention and you can interact with the same types of commands you use on the command line or the search window. It does require that Cortana be properly configured. I have Cortana installed on my iPhone (yes, you can do that and it works well), and the integration with the headphones when connected is good. I had a Windows Phone when they were still available and I would have liked to try this with that phone. There’s some debate on whether these are on-ear or over-the-ear phones. These are very definitely over-the-ear phones unless your ears are huge. The cups are buttery soft as is the head band, and these are very comfortable. I am able to wear them for long periods of time without fatigue. They do get warm after a while, so keep this in mind. One area a lot of headphones skimp on is the carrying case. The case with these phones is better than average. It is crush resistant and provides good protection for the phones. Included within is an audio cable for input as well as a USB charging cable. These headphones bring a lot to the table and were quite a shock for me. I had much lower expectations, for the ANC function in particular but also for the sound profile. Both exceeded my expectations and the comfort factor is the cherry on top. These are excellent phones and I am very pleased with them.

title: Cool idea, but could use improvement
text: This is a really solid idea for bloggers and others who want to mount camera and other recording equipment to their desk. I occasionally do video blog demonstrations and in my home office, that means setting up at least one light stand, a tripod with my camera, and a mic stand. Lots of stands on the floor taking up space. With this, I can clamp the camera to my desk and use the crane arm to adjust- sounds great right? Well- I'll be honest, this isn't designed to carry something like a DSLR or other ILC camera. Sure, I can get it to stay in place with some very delicate movements, but the slightest vibration and the camera wobbles which will show up in your video. I also am constantly afraid the balance will shift, I think this is better suited for a phone or gopro style camera. In fact, I'm fairly certain this crane arm was NOT designed for this use at all. Something about it seemed familiar, and then I recognized it- it is the same desk clamp that came with one of my condenser mics, and I found it even has the 5/8" standard mic thread on the end! Basically, someone took a mass-produced microphone desk clamp and threw in some extra bits to adapt a tripod screw (1/4 20 thread) or phone holder mount to a microphone standard sized 5/8 end. That's it! This kit doesn't even come with instructions so you have to figure it out, but I could honestly have put this together with equipment I already owned if I knew that's all it was. I was hoping it would be a heavier duty product to hold my larger camera. Oh well. It is still useful because I can mount a light to the 1/4" thread and use my other desk clamp to the hold the mic, thus leaving only my camera on the floor tripod. That's handy, but I can't give this more than 3 stars. I wish I could give 2.5, it's useful enough that it's not worth only 2 but 3 is being generous. Seems like a good idea that was thrown together without enough thought in the details.

title: Best headphones for the gym and doing random things around the apartment with I have ever used!
text: I've been wanting to write a review for this for a long time but kept forgetting. I've put these through hell and I've had them ever since they first came out with the X2's. They are by far the best headphones I have ever used. I bought them not from Amazon but from Best Buy. Maybe Amazon is selling slightly used ones to have all these bad reviews? I don't know but just a guess. Pair the headphones to your phone and hold the middle button down for 5 seconds to power down. All you gotta do then is hold the middle button down to turn on until the lady says "power on", let go of the button when she says this -- and boom -- "head phones connected". Very very easy. I use it mainly for the gym and doing stuff around my apartment. I sweat like crazy and they still hold up like a champ. They fit so well into your ears they are completely locked in. Sound quality is very good and they are also very loud if you like to blast music like me (rap, metal, anything sounds good with these). Bass is solid for headphones. Don't really understand all the negative reviews on this but I have had these for at least 2-3 years and they are holding up perfectly. If you buy these DEFINITELY take the time to watch a YouTube video on how to fold the cord with the brackets they give you so it fits nicely around the back of your head. Very simple and doesn't take long, you only have to do it once and you're good. Only complaint is VERY VERY rarely you won't hear anything when you hold down the middle button and you have to plug the charger in and press the middle button 9 times and repeat this process until you hear "power on". Usually takes a few tries but this has rarely ever happened so I still give 5 stars for these headphones. Don't listen to these reviews and go out and try it for yourself. They really are the GOAT and I have tried a lot of headphones.

title: Amazing
text: These are without doubt the best headphones I've ever owned. The sound quality is incredible- rich and full without over-emphasizing any part of the sound spectrum. Bass is amazing and brain scrambling. Mid range is clear and precise and not at all obscured by the lower and upper registers. Highs are crisp and clear. When I put them on for the first time, the fit was comfortable. They are very good at blocking outside sounds. The first music I found on my IPod that I wanted to hear was Dvorak's 9th Symphony. Wonderful. Instruments were distinct and clear. Horns sounded rich and full. A little Springsteen and the Beatles' Sgt. Pepper confirmed that these phones will handle a broad range of musical styles. Sgt. Pepper was the album that made an audiophile of me back in the 60's when it was released. At that time, I had a cheap portable stereo with a tilt out turntable and fold out speakers. I wore out Sgt Pepper on that thing, and then, a friend happened to have it playing on his parents' high end Marantz receiver and impressive speakers when I stopped by one day. I heard things from that music on his system that I had never heard on my cheap stereo, and I was hooked. These phones at less than a hundred dollars give me everything I heard on that high end system all those years ago. I've seen headphones listing for a hell of a lot more, and I can't imagine that they can sound that much better than these Sony's to justify spending several times more for them. I consider these to be an incredible bargain worth every penny.

text: Like most households today, we have so many cables. It seems like every device needs a different type of cable, be it HDMI, USB-A, USB-C, USB-mini... it's nuts! I personally can't wait for all of our devices to be switched over to the current industry standard USB-C so I can at least reduce *some* of the clutter! But until that day comes (if ever), I am trying to at least organize our cable collection to where we can grab what we need without having to sift through a sea of black cables, examining all of the ends looking for the right one. Enter these Avyxm USB C to C 4 foot cables. I chose the red and LOVE THEM!! They are very vibrant and bright making it stupid easy to quickly grab the correct cable for our USB-C devices!! The cables are nice and thick but still with a bit of bend allowing for maneuvering around furniture legs or nightstand lamps without putting a kink in them. And one of the biggest props? These cables FAST CHARGE everything I've connected them to. Our Samsung Galaxy S8 and S10 phones, my new Fire HD8 Plus tablet and my kids' Samsung S10 plus phones. The 4 foot length is perfect for bedside nightstands although it is a little too short to reach the couch and still use the phone or tablet in a comfortable position -- so I'll be grabbing a couple of the 6 foot cables for living room use. Bottom line... the color is fantastic, the build quality is supreme, I don't expect they'll ever kink given their thickness, I love the braiding and feel of the fabric -- overall a high quality product that I have and will continue to, highly recommend!

title: Lose Weight, Feel Great--No Subscription Fees
text: I love this gadget! It's very comfortable. I probably would have bought it just for the watch--time and date synched to the Internet--with no metal touching my skin (I'm allergic to nickel and watches make me break out.) It keeps track of my footsteps and movement while I'm asleep, It syncs to a free fitness community where I can enter everything I eat. (I admit, I don't always do this, but I've learned how many calories were in some foods I thought were healthy snacks. No wonder I gained so much weight!) Anyway, in a little more than a month, I've lost 10 pounds and I'm getting in shape for spring hikes, in spite of 27 degree high temps in our area. I don't have a smart phone. You don't need one for this. You sync the device with your computer. There's no subscription or any charge outside of the price of the band. It lets you know how many steps you have walked, how many miles you have gone, and how many calories you have burned, as well as the date and time. It's easy to read, and a red line tells you when to get up and move, if you've been sitting at your computer for too long. I recommend it!

title: Once this drive is format in NTFS, works great. 5-stars.
text: I have used this product for 5 years, and it works fine. Just make sure you format for NTFS, else you may experience a nightmare below. Do not read below, unless you feel necessary NOT TO FORMAT ( and be stupid, like me. ) ------------ I am editing the stars to 4 star - but cannot do it on amazon. After you understand the pitfalls (you must format this drive NTFS first,) then all other aspects of it are very good. So, below -- just keep in mind that I was "upset". If you do not format this external drive - be ready for some real problems. Forget the 2-page manual that says "preformatted." You need to format this one. Or else ... The WD 250 boasts on being an external drive, but neglects to mention that it must be unplugged from the computer before power down. Well, clumbsy me, I made the same mistake twice with this product. The desktop and the laptop both recognized the MyBook drive "ok" and had no problem writing data files to it. So, I thought all is well. But, in both cases, once the PC was turned off - it became a nightmare. Since both my PCs run in XP Prof mode, with all service pack software updated, latest and greatest versions of everything, I would assume nothing should go wrong. Not so. In both cases, the Xp operating system was damaged to "the point of no return." Furthermore, all data on my PCs were lost. Now - why did I buy this external hard drive? Ok, this is not the first time some hardware has gone wrong on my PCs, but usually it does not take out the entire operating system - such that I cannot run a "restore," and get back to working. After calling WD on the phone, they suggested that I must unplug the MyBook each and everytime, before turning off the PCs. I would have given this external drive a 4 star rating - if it was not for the bad experiences, and the loss of time, loss of data from my PC.
