title: Great radio, but very high battery drain.
text: This radio looks really good wherever you place it. Ours looked exactly as advertised. The design is not obtrusive, doesn't require a huge footprint, and the buttons are easy to use. The only DOWNSIDE I found is a major one - the battery consumption is apparently off the charts. I didn't put cheapie batteries in it either - I got the blue lithium Energizer batteries. That is the only one we use in our digital cameras due to their ability to last. I take an average of 500 photos a month between my home business and our family and pets, and get at least a month of camera usage from a set. (I date them all with a Sharpie so I know how long they last.) Even when they are spent for camera purposes, they still have enough power in them to get our haircutters going, so they are great batteries. Anyway, I put in a brand new set of the blue lithiums 24 DAYS ago. I had the radio turned on to program it, but other than that, turned it off until/unless severe storms were in the area. We hadn't had any, so it wasn't sitting in the "on" position, listening to the regular radio, etc etc. We had reason to use it this past Saturday, April 24th, when severe storms hit our area. (Part of that huge system of tornados that hit so many states.) I hadn't thought about it much, even though there were bad thunderstorms in the area, but there was no warning light on the radio. (It has three small lights on the left side that light up if there is a watch or warning in your area, and you can turn it on to hear the NWS warning.) However, suddenly, our town's tornado warning went off! We'd never heard it go off in the 3 yrs we've lived here, so it was a big deal. We headed downstairs for safety, and I grabbed the radio to take along. Guess what - dead as a doornail. THREE WEEKS without even being turned on, and the brand new lithium batteries were DRAINED!!! O-M-G. There ended up being a tornado only a few miles from us that did quite a bit of damage. I would hate to think what could have happened if I relied on this radio for our only information!! Once the worst had passed us, I sent my son back upstairs for more batteries. We popped them in and immediately had the weather warning lights and broadcasts. While that was good, it could have EASILY been too little too late. I cannot even believe how this thing ate the batteries already that power my digital camera for a month at a time with constant usage! There is NO low battery warning light at all, nothing to tell me that it was not working. No beeps, no lights, nothing but dead air when we needed it the most. If it had been nighttime and we were asleep, who knows what could have happened! Note to Midland - you NEED a low battery light! Make it blue, so it does not get confused with any weather warning lights! This is a potentially life-saving product - go the "extra mile" here and provide a notice of some kind when the power is gone. I never expected the batteries to be drained after about 3 wks without even using the radio. If you get this radio, I highly advise keeping it plugged in somewhere. I do not know if the batteries will continue to drain while it is also plugged in (without being turned on), so I'll revise my review when I know. I am now on a hunt for the adapter that was supposed to come with it. I do not remember seeing one in the package. I just got the original box back out this morning to get the adapter, and there's nothing in there. So, it is possible that the box I got didn't get one put in there. I hadn't planned on plugging it in, but now see that might be my only real way to get dependable notices. I'll check the radio to see what voltage the adapter should be, and see if we have an extra one from some other electronic item. This is the really bad weather season for us (Midwest), and it is very important this radio do its job! Do NOT rely on the batteries, and keep a spare set of batteries on each floor of your home (if you have an upstairs or basement, make sure you have some available for flashlights and radios on EACH FLOOR - I had not even thought about that, but we keep our batteries upstairs, and did not want to go back up during the actual tornado warning!). I have flashlights tucked every conceivable place in the home (including the bathroom, under the kitchen sink, etc etc), but never really gave a second thought to having extra batteries available per floor until last Saturday when we needed them. Lesson learned! S.A.M.E. technology is a must - don't consider any weather radio that doesn't have it, or you'll hear a lot of stuff that doesn't even pertain to your location. This radio has it. Note though that on this model, you have to choose the ALERT ON option to ON or you won't get the warning lights. (Odd that it is not a default to on, but it isn't.) You have the choice of a LOT of various types of warnings that will pertain to your area, which is great. You choose which warnings to turn on, except for tornado warnings. This is defaulted to the "on" position, although the manual does not tell you that. I wrote to Midland to ask about it. It took them two weeks to answer me, but they confirmed it was a default setting, which is why it was not included in the storm warning options lists. In summary - great radio, great look, beyond-poor battery usage. I've taken off one star for the poor battery life - I've never seen any electronic use them faster. The lack of a low battery light or beep is a huge mistake for this type of product. It takes four AA batteries, so be sure to keep a pack of them available on each floor of your home.

title: Works perfectly, but there are things you need to know
text: I received this as a free review sample five and a half months ago and it's been invaluable. If your laptop has a fully functioning internal fan this array will augment that and keep your laptop cool even in unairconditioned spaces on a hot day. That will definitely prolong the life of the internal electronics and the hard drive. This fan array moves a lot of air, promoting excellent heat transfer. However, it can interfere with other USB peripherals if you power it directly from your laptop, and especially if you add even more peripherals to the ports integrated into this unit. That is because the power draw form USB ports may exceed the capacity of the laptop, especially if you are running it off an unpowered USB hub. I had that problem when I tried running this, a mouse and a speaker from my laptop. I moved this one to a powered hub and the problem was immediately resolved. If you are set in your ways regarding keyboard height know that this raises a laptop 1 3/8 inches higher at its lowest setting. For me it took a day to become accustomed to the raised typing height, but since it solved a genuine problem the tradeoff was worth it. Just know that you will need to adjust to that and it may slow down your productivity for a day or two. One final note: if you live or work in a high dist environment take the time to shut this fan array down and blow it out with compressed air. It will greatly prolong the life of the array and will also ensure that it doesn't become noisy.

title: Dell Skimped on the RAM; Otherwise, a Decent Multimedia UltraBook
text: I have worked with a large number of computers by various manufacturers, and I can say that each one has had its strengths and weaknesses. I was really intrigued by this one with the 8th generation i3 chip – I didn’t even realize that the i3 was still being developed or produced. My experience with this laptop (and its chip) has been a mix of positive and negative, though the positive does outweigh the negatives I see. I will start by addressing the elephant in the room. This system comes with only 4GB of DDR4 RAM. Why? The standard anymore is 8GB unless all you’re doing is surfing the web and writing documents. I am not sure what possessed Dell to release a machine with that little RAM running Windows 10. So, my first order of business was popping the case open and expanding to 8GB, which was easy with a single 8GB stick. There are two slots in there, and 8GB works in one slot, so I am hopeful I can bump it to 16GB. While I was in there, I was in for a surprise. There is a hard drive bay, and it’s empty. The system registers 256GB in a solid state flash, which is elsewhere (I did not see it but I know it's in there). Oddly enough, the Dell software does not recognize the increased RAM though Windows and the system BIOS definitely see it. That’s a minor issue, not a real concern. It does need to be kept in mind though – it could be confusing for the unaware. This is an UltraBook system, meaning that some shortcuts were taken to keep things light and small. The main thing missing is a CD/DVD drive which is hit or miss on UltraBooks. There is HDMI for video output but no other video output interfaces. Surprisingly, there is no USB type C port, but there are three type A ports. There is also an audio jack and an Ethernet port, as well as an SD card reader. The power button doubles as a fingerprint reader. I booted the system, set it up and was on the network downloading updates within about 10 minutes. I was presented with a fairly clean desktop and not too much extra fluff n’ stuff. The system was a bit sluggish for the first hour as things were updated and cached, but it has settled down now and things are reasonably peppy. I have done little with the graphics beyond loading a video and looking at image files, though I expect it will handle light gaming and graphics work like photo editing, though the latter would definitely benefit from having 8GB instead of 4. Windows did not activate automatically and I needed to run the troubleshooter before it activated. I am not sure what went wrong there, but it was a temporary issue and very quickly and easily resolved. I suspect there was a network issue. The screen is slightly less bright than that of my Acer and Toshiba systems, though that’s not necessarily a bad thing. The screen is not as harsh to look at, and the resolution is good, so I would consider this a plus. Pictures and image files look clear and sharp with good color. The real surprise is the sound, which is much better than I expected and is great for watching videos without requiring headphones or speakers. I will still use BT output when I want to listen to anything important (music or a movie), but the built-in speakers are more than adequate. I like the keyboard, though I do not care for the touch pad. The pad is on par with most, neither better nor worse, and I am not a fan of touch pads. It’s nice that it can be turned off, and it is nice that it’s available if it’s needed. The system comes pre-installed with McAfee which is unfortunate but not unexpected. I stripped that joker off in a hurry and installed something for which I already have a full license. There’s also the trial for Office 365 which I unceremoniously uninstalled. Other than those two, there isn’t much bloat on the system. So, my main major complaint is that Dell went cheap and only put in 4GB of RAM instead of 8. Adding 4GB is very economical – less than $30 – so I fail to understand why that shortcut was taken. It’s easy enough to add after the fact, though, and the overall cost of the system is quite reasonable. This is not a heavy gaming rig by any stretch of the imagination, it’s instead a multimedia and light-duty graphics workstation which is what I need the majority of the time. I’m satisfied with this system in spite of the skimpy RAM (which can be fixed cheaply). If you need a system that isn’t for heavy graphics or gaming, this one is a reasonable choice.

title: How to look like a pro (even if you're not)
text: This Targus notebook case is a great way to carry around all your stuff and still look cool, professional and ready for action. The good stuff: 1. It can fit notebooks up to 17" securely and safely. There's a strap to hold your equipment in place, it's nicely padded and has an adjustable inner wall for extra snugness. It even opens like a briefcase for easy packing. 2. The same compartment has three pockets with Velcro closures, a zippered mesh pocket and some slots for letters, warranty cards or other documentation. There's ample space for a mouse (optical, cordless, furry with tail), flash drives, cds and dvds, cords, headphones, bells and whistles. 3. The zipper for the notebook section can be locked with a padlock. 4. The front flap has an easy fastener, and also has a zip pocket, right at the front of the bag, perfect for passport and tickets when traveling 5. Lift the flap for another compartment, and more pockets, with slots for pens, glasses, keys, and whatever else you like to carry around. 6. The back has a padded Velcro-closing pocket for newspapers, magazines or other reading matter. 7. Yet another zippered compartment has two slots for important documents and not so important documents. 8. The handle is tough rubber, defying you to mess with it, and the bottom and sides are protected with a corrugated rubber strip. You can set this one down on the floor - it can take it. 9. Even if you aren't carrying around a laptop all the time, this case works fine as a large briefcase for work or travel, with lots of room for a change of clothes, product samples, toiletries (make sure they adhere to airline safety regulations), files, manuals, snacks, extra undies and more. The other stuff: 1. If you have a 17" laptop, there's not enough space in the padded section to hold your power cord, but there are so many other compartments and pockets that this shouldn't pose a major problem - other than remembering where you put it. (There's plenty of space for a bottle of Ginkgo Biloba) 2. It's pretty durn heavy to lug around, especially for those already having to contend with a full handbag, cup of coffee and high heeled shoes 3. The generous number of compartments encourages you to carry around more stuff than you need - just in case of emergencies. (And then of course you can't remember where you packed them in the first place) 4. It tends to fall over if you don't put it down carefully or lean it against something. 5. The strap looks a little cheap compared to the rest of the case, and keeps spinning around, so you always have to check that the padded piece is on the inside, but it's easier to carry it by the handle anyway. title: Light & simple, ***UPDATED***
text: Overall I think these are decent walkies. If you want basic push button to talk and small lightweight with adequate range. This is a decent choice. Choosing this model for the Secret Service earpiece can be hit and miss. Guess some earpieces can be just as bad as the black over the ear units the other companies use. In all but a library, the earpiece audio is too low to hear in outdoor conditions. ***see update*** The RT22 cost the same as other models with displays and buttons (thicker and heavier walkies) to control functions this model has to do with button combos, but it does talk to you as you change settings and channels. Sadly as with many other models I tried. VOX is near useless. VOX works as bad as any sub $100 walkie I've tried. It lags and maybe sends half the message at best. You won't know how good/bad VOX is for you till you try it yourself. Get these for size, lightweight, basic, rechargeable walkie needs. Try to catch it on sale...if possible. I paid a fair amount. Btw, my wife and I use these for trail rides on our e-bikes. The true two-way communication devices did not fit our non-helmet style and will cost substantially more. ***UPDATED*** Company did reach out to offer a refund because they thought I was unhappy. And only the earpiece was a bit of let down for me. They told me about another similar earpiece that seems to be working a bit better and now I can actually hear outdoors with the earpiece. It is still a Secret Service style, but somehow it is a bit louder. Maybe my original earpieces were faulty. Let me be clear. I still paid the retail price, kept the walkies and did not take any money/refund for my review. The walkie has so far been a great trail ride walkie. I might have received a bad earpiece. The company seems to care about their ratings and customers use of their products. And we all need to keep what we buy if it works to avoid landfill waste. I just came back to add a star and let others know. Good walkie. And maybe consider one of Retevis's many other earpieces or handset mics. Some of their other walkies include better headsets.

title: really great tablet, just a minor design flaw
text: Let me state that again, wish there were half stars because this is more like a 4.5 review than a four, but it's not quite a five. The Sony Z4 is a really nice tablet - it's very slim, has a sharp screen with great resolution, is mostly intuitive (could figure out most things with it pretty easily) and yes, it's great for playing videos and media. It IS an Android-based tablet, so when surfing the net (Chrome browser is supplied), some sites might not load as you'd like because they are loading based for Android and might lack some other OS features you're used to on other tablets or laptops. But you can have multiple tabs opened without much lag or issues. And again, it handles the videos really well, and takes decent pictures. The issue I do have with this? The power button is not recessed but a nice shiny silver button that sticks out on the side so when you're holding this, you (if you're anything like me and the boyfriend) might tend to hit it accidentally SEVERAL times while using the tablet. Yes, it sticks out that much. Yes, it's that sensitive. Fortunately the tablet powers up very quickly, but again, to me it's a design flaw. Not sure if a cover will fix it or not. BUT, the tablet did come with a free keyboard, which is really nice if you want to use it for any long-term typing (or hate typing on the screen). That was an unexpected bonus. As well is being able to expand the memory and a pretty good battery life. Now, obviously I'm not going to try the water resistance aspect of this table (not unless offered a free one should it fail), but this is a very nice, sealed tablet so I think it should be good on the dust and water side. Don't quote me, but yes, it's thin and doesn't seem to have too many obvious cracks and such. It looks well made, acts well made and over all is really good. Just have to get used to that annoying power button sticking out on the side.

But this is a happy surprise because, boy, do I have cables that need taming, the main one being the cable for the gaming keyboard I bought myself as a retirement gift. I don't game, but I wanted a clicky keyboard! But being a gaming keyboard, it comes with a big thick cable that has branches so you can plug headphones into it, and there it was dangling under my desk like a snake, in the way all the time. Well, no more! The nice thing about these cable clips is that you have an assortment of them, the smallest being an inch in diameter. There are a couple that hold one cable, several that hold two cables, two that hold three, and two others that hold five. The keyboard cable no longer dangles in my way, but is tucked safely up and holding very firmly, and I look forward to taming several more cables that infest our bedroom where we charge our gadgets.

title: Very nice product, but documentation is pretty limited
text: This is a very nice package, containing a decent DAC with a bluetooth receive/transmit capability (supporting AptX HD and LDAC). Now that I have it set up and connected, I'm very happy with the sound quality, both through the stereo amp to speakers, and through bluetooth headphones using LDAC. For the price, this is really an excellent value, I haven't found anything else nearly as good for this price. Setup is a bit involved and tricky, and the documentation is quite poor. (Fiio has this reputation.) I hooked it up to my computer using USB, to update the bluetooth & DAC firmware. Then I used the Fiio Control app on my phone to complete the setup, choosing the desired EQ settings and BT codecs. Pairing the headphones was a bit tricky: I found very sketchy instructions on their forum (there's also a Youtube video), which worked after several tries. But the end result is that I have excellent results for a very moderate price. So if you're a bit technically inclined and not afraid of doing some digging and figuring things out, check this product out. I would recommend it highly.

title: More than enough for a grade school notebook with gaming capabilities...
text: THE GOOD: -------------- * Aesthetically, a very cool looking laptop. Looks menacing, looks high tech. Makes me think of Alienware. * Ram is not soldered, and is upgradeable. * SSD drives are upgradeable too. * Windows 11 Home out the box. * Comes with 3 months of Ultimate Game Pass from Xbox/Microsoft. Doesn't say this anywhere in the listing, so it was a pleasant surprise for my kid. You find this out during the process of setting up the laptop. * Advertised for gaming, but hardcore gamers will find it a little light. But for a child using it as a school laptop, this is perfect. Much better than the Chromebooks, and for Roblox and Minecraft, which is all my kid does, this is more than enough. * Nevertheless, if you want to boost the ram and ssd, you'll need to know how to remove the bottom cover. Search YT for, "Lenovo IdeaPad Gaming 3i (15", 2022) - disassembly and upgrade options." Click on the very top search result. The video is by LaptopMedia. They disassemble several similar models, but this is the term you want to search to get to the exact model. However, keep in mind that he's disassembling the intel version which uses DDR4 ram, you'll be using DDR5 for this Ryzen model. THE BAD: ------------ * Screen is only 1080P. * Ram is only 8GB, but again, you can upgrade this. * If truly using for gaming, you'll need more than 256GB. My son will mainly use it for school, so this won't be an issue. * No touchscreen. * Regarding power supply... Not necessarily a bad, but a tip. If you have children, then you know when they take their tablets and notebooks to relatives or friend's house, the power supply goes too. Instead of constantly unplugging the one that is supposed to be stationary at their table, I always buy a second power supply. For this model, it's a 170W with the Lenovo rectangle usb head. You can buy any 3rd party, just make sure it's 170W with the rectangle head. Despite listing some items in the bad list, I understand it's par for the course in terms of the price you're paying. If you want all of those features included, your price would go up. My kid loves it, we'll go 4-Stars overall for now.

title: Very Deep Bass
text: Sound Intone makes good, inexpensive headphones and the HD-680 headphones is one of their newest offerings. I will admit to initially disliking this particular set of headphones, but a conversation with the manufacturer and persistence with a 16-channel equalizer has brought my opinion of these headphones up substantially. I can’t say these are my favorite headphones as I am not into heavy bass, but I am pleased enough with them to use them when working on my computer. Right out of the box, these are striking phones that are quite attractive. I have the blue and white edition and my teenage son’s first words upon seeing them was, “those look great!” These are very lightweight and as a result they have a light fit. I personally prefer a headphone that fits more tightly but that’s a personal preference, not a criticism. The padding is firm but not hard and I did notice that these phones get a little warm on the ears over time - again not a big deal but worth mentioning. When it comes to the build quality, the strongest feature of these phones is the cord - I am exceptionally partial to woven tangle-free cords versus plastic coated wire. The difficulty I had with these phones initially was the sound profile. The bass is exceptionally heavy, so heavy that it drowns out the highs quite a bit and the result is a sound that is muddy and unclear. It took some time to realize that this is very reminiscent of the sound you get at an outdoor concert - the bass carries forever and the highs get lost. The midrange is absolutely solid. I don’t care for bass that’s so heavy (it gives me a headache) so I fed everything through a 16-channel equalizer. It took a good bit of experimentation and persistence but I finally was able to find the balance to get the sound right and now these phones sound great. The issue is overdriven bass; once I squelched some of the bass and boosted the treble channels just right the profile is solid. They still sound a little like being in a concert hall but that’s more than acceptable. Oddly enough, these sound good when listening to straight speech, even without an equalizer. These headphones are different than most other phones I have used. The typical headphone I use does not boost the bass to an extreme. If you really, really like bass, then these phones are for you and will not disappoint. If you prefer something a little more balanced, feed the sound through an equalizer and the sound can be made near-perfect. Although I did not initially like these phones I have come to appreciate them and can recommend them. Disclaimer: I was provided a sample of this product in exchange for a fair, honest and thorough review. Even though I received this product at no cost I have rated and reviewed this product without bias, based solely on my experience with it.

title: Looses interface too frequently
text: The DLINK COVR was setup to a replace an Apple AirPort Extreme. The setup was relatively simple via the iOS App. Each access point needs to be setup individually. This product is brand new for DLINK and the iOS app felt like a 1.0 product. The iOS app did get an update during my testing but it’s still a bit crashy and does not offer as many features as the web interface. Hopefully there will be more firmware updates. Pros - Units are small and unassuming - Allow you to disable the bright white lights (warning lights will still function). - Supports multiple connected devices (over 30 WiFi enabled devices) without issue. Cons - Primary access point needs to be rebooted (it seems like once a week) as it loses access to the Management interface (via the Web Page and App). - Comes with different color faceplates for the access points (Metalic Blue and Copper) but it only comes with one of each color. If you want to change the color of the other access points you need to purchase two more faceplates. - Product is still new. Other more refined products on the market from NetGear, Linksys and Eero.

title: Fine Two-Pack of Cables
text: If you own an iPhone, iPad or one of the newer iPods you are familiar with the need for lightning cables. These are basically special USB cables that are reversible and are specific to Apple electronics. The problem with these cables is that they can be expensive, especially if you go with Apple-branded cables. There are some good third-party cables available, and they represent a good value when compared to Apple cables. I get offered a LOT of lightning cables for review and I have noticed an unpleasant trend: most vendors hawking their cables to not mention MFI certification at all, anywhere, and this is important. This particular cable is clearly marked in the description as certified, so it is worth some consideration on that basis alone when compared to many of the others available. These come in a two-pack, and they are a little more than 3’ in length. These have hard plastic plugs on the end and the length is insulated with a vinyl sheath for protection. So far these have shown no signs of fatigue at the plugs and they work very well. I have been able to charge my phone as well as connect it to iTunes. I attempted to pull the shielding away from the plug and was unable to do so, so I see no risk of exposing the wires inside the casing. I mentioned the certification above, and I will restate that this is a very important feature. Because these cables are certified, they are guaranteed compatible and will work. If you use a cable that is not certified you might get messages on your phone or tablet that the cable is unrecognized and it might not work as desired. I have worked with non-certified cables and sometimes they work while others do not work at all. I have never had a certified cable fail me. If you are looking for a less expensive alternative to Apple-branded cables, make sure you select a product that has been certified to work. This two pack of cables is a good choice; so far, they have given me no trouble at all and they have worked well for me. They are definitely compatible and are made well too. And, getting two of them instead of just one in the package means you can have one in the office and one at home. Disclosure: I was provided a sample of this product in exchange for a fair, honest and thorough review. Even though I received this product at minimal or no cost I have rated and reviewed this product without bias, based solely on my experience with it.

title: Nice kit, but not for charging.
text: UPDATE LE DEUCE: Turns out the adapters can be used with other cables, but only if the USB-A adapter provided is linked to the other end. Plugging an adapter into the USB-C end of a cable with an USB-A port on the other end doesn't work. But if you have a cable with USB-C ports on both ends, then you can use any of the adapters as long as the included USB-A adapter is plugged into the other end. Now I know why they included it. Updated from 3 to 5 stars based on customer service and the fact that it just werks. UPDATE: With my first review, I thought it was the adapters that were the issue. However, I discovered that the adapters work only with the cable they come with. While I'm happy that they work, I'm disappointed that they work only with the cable provided because the cable is very short. This also means I can use only one adapter at a time. I hoped I could use these adapters to charge multiple devices at once. Unfortunately, that's not the case. Original review follows: Great little kit that gives me most adapters I need (the one missing is a miniUSB adapter) in an all-in-one kit. What I also like is that there are little compartments for microSD. I toss it in my electronics organizer. While I hoped it would eliminate the need for extra cables and my microSD case, I may need to get my other cables back. What I noticed immediately is that they're not meant for charging. I used the adapters to charge a copule devices and no power was drawn. At first I thought it was the cable, but the cables I've used are brand new, name-brand cables, and work fine without the adapters. So I tried other cables and there wasn't any power when charging. They work fine for data management, so I'll still keep the kit around.

I have a large house, but I can still reach my router with this lengthy cable. The main reason I got it is so that one of my kids can obtain a faster connection when she does streaming, gaming, and other things. This connects snugly on each end and it does make things move along faster. I like that this cable is flat- it makes it easier to keep it out of your way. It also comes complete with 20 small fasteners. With these, you can clip it to the wall and run it, say, around the edges of a doorway, keeping it safely removed from the floor area where someone might trip over it. Ethernet connections can speed up processes and this ethernet from GLCON is an excellent one overall. It performs as promised, is easy to use, and is designed to last.

title: Cisco wants more money for VPN Client so they'll let it break after 1-2 years
text: I've been a network engineer and architect for over 15 years, with most of the time configuring and installing Cisco gear. I bought this for my home for two goals - VPN in and better firewall protection than what an ASUS router comes in with. I prefer working on CLI, in the last few years with the push towards GUI, Cisco ASA's have become a lot less intuitive when configured from the CLI. GUI isn't so great either. I've known Cisco to provide hardware and then charge extra for Licenses to enable features in the Enterprise space. So for home use I expected them to ask more money for Firepower etc. I didn't feel the need for firepower so I didn't bother with that. I do care about ability to VPN in - which at the time when I purchased it was provided along with VPN client software for Windows and Mac (version 3.1). Now < 2 years later, after a routine OS upgrade on my MacBook, the VPN client stops working. I contact Cisco and jump through a few hoops just to get a ticket opened. And then I am told that I need to buy a contract/upgrade in order to download version 4.x. This to me is a new low for a company I once admired. I refuse to do that because I've already paid for that functionality when I bought the ASA. I expected a company like Cisco to support the features I paid for until the End of life of the product. I'm not expecting configuration or installation assistance or any new features from them. Simply a basic VPN client that was promised when I bought the Firewall. But their Supports unwillingness to do that leaves me with no choice but to stop supporting/recommending Cisco going forward. If you are a home user or a small business owner then give other competitors a fair shake.

The Intel graphics don't seem to lag and you can even run games with decent framerates. The speakers are good enough. The screen can get reflective if there's a lot of light behind you. The battery says it last 7 hours though with full screen streaming video it lasts three. There's a sliding shutter over the camera. There's not a lot of bloatware, just a few apps that are easy to delete.

title: The best bang for you buck - bar none
text: Let me start off by saying that I have used many, many different earbuds and headphones over the years, and these are by FAR the best bang for my buck in terms of quality, durability, and comfort. I've always managed to land jobs where I'm able to listen to audio content throughout my workday, and have consequently spent most of my working hours with at least one earbud tucked into my ears. I thread these down to my pocket where they are plugged into my iPod full time. No matter how much running, walking, lifting, jumping or moving around I do (and I work as a package delivery guy, which means hundreds of movements and ten miles of walking a day), these things take ages to wear out. Under normal use, I'm sure they'd never need replacing unless you managed to blow out the speaker somehow, or cause blatant damage to them. They don't cut in and out from wear on the jack end or on the headphone ends like some other earbuds, which will give way after being cranked around a bit. Next, I have to address the comfort of the ergonomically shaped buds themselves. They are formed to fit into ear, with the speaker being offset from the center of the housing. On others, such as the classic white Apple earbuds, the round shape causes me some discomfort and soreness after hours of wear. But these are just fantastic. I wear them for up to 11 hours at a time with no discomfort whatsoever. Finally, the sound quality. While an audiophile purist will say that they probably don't meet the highest standards in the land, they do a pretty good job. They stand up to much more expensive earbuds, and if you use the proper sized adapter for your ear (three sizes included), the speaker isolates sound very well, so you don't even need any noise-canceling feature. I wear them while driving a diesel truck with the doors open driving down the freeway, and I can hear every word of the audiobook I'm listening to. The treble is good. The bass is excellent for such an inexpensive earbud. And the overall sound quality is going to put other in-ear buds to shame. For less than ten bucks, there is no better ear bud out there. I've owned three pairs over the last two years, and I would only have needed one if I didn't abuse them so much with daily use and extreme wear. If you want to try these, but would like to keep a microphone feature for hands-free calls, you can get some identical to these with in-line microphone in the Panasonic RPTCM125K model.

title: Took some time to get used to, but they are great!
text: I'm going to swallow my pride and admit that it took me a REALLY long time to figure out how to get a good fit/seal with these headphones. Honestly, 30 minutes of fiddling with them, and I was ready to give up and send them back - they were uncomfortable and offered no better sound than my $10 pair of bud headphones. BUT...if you happen to have the same problem, hang in there! These headphones really are incredible! What I didn't realize at first was these come with one of the optional "wing" skins already on. I had been trying to put another pair of the wings on over it, and it was awful. But lo and behold, when I took the one skin off and put the correct wing size on...then figured out the right bud size for me...then figured out exactly how to twist them in, allow the wing to hook under the groove in my ear, and press slightly to get a good seal in my ear canal...WOW, these have amazing sound! They have been very easy to pair with any device, I love the charging dock, and I've been able to run them a long time before re-charging. I might be most impressed by the range, though. I do use these when working out, but more often I use them while doing housework, and I can go several rooms apart - putting laundry away, even going outside to dump the garbage - and the sound stays constant. And once I figured out the fit/seal in my ears, they have been comfortable to wear.

title: Very Quiet and Moves a Lot of Air
text: A good laptop chiller pad becomes important when you run a lot of graphics-intensive games or if you are doing anything requiring the CPU to run a heavy load. Heat is the enemy of a computer system and the fastest way to a dead laptop is to not deal with heat issues when there’s a problem. I had a chill pad some time ago and it was sub-par. This one by LotFancy is much better and it does a good job. First off, this is a big chill pad, the same basic dimensions as my laptop but much thicker. I have a 15.6" laptop and this pad is almost a perfect and exact fit, with only a narrow area in the back that extends past the end of the laptop. If you want a small and petite chill pad, this is not the one you want. It does incline too with a metal bar and slots in the case so that the laptop can be angled up. This actually is good for exposing it to even more air. This chill pad connects to a USB port so it can either be plugged into a port on the computer or into a hub. There are two ports on it and both seem to be the same in terms of how the pad operates. That is, the pad operates at the same speed regardless of which port is used. There are also two buttons on the pad for turning it on. One turns on the larger fan in the middle, and the other turns on all the other fans. Two green LEDs next to those buttons indicate that the pad is powered on and working. This pad moves a lot of air and it does a good job of cooling the laptop. But, it does so very quietly which is a huge plus. I have it set up in my home office and the pad makes about as much noise as a desktop computer, maybe a little less. The sound it makes is a gentle hum. When powered on each fan has a bright blue LED in it. While they look cool when there’s no laptop on the pad, they’re all but invisible when the laptop is sitting on top. There isn’t even much of a glow coming from the pad. I turned on the pad, turned out all of the lights and the glow from the laptop screen was enough to overpower any light that might have come from the pad. Except for the low hum I wouldn’t know it was even turned on. This is a good chill pad. It works quietly and it moves a lot of air. Compared to other chill pads I have used this one is very good and I am happy to recommend it. Disclaimer: I was provided a sample of this product in exchange for a fair, honest and thorough review. Even though I received this product at no cost I have rated and reviewed this product without bias, based solely on my experience with it.

title: Great TV! 59" Samsung PN59D6500
text: I was hesitant in purchasing a large TV online without at least seeing the same model at a store first, but I saw this TV with a good blu-ray/3D glasses/Shrek combo that was difficult to pass on. My girlfriend was doing everything she could to convince me not to buy a huge, expensive TV. I was tired of my Samsung 32" LCD TV and wanted something bigger. I still can't believe I pressed the submit order button, but I'm glad I did. I spent 30 minutes weighing the pros and cons while the TV was in my shopping cart. I wasn't sure if it was my imagination or a sales tactic but the quantity shown was going down as I refreshed the description page for this TV as I was deciding. After submitting the order I went through the "there's still time for more research and cancel the order" phase and spent a couple more hours looking up reviews and trying to not look at my gf directly in the eye that night...I survived and a couple of days later two guys carried a giant Samsung box up to my place. The TV is HUGE compared to my old 32" LCD. Physically this TV is beautiful, thin, it has a clear border, and has Samsung's smart tv WIFI access. The picture quality looks great with deep blacks and vivid colors. There are many options to adjust the picture and sound. My friends come by to play games sometimes and I found out that this TV even has a game mode. Another benefit of this model is the realistic 3D effects. You have to wear the 3D glasses to enjoy the 3D effects, which will add to the cost of the TV if you didn't get in on the combo deal. I did find a dead pixel on the screen during my trial period and called Amazon to exchange the TV right away. I was amazed how easy it was to process the exchange and schedule the delivery of the TV. The delivery company Amazon used didn't provide very friendly or timely service but that's not Amazon's fault. The only thing I wasn't informed of until I was surveyed is that the TV delivery service includes setup and disposal of the box and packing material. Those lazy delivery men didn't even offer any of that service but even if they did I wouldn't let them touch my new baby. The second TV didn't have any dead pixels or other issues. I've been enjoying the TV so much it took me almost a year to write this review. I highly recommend this TV for value, it's features, and great picture quality. It has other cool features such as the ability to stream Netflix through WIFI and use your Android phone as a remote control. I was laughing the first time I downloaded the remote app on my Samsung Galaxy phone and tricked my gf into thinking the TV was changing channels by itself. Pros: - great picture quality - lots of customizable settings - gaming mode - realistic 3D - huge 59" screen - loud speakers - Samsung smart tv WIFI DLNA support - use Android phone as TV remote as long as you have Samsung's remote app installed. - stream netflix on the TV without extra equipment - software update through WIFI - connect your laptop to the tv via HDMI cable to use the TV as a computer monitor. Cons: - heavy TV - slight buzz noise when TV is on but not really noticeable unless it's really quiet. - you will be watching more TV than normal. - your friends and family members will come over sometimes uninvited to watch TV or play video games. - wish it had more ports

title: Electronics can become TOO small...
text: After a career in advertising, during which I've been around video cameras of all sizes, weights and shapes, this is my first camcorder for personal use. After one day of monkeying around with it, I'm delighted --with a couple of reservations. It appears to be of very good quality, feels lightweight and yet sturdy (with the exception of the rather stringy hand-strap/lanyard) and the user interfacing seems straightforward and intuitive, once you figure out where things are. I'll be back in a few weeks or months to describe my experiences with the software, data downloading and manipulation, etc. The main criticism I have --more of an observation, really-- is that, for my somewhat arthritic hands, operating the controls are challenging because of their placement on the camera and because the thing is so darned small. (As in the length of a soft drink can --at only 2/3 of the diameter!) This may be simply because I'm unaccustomed to using camcorders, or indeed holding any camera as one does with the JVC GZ-HM550. Many reviewers have complained that this 2010 model still has scrolling and MENU, OK, and INFO buttons rather than Mac-style rolling touch screens. This doesn't bother me, although it takes a very delicate touch to get to the right menu selection on this camera, because they zip by so rapidly under a man's stubby fingers. Low light results seem more than adequate, especially for daytime indoor shooting, and the shake reduction seems to me to be working very well. The zoom function is smooth and quiet, but --for me-- will be difficult getting used to because the toggle from wide to close-up is at the top rear, where it's difficult for me to reach with my admittedly somewhat inflexible fingers. As I said, I'm new to these mini-cameras, so they may be designed for two-handed use...?? All in all, I think it will be a very good mid-range camcorder with the advisory that people with large hands might find a somewhat physically larger brand/model easier to handle. GET A WARRANTY. (Square Trade offers a good policy against damage, etc.) This JVC model is small enough, the finish glossy enough, that I feel it would be fairly easy to drop it.

title: A great alternative to a popular version, but at half the cost...
text: THE GOOD: --------------- * In terms of movement, and smoothness, it's just as good as the popular "L" brand version. * Perfect for browsing your laptop or notebook while laying down. * This is great for desktop as well if you don't have a lot of desk space. * Compared to the popular L brand version, I do feel an improvement in ergonomics. There was a longer "getting use to it" time with the L brand version because my hands wouldn't completely rest on the mouse, and it created this tense feeling, when the objective is to make you feel less stressed and more relaxed. With this MicroPack version, due to a taller design, it took less time to get used to. * The scroll wheel is perfectly placed. * There is also one more button, it's the DPI sensitivity switch button. * The track ball/marble size of the MicroPack is the same size as the L brand blue version, but they ARE NOT interchangeable. * Battery compartment also has a magnetic compartment to house the 2.4 receiver. THE BAD: ------------ * No bluetooth, then again, the L brand doesn't have bluetooth either and it cost twice as much. * Now, I've gone through many L brand ones in my life. They all eventually go bad because the trackball collects debris, and no matter how often you clean the mouse, they eventually go bad. If you don't believe me, look at the picture I uploaded with the 5 blue marbles. That's 5 L brand versions that have gone bad, but I saved the marbles. So I'm anticipating the same sort of life span for this Micropack version, which is about 1 to 2 yrs. * The L brand also beats the Micropack with it's unifying receiver system, as well as a dedicated software. This Micropack mouse simply uses your windows software for configuration. * The L brand also has 2 more buttons next to the scroll wheel that you can customize. Ultimately, I still rate this Micropack vesion 5-Stars! Because though it lacks some features compared to the L brand, it is half the cost. 5-Stars... for now.

The movements feel a bit more muted than my old one. In no way is it BAD, believe me I tried a wireless mouse before this one that was just awful and would frequently disconnect and lag. The keyboard uses 2 AAA batteries and the mouse uses a single AA, both of which come included with the set (nice!). I like this pair and I'm happy with it so far!

title: Only works fully on one of three devices I tried but has a nice stereo sound when it works
text: I tried this speaker on the Google Nexus 7, iPhone 5, and iPad 3. - With the Google Nexus 7 the speaker would only work very well while on Bluetooth connected to Rock Out (the speaker's Bluetooth). If I connected the cable to the headset port, the sound only came out as a whisper. I verified that the volume was on max on both the speaker and the Nexus 7. The documentation says "Know that when you're hardwired in for sound, the external controls are optimized for Apple devices." This explains why my Nexus 7 does not work when wired to the speaker. - With the iPad 3, I was able to connect via the headset port and via Bluetooth without any problems and the sound was adequate but not fantastic even on the highest volume. - With the iPhone 5, I was only able to connect via the headset port. When we connected via Bluetooth no sound would come out of the device. My boyfriend and I tried several times (about 6 times). We tried while on both the cellular network and also on my wi-fi network and were able to connect to Rock Out but never could get the sound to work. There was one time where it disconnected itself when we went back to look but except for that once, it remained connected throughout. There's a Bluetooth Memory reset by turning the speaker on, pressing and holding the power button for 10 seconds and then pairing the device with the iPhone. I verified that the unit was also fully charged. As long as I was connected via the headset cable I could answer calls by pressing the play button on the unit. However, no matter what my boyfriend and I did, we could not get Bluetooth to work on the iPhone 5. The R0S2 is definitely rugged and when it works the sounds is adequate to very good. Only my boyfriend's iPhone 5 with the OtterBox installed would fit into the case of the unit for storage. Anything larger than this won't fit, which means the device has to be hanging off the unit if Bluetooth does not work or Bluetooth will have to be used so that the case can remain zippered up. Based on my measurements, any item that is 5-1/2 inches wide by 2-3/4 inches tall by 1/2 inch thick should fit inside the case. I also weighed the case and it was almost one pound (15.7 ounces). For me it is not worth it to carry an extra pound of gear backpacking. Also, unless one is kayaking, biking, or out in the sun, the unit does not charge in the shade and according to the directions, it takes 8 hours to fully charge via solar power. As long as there was some sun, I noticed the unit charging. As per the directions, it will take about 8 hours to solar charge. I charged mine in about 2 hours using my computer's USB port. The documentation says the run time is 10 hours but after almost two hours of use, I noticed only 2 bars on the charge when I plugged it back in for recharging. The unit can be daisy chained to other Rock Out speakers for more sound. The unit has a 3.5 mm audio jack (line-level) and a 5V USB charging cord. According to the box, the speakers are 3W. The battery is a Lithium-ion, 3.7V, 800 mAh, 3Wh. Another thing I noticed is that because the case bottom is not totally flat, the speaker has to be balanced to sit upright without falling over. Every time I tried it either fell forward or back so I had to lean it against something. Of course if it is secured to backpack or bicycle basket this is a non-issue. In summary, I love the rugged case and think the sound is great to just okay depending on the item or material being played or the device it is paired with. I like that movies can be paused just by pressing the pause button on the speaker or that I can go to the next song by pressing the advance button. I have a hard time believing that backpackers will want to carry this almost one-pound speaker with them especially if hiking in the woods where there is usually no sun to charge the unit. Even though the bungee cord around the outside of the unit is thin, it has some clip hooks attached for fastening to a backpack or bicycle basket that should keep it secure. I like the olive green color because it is camouflaged for nature as it was designed to take backpacking. I give this 2.6 stars and recommend with reservations because the unit does not always work with the headset jack or on Bluetooth. Whether it is going to work or not apparently depends on the unit being connected to the device. When it works it's great; when it doesn't it is disappointing. I think the current price of $129.99 is also a bit steep. UPDATE Dec 20, 2014 March 10, 2017 - Today I tried again to pair this with my [[ASIN:B00O0M750W HP OMEN 15-5010nr Gaming Notebook with Beats Audio]] and I was finally able to get it to work where a couple of years ago no sound came out of the speaker. The unit was fully charged as usual. Pairing seems to be hit or miss.

text: The came well packaged, but with only visual images of random possible settings (of every one except the one I needed), it took a few minutes to figure out how to lay it out. No instructions at all. I was left to figure out if I install the bracket behind or in front of the tv mount. In my case I was mounting a TCL 40" LCD with a Vizio 2.0 Soundbar. Laying both items on a solid flat surface is the best way to figure out how best to mount it. I ended up placing the brackets in a manner that allowed me to flush mount the soundbar to the tv, and I don't see the bracket or wires. I laid the bracket on the tv then the articulating mount over the soundbar bracket. If I did it the other way, the brackets would not have lined up over the screw holes. To make the TV mount flat and even, I placed two washers in the upper screw holes of the tv to make up for the thickness of the soundbar brackets that attached to the lower two screw holes on the tv. Overall it looks great, but these brackets probably weren't the best choice for me because the width of the soundbar screws only allowed me to screw the brackets into two holes on the tv instead of four, which I think would have made it more stable. If I push on the soundbar, it will flex backwards a little bit, but I have no actual concern of it falling off. I wish these came with any kind of instructions to guide me to the best possible install. Luckily, I'm smart and made it work. These brackets would have to be about 4 inches longer to make an ideal fit for my situation of widely placed screws on the soundbar, and a narrow VESA pattern of 100*150

title: The good ol' 3.5mm jack!
text: I'm a fan of Panasonic's electronics, and have been since I first used their GH camera line for work, and the DVX prosumer cameras before that. I was excited for a chance to replace my working headphones with ones from Panasonic, however I found it interesting that the plug type was NOT MENTIONED on this listing! The last pair of headphones + mic that I ordered came with a USB plug. It worked fine on my laptop, but not on my phone or tablet, obviously. I want a regular headphone jack, as in 1/8" or 3.5mm (whatever you want to call it!) to be compatible with all my things, and not rely on the limitations of bluetooth. I was a little bit nervous about buying this one since it doesn't say anywhere that it is, in fact, a standard TRRS headphone-jack sized plug, but I'm happy to report that it IS! And the sound quality is what I expected from Panasonic, a good value for the price. One thing to note, however, is that this requires both sides have a wire going to the plug. This is a seriously old-school design that I almost forgot existed. Modern headphones have a single point of cable that splits to the other side in the band, however this is more like earbuds in that there's a wire going to each side. It's not a deal breaker, it works fine and some people may actually prefer that look, but personally I'm worried about it being another point of contact that could get snagged/rip/break/go wrong, and actually prefer the more modern single-side cable style. But this is still a great pair of traditional tried-and-true headphones!

text: I haven't used many wireless mice -- I've almost always just used the track pad on my laptop and was fine with that. But I've been having some problems with the track pad locking up on my new (Mar 2015) MacBook, so I went shopping for a wireless mouse. Clearly, this Logitech M325 model is popular -- hundreds of reviews, most of them good -- and the price is certainly reasonable (depending on the seller and color/print, prices are within a few dollars of each other). So, I ended up ordering this "Freespirit" model -- sleek black with a multi-color "splash" design -- primarily because I like the colors and the overall look of it. I'm not disappointed. In fact, I'm more impressed than I thought I would be. It performs just fine. I don't have a lot of room -- I use my laptop most fo the time on a small rolling stand with just enough room for the computer plus a beverage and a few pens in a pencil cup. No problem -- this thing works on a pad of paper, my leg, on the sofa seat beside me, etc. I wish the scrolling wheel operated a bit more smoothly, but it's not enough of a problem to warrant any drop in star rating. The color was a bit of a surprise, too -- the "splashes" are metallic/pearlized, so they really shine against the black background. Overall, I like this more than I thought I would. I've been using it for about 10 days, and I've noticed no problems at all. I'll definitely update this review if I notice any issues in the future. But so far, I REALLY like it. It's making my life a lot simpler, for sure.

title: Flat Midrange
text: I will start by saying that I am typically not a fan of ear buds. I will also say that the very best headphones I have is a set of Sony active noise canceling ear buds. I have always had good success with Sony audio products and I regard the brand as an upper mid-range line. These buds are disappointing in that, while they’re comfortable for ear buds, the sound profile is lacking, specifically in the midrange. This is a basic set of ear buds that work as you might expect. They do not have ANC functionality, there is no battery or battery pack and there is no Bluetooth functionality. These are straight plug-in audio gear with no fancy features. These will work with just about any device that provides a 3.5mm audio jack. Most ear buds are uncomfortable to me, but these are surprisingly comfortable and I can wear these for a good bit of time. They come with several inserts of varying sizes so you can find the size that fits your ear canal just right. The included inserts are soft and do not irritate my ears as much as others have done in the past. I still very much prefer over-the-ear headphones which is a personal preference more than anything. So, where’s the problem? The problem lies in the sound profile which is unbalanced. Having used literally dozens of sets of ear buds and headphones over the past 3-5 years I have developed a good feel for what sounds right and what doesn’t. Most ear buds have one of two issues, either way too much bass or almost none at all. These are surprising in that they actually get the bass just about right, not too heavy and not too light. The highs are very bright and clear, perhaps a touch too piercing for my tastes but not to the point of being unacceptable. The midrange, though, is flat. The result is that music sounds somewhat sterile and lackluster. This is similar to the issue I have seen with my Bose headset. These still sound good, but they fall short of where they could and should be. Normally I would rate ear buds with a glitch in the sound profile a little higher, as they’re still quite usable and they sound decent. But, these are much more expensive than the average and I have heard less expensive buds with more balanced sound. Sony makes a number of sets of ear buds and headphones, but these are not among their best offerings.

title: Nice Video Light for Vloggers
text: I selected this light for working with my camera, though I have other lights that are good for the tasks I have in mind. I have had good experiences with Logitech products and I had good expectations for this light. For the most part I am pleased with it. This is a small LED-based light that is designed to attach to a flat screen monitor and be used for video lighting. The target audience is very clearly vloggers and video makers, though it can be adapted for other uses with some thought. The main feature of this light which is important enough to mention is that it is a non-strobing LED, meaning no striping when at high shutter speeds. The light has a couple of settings that can be adjusted by buttons on the back. It can be brightened or darkened, and the color temperature can be adjusted as needed. I personally like using a warm setting more than a cool setting, but that's a preference and it changes based on what I am doing. There is some graduation in the levels though they are granular enough for my needs. The head of the light can be tilted forward and backward, so there is some adjustment that can be made. It is not adjustable from side to side, though. Also, it must be mounted on a surface where it can hang, not placed on a desk stand-alone. As such, it is specifically designed for hanging on a monitor. The one thing about this light that I do not care for is that it does not have any battery capacity. That is, it must be plugged in to a USB outlet to work. That's probably again related to being marketed as a vlogging tool, but it does reduce portability when a USB port is unavailable for use. It can be rigged to work with a power bank which is a sloppy work-around, but this is simply a patch. This is a decent light for video work and it is very definitely a tool for vloggers and video makers. It has no battery capacity so it must be plugged in to work, but otherwise it is a nice light that works well.

title: Awesome! Everything Fits!
text: I need more of these! It ends the clutter and ends the hassle of trying to get oversized plugs to not overlap the other outlets. Until there is a universally sized plug standard, plugs come in all different sizes and shapes and when using a normal end-to-end power strip, sometimes only 3 plugs can fit when there is room for 5. I don't know why I waited so long to try a tower outlet, but now that I have, I will buy more of these to replace my narrow strip outlets. I did my due diligence and checked all kinds of towers and read all the reviews and GLCON seemed the best. My bulky plugs are now spaced away from the outlets so instead of having to unplug one plug to use the vacuum cleaner, I now have 5 empty slots to choose from. At age 75, always learning and I am glad I learned about this tower's capabilities.

title: Port Replicator not a true docking station
text: I'm rating this 4 stars since there is a bit of a gap in what Dell is calling a Docking Station versus what this unit really is. This is essentially a port replicator meaning that you have to have a separate power source to charge the laptop. If it were a true docking station you would be charging your laptop simply by being plugged into it. For this device you plug via USB and having an extra power plug in. Now the good stuff. It works really well and I run triple monitors. Please keep in mind that if your monitors are a bit older and have the pin style plugs you will need adapters to convert to DVI /new style HDMI. Would I buy this again? Yes. Am I happy with my purchase? Overall yes but disappointed about having extra cabling for charging the laptop.

title: game changer for comfort. Mouse and Arm are in natural alignment, instant relief
text: This is my first ergonomic mouse. Wish I had tried one way before now. I dont know what my hold up was, maybe because they looked weird and uncomfortable, haha. Well I know what prompted me to try one: my wrist has been bothering me, and it seemed to be made worse with mouse use. I guess all my gaming years have added up. I didnt know which to try. So many! I didnt know what would fit. There are some really small looking ones and some that look bulky, some that are nearly same in appearance as a standard mouse, and some that have a deeper cut out where hand rests. Very confusing. So I just spent some time reading through various reviews of a wide assortment of ergonomical mice. While I think some of it is trial and error for getting the right fit, I think I happened upon a really good fit and feel with my first try. I really have nothing to compare it to, but so far I am really quite pleased. --I dont have small hands, but they aren't exactly big either. Size can be in length and width. I have quite small fingers, but wide palms (3.75"). So typically a size "large" in gloves, but the ends of the fingers always dangle quite a bit. Hope that gives impression going forward with my review: --My hand easily grasps the width of this mouse. Feels very comfortable. My pointer finger just reaches the scroll wheel. I dont need to reach for it; my finger pad sits directly on top of apex of the wheel. --Press of left or right side is easy and comfortable. Plenty of length to accommodate even the smallest of fingers. Yet plenty of spare room for fingers longer than mine. -- Not a loud click, but I can feel the click. I personally find that satisfying. Gentle yet makes me feel my action. --Scrolling is smooth. --No stuttering or jerkiness --Instant connect when I first got this, and no problems since --Impressed with how natural it feels. Didnt realize how much the wrist and forearm twist to use a standard mouse. With this, the mouse and arm are in a natural alignment --Charging is quick and easy. Comes with cord for charging. I charge it directly through use of my laptop. It can be used as a corded mouse with ease. Can store dongle in the mouse if need be. Has on/off switch. When in the ON position, it will go to sleep when computer shuts down (or after x amount of time when not used), and wake up along with computer. Typical for rechargeable mouse. If this is anything like any other rechargeable mouse I have had, it should hold a charge for a very long time. --Haven't used this for gaming yet as I have it for use on my laptop, but may try it out on my gaming PC. If I like it, I will just buy another. I think it will work out. This is just so comfortable and easy to use. Take a little getting used to, yet at same time feels really natural. Good grip, good responsiveness, all around good experience in the short time I have had it. Your experience may vary in terms of fit, but I think the quality is evident. I will update if I have anything else worth sharing :) EDIT/UPDATE: Seriously love this mouse, and this company is great. They are VERY RESPONSIVE and helpful when I inquired about an issue I had. WOW really good customer service. Rare these days, thank you. Truly, 5 STARS ALL AROUND.

This will assist in backing up the boat around obstructions. Also I'll be able to see those folks who pull behind the boat and don't take the hint when the trailer backup lights come on. Hopefully it will assist with lane changes in traffic for the same reason. The van has a significant blind spot on the passenger side due to the door pillar. Putting the camera on the rear side of the van gives me a good view in that direction. This pictured mounting assembly is the prototype. I will update with the final assembly of the camera and the install of monitor as well. Update: 3.10.21 added a pic of the view of our boat behind our van. It will help greatly with some of the challenging boat ramps we encounter. Curves and obstructions can be challenging with just mirrors. The sideview has helped several times in crowded parking lots. Nothing like speed demons who think they can sneak by a van backing out.

title: Fantastic Camera for a Very Reasonable Price
text: I have always liked the video footage taken by bikers as they barrel down a mountain trail or through a wooded area on their bikes (or the crazy base jumpers wanting to capture the drop). It gives a really good sense of what the rider was seeing when riding and you catch every bump in the road, every acceleration and deceleration. I have never used on of these little cameras before now so I was very excited to have a chance to evaluate one of these cameras. So far I am very happy with it and it works well, though I have learned that those with big hands and fingers will struggle a little bit with it. This is not just a camera, it is a complete kit with the camera, a waterproof housing, cables, mounting brackets, double-sided adhesive pads - everything you need to use this little camera in just about any outdoor activity you might want to capture. I will write mostly about the camera but will touch on a couple of the accessories as well. Except for cameras designed to be hidden and concealed this is the smallest and lightest camera I have ever seen or used. This is of course required for a device like this that is intended to be used on the go. It very easily can be slipped in a pocket or a bag or purse. It comes packaged in the hard plastic frame that is the waterproof case but even this case is small enough to fit in a purse or smaller bag. I will say this - read the included manual! The manual is not perfect but it is better than what comes with most electronics and it gives a very good rundown on how to use this little camera. I had no clue at all what to do with this camera until I looked through the manual. I suspected it was touch-screen and I was right. Even though the screen is touch screen and easy to navigate the interface is only semi-intuitive. It is also complicated by the fact that there is an anti-scratch cover on the screen for shipping and if you do not peel this off the screen is a lot less responsive. This is where I had the most trouble - trying to work around the plastic sheet over the screen when I didn’t really even notice it before. This camera has a lot of the same capabilities of a good phone camera. You can take still pictures, video and also play back that which you have recorded. The camera can snap stills up to 12MP resolution and the video recording can go all the way up to 1080P. These equate to very good image quality when used properly. There are a million little settings for these modes too, from white balance, ISO, anti-shake, motion detection and a lot more. Many of these will be familiar to anyone who uses their digital camera for anything more than just casual shots. In testing my images and videos came out ok. I am still learning how to balance all of the settings on my big camera so I am no expert, but what I was able to produce was acceptable, particularly the video. I did manage to get the date set correctly and displayed in the corner of the video, I got the lighting right and color straight. I spent too much time playing with the zoom and I learned I am very adept at shooting videos of my hands. This is a rechargeable device and a standard micro USB cable is provided for charging. I ran into one issue with the camera and it related to the fact that I have huge hands and the camera is so small. I found that the only way I personally can work the touch screen is to use a stylus. Using my fingers I am unable to pinpoint the spot on the screen I want to touch and I got very frustrated trying to get certain things done on the screen. If you have really big hands, consider using a stylus on the screen. There is no storage included in the package. The camera takes a microSD card so you will need one to save any pictures or recordings. The waterproof case is a perfect fit for this camera and it seals wonderfully. It snaps closed without effort and it keeps the water out. It is supposedly good for submersion up to 30m (according to the description). I have not had an opportunity to test this. There are a number of mounting solutions in the kit, including mounting screws for attaching to a selfie stick, bicycle frame/handlebars and helmets. In the case of the bike mount, it holds very tightly and a good series of bumps is not enough to rattle it loose. Having never had a sport camera like this I have no basis of comparison but so far I am happy with this little camera. It is very compact and lightweight and it does a great job of taking pictures and video (though it is obviously not a full replacement for a dedicated point-and-shoot or a dedicated video camera). It’s inexpensive too (a LOT less expensive than GoPro) so I think it’s a great option for someone wanting to capture their adventures. Disclaimer: I was provided a sample of this product in exchange for a fair, honest and thorough review. Even though I received this product at no cost I have rated and reviewed this product without bias, based solely on my experience with it.

text: OnePlus already has standard buds which are more expensive than these. I've tried those and was fairly impressed with them for the price compared to other leading brands (*cough* airpods). I'll be honest, I wasn't sure what to expect here with these "Nord" brand ones. Nord is supposed to be OnePlus' value brand, and the price reflects that. I've tried out headphones that cost more than this which I didn't care for, so I was a little anxious. However, I have to admit I'm fairly impressed. They sound better to me than other wireless earbuds at this price range. While they aren't as well made as the standard Oneplus buds, they aren't bad at all, especially for the money. Much like the Nord phones themselves, Oneplus seems to know how to make a value product that punches way above its price tag. I know the brand has taken a hit lately with their Android 12 offerings (still holding out for OOS13 to fix this colorOS mess they are in), but I can say with confidence that their earbuds are pretty great and worth considering whether or not you are a fan of the brand.

title: Not using it in my expensive laptop! Cheap, crimped, ill fitting USB-C connector.
text: This review is for the 7-in-1 Type C Adapter with 4K USB C to HDMI,USB C Charging Port,1 USB 3.0 Port,2 USB 2.0 Ports. I have never had an issue inserting a USB-C cable into my MacBook Pro computer before. I had to force it a bit. Not a good fit and when I compared it to my other USB-C plugs and when I compared it with well made plugs the problem is obvious. The other plugs are slimmer and smaller with clean edges. No crimping or scalloping on the inside. I included pics of this alongside another USB-C connector for comparison. My other plug has the same standardized fit and smooth finish to match OEM quality. This one is oversized with crimping on the inside edges. Quite a big difference. While I had it plugged in, which I regret, I inserted my original OEM Apple USB-C charging cable in the single USB-C slot to see if it would fit or charge. It was an awkward fit and it did not charge. I then plugged this hub into another cheap hub as a piggyback and it fit okay, however, I will not plug this directly into my laptop again due to the poor fit.

For example - I have my sleeve set up with the printer, a lamp and a shredder in the home office, but only two items can be plugged in at once. title: Allowed me to add a 2009 TV to a 2008 laptop via HDMI
text: This device connects to the VGA port and from there connects to a monitor or TV HDMI port, It WILL NOT work in the other direction, and it requires a source of 5V 1A power (any standard phone charger will work.) It comes with a USB charging cable and a 3.5mm cable for transmitting sound. Apparently it will not send sound interlaced with the HDMI signal through the HDMI cable (that you will need to supply.) For me this is not an issue because I am using it on a legacy laptop to send video only to a legacy TV, which I am using as extended video under Windows 7. Yes, it's using some ancient gear. But that is why I rated it five stars. Limitations: even if your TV or monitor is capable of higher resolutions this is not going to provide more than 720p from an old laptop or PC to an old monitor or TV. I know the specs say that this will go higher, but I could not get it there. Yet, going straight VGA from my T400 to my Sanyo TV it went into higher resolution as an extended screen. I am OK with the limitation because I am only using the TV as a second screen with MS Office, On an old Windows 7 system it's truly plug and play. I had it up and running without the scant instructions in mere minutes. For me it is a perfect solution.

title: Good price, functionality and features are as described

This means I have to remove the case in order to use the drive. I'm thinking about cutting out the bottom of the case to resolve this. EDIT: The drive doesn't function properly. I copied 400mb of mp3 music to it. None of the songs would play using the drive's internal player or a 3rd party player. Included in the music were 2 folders with additional mp3 files. The folders copied over, then after the transfer was complete, there was nothing in the folders. The password protection is also incredibly easy to crack because it's not tied to the device, but to the app. Just delete the app, then reinstall it and you have access to everything on the drive. You get what you pay for. Buyer beware.

title: Wobbly stand, distorted screen cover, and difficult to install
text: [[VIDEOID:3d500923c5dfe390b9d3648dffb22719]] This took a while to get apart without breaking anything. Despite the instructions (see photo) suggesting that disassembly start with the long side, I found that the short sides were the only ones that would separate first, then followed by the long sides. But I did eventually get it apart. FWIW - i would not call the front panel a screen protector as it appears to be just a thin piece of very pliable plastic. Unfortunately, the screen cover is very loose and does not lie flat, which results in a very garbled and distorted view (see photo and video).
