title: This is docking station for the RGB crowd, but what niche does it fill?
text: The WD_Black 1TB D50 Game Dock has quality written all over it. Metal construction for heat dissipation, thunderbolt support, nice RGB lighting, and a fast NVMe drive. It is quite obviously geared toward the PC Gaming crowd, but which segment? The build of this product screams high-quality gaming accessory. Its outer design is reminiscent of a cargo container and its collection of ports are impressive. Each USB port is well labeled with its speed (4x 10Gb/s Type-A and 2x 10Gb/s Type-C), two thunderbolt ports, one of which outputs 87 watts of power, one displayport, one network port (gigabit), and one 3.5mm headphone jack. Add to this, the 1TB high-performance NVMe drive. All in one sleek-looking, compact container (pun intended). It is obvious that this dock is geared toward the Gaming Laptop crowd, whether its for gaming or for video/graphics production work, but I find that it really doesn't make sense for me. In my tests, this product does not work without the power adapter (it really should be able to power itself through the thunderbolt port). This means an additional outlet that's drawing power at all times. This would make sense if gaming laptops can actually be powered and charged from the 87w thunderbolt port but I have not seen a gaming pc with discreet graphics that actually accepts anything in that range. So what other advantage would this product provide? External storage through a fast Thunderbolt connection. At $450, this product provides 1TB of additional storage. Which 2TB NVMe drives available for much less, I would opt to upgrade my internal storage to 2TB instead. Then that leave the docking station. The ports are all of the high quality and your monitor, network, keyboard, mouse, headset, and speakers can be connected via a single thunderbolt connection. This is convenient, but I don't think this justifies the price as there are many docks out there that work just as well, albeit through a USB 3.1 connection instead. WD has really done well to package a lot into this dock but is charging a premium for it. Personally, I think that there are just better ways of achieving equal or better results when it comes to upgrading a gaming laptop. **update** Coming back to think about it some more, I realized exactly what niche WD was going for: Macbooks. Some Macbooks have only two thunderbolt ports and all of them are not easily upgradeable so this product does indeed provide a value to those consumers. But then does it make sense to spend an additional 35% of the price of the laptop (Macbook Pro 13" is $1299) to address problems that Apple introduced into their laptops? I guess if you're already in their ecosystem, it's par for the course. That's not to say Apple is the only one. Microsoft has made their products impossible to upgrade as well, but none of these are actually gaming laptops.

title: Durable High Performance Speaker- Even Coworker Has One!
text: Having outdoor safe speakers is essential for my household. Not only do I have audiophiles who are addicted to headphones and speakers, I have a husband who insists on taking his speakers to outdoor jobs. that is fine except when the job turns out to have a sudden rainstorm or high dust going on. Before giving it to him, I had our daughter put it through the grind at work (food prep) and in town events. Let's see how this speaker performs.. WHAT IS THIS SPEAKER LIKE? This speaker is gray in color. The housing is very rubbery in consistency. The right side of the speaker has sealed controls under a rubberized pad over top of the buttons. The speaker stays put and is heavier than expected. ON/OFF BUTTON/PAIRING Holding down for over a second powers up the speaker. Next to the power button is the PAIRING button. This is not for pairing the speaker to a device but it is for pairing with another speaker just like it. A coworker with my daughter has this speaker and they tested it out- works GREAT so you now get two synced speakers. CALLS BUTTON You can send and receive calls with this button. You can also access your voice commands on your phone with this button. VOLUME BUTTON This is plus or minus sound controls. You can also skip by holding down- + is forward, - is backward. PAUSE/PLAY You can control pausing and playing with this button. DOCKING USB for charging, mini USB port (to use to charge a device if need be), and auxiliary outlet for like an old MP3 player or other device that does not have Bluetooth capability. CHARGING INDICATOR LIGHT Red when charging and turns white when fully charged. Will indicate when the power is running low so you will know. SOUND QUALITY Sound quality with this is very good. My daughter has a coworker with the same speaker so they both have had a chance to experiment with their speakers. So far it has been a great speaker. The balance of the recording of course affects the speaker, but so far it has not been overly muffled, out of balance, etc. Where you put the speaker affects how it sounds too. When put on the floor, it really does sound better than on, say top of a box, or couch, etc. A very hard surface seems to really make it sound best. VOLUME When using the volume controls on the speaker, the sound is better than when you use the volume controls on the device (tablet, phone, etc). DURABILITY This speaker was taken to a huge town chicken and biscuit dinner and was dropped and still is working perfectly. BATTERY Two 10 hour shifts at full volume, and this speaker kicked through it all before needing to be recharged. THE BOTTOM LINE For a really nice durable speaker that is dustproof, shock proof, and supposedly water proof, this is a good choice. It is solid, has decent sound , and even can be synced with another speaker if you get two of these for coverage around a room. MY RATING FIVE STARS. This is a decent speaker, and I feel more comfortable having my husband use this on his jobs than the other really nice speaker he was beating up.

title: A great multi-function USB-C hub for my Samsung Galaxy Tab S6
text: This USB-C hub worked well with my Samsung Galaxy Tab S6. I didn't have any issues using it. Pluses 1. Great build quality 2. Aluminum case 3. Braided USB-C cable 4. 3x USB-A ports, 1x SD & Micro SD ports and 1x 4k/60p HDMI port 5. Plug and play; no drivers needed Minuses 1. No USB power pass-through to charge a mobile device while using the hub 2. The USB hub draws power from the device it's connected to, limiting the number of devices that can be connected & used at any given time I was able to plug the USB hub directly to my Samsung Galaxy Tab S6's USB-C. I connected a 32GB flash drive, a 128GB MicroSD card and a HDMI cable to the hub. The hub was instantly recognized by the tablet & showed the drives connected to it. I could browse the contents of both drives using Samsung's My Files app. Once I connected the hub to my Vizio PX 4k HDTV, the tablet's display was mirrored to the TV. Movies, TV shows and streaming videos from popular websites all played back flawlessly. There were 2 downsides found while using the hub. The first one was not being able to charge my tablet while using the hub. The second one deals with using an unpowered hub such as this with mobile devices that provide limited power to their USB port. While the flash drive & SD cards worked fine during my use, I can see where higher power draw input sources can fail. My tablet only provides so much power, so it can't provide enough power for sources like unpowered external hard drives.

title: Superb Sound
text: I spend a lot of time wearing headphones, usually for noise cancellation but also for listening to music. I was unaware that Monster made headphones (I have favored Sennheiser for many, many years) and I was eager to try the NCredible NTune phones and to compare them with the best set of headphones I own - a pair of Sennheiser PXC-450 noise canceling headphones. The Monster phones compared extremely well - much better than I ever imagined. I will just refer to these as Monster phones from here forward, for simplicity. The Monster phones are very lightweight though they seem to be quite durable. I was a little concerned when I took them out of the box because they look small and flimsy, but they are actually well made and I believe they will stand up to daily use quite well. The styling is very aesthetically pleasing as well, with very smooth contours and dark colors that don't "pop". These are on-ear phones, not over-the-ear phones and this is important to remember. Most on-ear phones tend to be slightly uncomfortable to me, either fitting not quite right or having hard pads that eventually get uncomfortable after a period of time. The Monster phones don't give me the same issue, though, and are quite comfortable. The pads are soft enough to counter the smaller can size. I did a side-by-side comparison of the Monster and Sennheiser phones for sound (not considering the noise cancellation feature of the Sennheiser cans). I was absolutely floored - the Monster headphones simply sound better than the Sennheiser phones which cost almost 3 times as much. The difference is in the midrange. I have always been pleased with the sound the Sennheiser cans deliver across the spectrum, but when I played the same track through the Monster phones after listening to the Sennheiser phones I was stunned with how much fuller and richer the music sounded. The Monster phones are advertised as having good bass and this is absolutely true. What is not advertised, though, is that the highs and midrange are also excellent. It is hard to find a good set of phones that get all three ranges right, especially in this price range. I subjected the Monster phones to a very brutal critic - my teenage son, who spends more time listening to music than I do. He loves all kinds of music, but he especially loves metal and techno. It did not take him long at all to rave about the Monster phones and how good they sound. Coming from him, this is very high praise. Other reviewers have commented that these phones are a little overpriced. I cannot say that I agree with that. These are good headphones and are, in my experience, better than average. In fact, depending on what you're looking for, these can outperform much more expensive phones. I primarily use active noise canceling phones in very noisy environments, but these Monster phones are great for quieter settings.

title: Low price to be sure...But if you would like to know what that price gets you in quality and reliability read on.
text: Well, I was asked to share my thoughts on this product. I will give you an excerpt from the most prominent product review company group out there..."A number of secondary brands had reliability records comparable to the major brands, but one—Sceptre—was significantly more problem-prone." I moved to LG (quality on par with Samsung as far as ratings and reliability are concerned) but at a lower price than Samsung. Sony beats Samsung in the ratings from the above mentioned product reviewer but just barely, and at a far higher price point. So for price, satisfaction, and reliability stick with LG first and Samsung second. Sony is higher priced but that is because of added features. This proves to be the case in all screen sizes. Lastly, as specifically regards the 32 inch class televisions you are looking at now, a final quote from the reviewer: "Vizio, Toshiba, and Sharp—appear to offer a nice balance of price and performance, but you may not have to pay much more for an LG, which scored better." As an side, TCL does poorly in the 32" class, but moves to the middle of the pack for sets in the 39" to 43" and 46"to 52" classes. As most all Amazon shoppers know, an extended warranty is offered by a third party company on items such as this. One might think they could just purchase the cheap set with a poor frequency of repair record and then be taken care of by purchasing an extended warranty at checkout. It must be known that regardless of whatever brand you pick in the 32" class, if something goes wrong you will packing up the TV and sending it off for repairs. In-home repairs do not generally come into play until your set is 39" or larger. Buy right the first time. Happy shopping!!!

title: Belkin Cinema Leather Folio Case
text: This is a reasonably attractive leather case. About a third of the cover on the spine side has a pebbled texture while the rest of the cover is smooth. The Good: I really like Belkin's four corner mounting system. It's very secure and doesn't interfere at all with using the tablet. The inner material is a soft micro-fiber that's gentle on your screen. It doesn't add a lot of weight to an already heavy tablet. There are a plethora of positions the tablet can be stood in. But...(see below) A stylus loop is included. The Not-So Good: If mounted in the case so you can use the rear camera through the camera cut-out all the controls as well as the 3.5mm headphone jack are exposed. This also means that to charge your tablet you have to leave the cover open or pull your tablet out and mount it backwards to access the charging port and protect the screen at the same time. The front cover has 20 very shallow grooves pressed into the micro-fiber material of the lining. These catch the bottom edge of the back cover and hold it in place for viewing. These actually hold the tablet in place reasonably well but because of the soft folding 'hinge' design the table is very bouncy at any angle. Good for viewing not so good for typing or playing games. The case material is thin, 4mm on the front cover and thicker on the back because the stand system. There's also a grooved 'hinge' as part of the front cover which lets the cover flex. This reduces the protection already marginal case material can provide for the screen. Because of the Cinema design the front cover can't be folded over flat against the back for comfortable use while off a table. The elasticized strap that holds the cover closed is flimsy and will certainly stretch out over time. Cover 'creep' happens very easily on this model. When strapped closed the cover can, because it's 'hinged', slide up exposing the screen. There's a notch cut into the back cover that allows you to flip the cover around and stand the tablet up at a high angle. This position is very unstable and I would never use it. I wouldn't recommend this cover. It just doesn't offer enough protection for an expensive tablet.

title: Decent Product for Appropriate Location
text: I have had the opportunity to test and review several of these TV antenna sets over the last several years and I have been amazed by the advances in the technology. While a few years ago the only way possible to receive decent signals in my particular location was to have a roof mounted antenna. Thankfully as technology has advanced that has become less necessary, but we are not yet to a point where topography is not an issue. The 120 miles listed in the product listing on this item may be accurate but I can only assume that would be in a perfectly flat area with few obstructions. In my testing the area was around half that listed. Broadcasts beyond approximately 45 miles suffered loss in quality with those over 60 being severe. This is a decent antenna system especially at this price point. For those living in relatively flat areas and where broadcasts are within a 60 mile radius this will be an outstanding product.

title: Excellent Replacement for the Fuji-Branded Battery
text: I needed a second battery for my Fuji X-T3 and I really wasn't excited by the price of the NP-W126S battery, so I looked for third-party replacements. After scraping user forums online and doing some additional research I settled on this one from Nitecore. It has proven to be every bit as good as Fuji brand batteries, and in some ways it's a little better. I knew Nitecore as a manufacturer of flashlights and other light sources in particular. I was previously unaware of their batteries, so I was pleasantly surprised to discover that they had a battery that was an exact fit for my camera. It does fit perfectly, too - pops in and out without fuss and fits the charger without issue. One of the issues with third-party batteries is that the camera will sometimes complain about it and throw up a message about a non-standard battery. I was initially concerned that this one would give me that issue but it has not - my camera accepts it without complaint and it just works. I have been working with it for several months now, and it seems to actually last longer than the original battery that came with my camera (an actual NP-W126S battery). I have fully charged it one time, and since then I have taken well over 200 pictures with my camera (probably closer to 450) and it still has roughly 80% of the charge which is a big improvement. The thing that seems to drain my Fuji battery fastest is powering the camera on and off; this Nitecore battery seems to handle those cycles better. It has overcharge protection and it stops charging when it reaches full capacity, even on the Fuji charger. I was concerned about using it on the original charger and considered getting the Nitecore charger to go with this battery, but it's perfectly happy on the Fuji charger. I like the harder shell on this battery as compared to the original Fuji battery, Eventually this battery will fail; when it does, I am optimistic that the hard shell will keep it from swelling quickly so I can get it out of the camera when it finally starts to fail. I am very happy with this replacement battery and based on my experience with it I can heartily recommend it. The camera doesn't complain about it, it has some built-in overcharge protection and it has a longer duty cycle than the original NP-W126S that came with my camera. When this one eventually fails, I'll get another one.

title: Disappointing. Alexa's voice sounds awful.

Well, friends, it does not sound as good. I don't know if I have a bum unit — I don’t think I do — but Alexa's voice sounds awful. Podcasts are fine, and music is fine — not great, but fine, and so much better than her voice, which is why I don't think it's a bum unit. Alexa's voice is so muddy it's almost un-understandable. I'm returning it. I'll just get a new Bluetooth speaker to go with my old Dot.

text: I originally got this unit so I could transmit video or presentations to a larger screen for more people to be able to view. Sounded simple enough, so I could not wait to get it. The whole process has been a pain. At the time of the order, I saw no reference at all to the fact that I would have to buy an adapter for this unit. I had to spend the $ and then had to wait for it to come in the mail. I got the CNE70196 High Quality Black DisplayPort Male to HDMI Cable Male 6ft/2M as well as the micro port adapter so I could try it from my tablet as well. After all, I planned to use this so why not? Yeah, right. Once the adapters arrived, I set out to test the unit. I tried to download the drivers from the online site. No dice. They didn't work at all. Then I got the disc that came with the unit and tried to put the driver on from there and software. Well, after all of this, I can say at this point of trying and trying to make this work, neither I or my fellow resident techie fanatic can get this to work on any laptop in the house. We also can not get it to work with a tablet as there is not software for it. title: Perfect TV Box control
text: There are some incredibly detailed and helpful reviews of this keyboard, so I will stick to how I am using the review sample that I received, and a few of my impressions. First, this is not a bluetooth device (it was described as such to me when I was asked to review it.) The only weak point is the documentation, which is terse, but well illustrated. Had it contained specs and compatibility data it would have been significantly more useful. However, this is not a major flaw. I first tested it with an ancient ThinkPad T61 running Windows XP. It was immediately recognized and functional. My next test was to see if it would work with my Samsung Galaxy Note 3 phone running Android 4.4.2. I connected with wireless dongle via a [[ASIN:B00C7SK2J2 Micro USB OTG Host Cable to USB 2.0 Adapter]] and it worked. To be honest, that arrangement is a bit on the clumsy side for me. My final test and where this keyboard shines is when I connected it to my [[ASIN:B00JA5DCNS Keedox® Quad Core RK3188 Android 4.2 Smart TV BOX]] running Android 4.2. This keyboard made a lot of sense connected to that box because I rarely used it prior. I was using a corded keyboard and it was a trip hazard. Plus most of in interaction with it included few keystrokes, so this mini keyboard is perfect. In fact I find myself using the TV box a lot more frequently now that I have this keyboard. I think of it as a smart remote control - which it is - and have quickly grown to love it. As for prolonged typing, this would not be my favorite solution. However, that is subjective. I hated my Blackerry when I was required to carry one back in my consulting days too. But for occasional use this is wonderful. Bottom line: I would have never guessed that this would be the missing link between me and my TV box. Had I only tested this with phones and tablets I would have probably written a single word review: meh. Now, though, I see the wow factor.

title: Pretty sweet
text: It's small, smaller than I expected. The remote is small, light, and easy to handle. Picture quality is outstanding, as you would expect. The voice search feature works beautifully, and as of now has recognized perfectly everything I've said to it. Very nicely done, Amazon. The out-of-box presentation is elegant. You have to connect the power adaptor, and the HDMI cable to your TV. (That means, your HDTV.) You have to put the 2 batteries (included) into the remote, and then turn it on. Configure it for your WiFi Internet connection, and you're good to go. Amazon has already thoughtfully registered the device to your Amazon account, so there's absolutely no additional setup necessary. Why only 4 stars, and not 5? Well, the Roku gets 5 stars because of the many dozens of additional channel options with the Roku. However, all your favorites are here on the Amazon Fire, including Netflix, Hulu, Pandora, for example. Should you get the Roku vs. the Amazon Fire? You'll probably be happy with either, especially if you don't think you would bother with all the additional channels available with Roku. For the usual things, the two are essentially identical. If you need a wired Ethernet network connection, there is a Roku model with that, and the Amazon Fire (like many other boxes) uses only WiFi. However, the Amazon has the nifty voice search feature, which is a clear improvement over Roku. (And I've been a Roku fan since it first came out.)

title: If you are looking for a good AM radio, keep looking. Not well-made and performed poorly. Expected better from Sangean.
text: I returned this radio--it simply was not very good at the job I bought it to do (picking up AM radio) and was poorly made. I bought it to listen to Los Angeles Dodgers ballgames on AM570. Simply put, this radio could not hold the station. AM570 is strong and clear on most other devices, but the Sangean, despite the other reviews, seemed to have terrible reception in my apartment, even near a window (I live in an interior unit in an apartment building--windows on the courtyard). It could not hold the station well, and there was a lot of interference no matter what I tried. At no time was I able to tune into AM570 with a clear signal--far too much noise. This is the only reason I bought the radio and it's inability to pick up AM570 rendered it useless. I replaced it with the [[ASIN:B012F0DGYE Sony ICFP26 Portable AM/FM Radio (Black)]], which performs a lot better and costs about $3 more. The Sony picks up and holds AM570 in my apartment perfectly, with no noise/hiss. The Sony is clearly the better product. It also looks much nicer--the Sangean SR-35 looks and feels like a cheap piece of crap. The Sony is actually a far more appealing, sleeker-looking device (I wish it were a bit smaller). The second flaw was that the battery door would not stay closed. the little tab that clicks to hold it closed seemed too small or something (it was not bent). It just did not stay closed. Even if it had worked well (which it did not), this would have beeen enough to return it. it would literally have required a rubber band to stay closed. I was really disappointed with this radio--I had expected better, given Sangean's reputation. I cannot recommend this radio for AM use--get the Sony instead and save yourself the headache of returning it. (*I would like to thank Amazon for making the returns process so painless though!)

text: I don't really feel the need to criticize the keyboard since it is, and does, everything the manufacturers say, but I'm back to Amazon today to buy a different lighted keyboard and I'll keep this one for emergencies. First, the positives. The keyboard is sleek and visually appealing. I love the blue back-lighting. It would probably be a good keyboard to take along for use with a laptop, and that may have been the intent because the condensed keyboard is laid out like a laptop keyboard. That, unfortunately, is also part of the problem. The layout of the keys makes it difficult for me to use, with keys like the backspace, which I use a lot, surrounded by other small keys such as Home & End, which sends your cursor all over the page when you hit them by mistake. It's probably not the best keyboard for people who do a lot of typing. In fact, I plugged in my old keyboard to write this review. For the price, I am not displeased, and it will be a good standby in case I need it, but for long-term use, a different keyboard is probably a better choice.

title: Excellent features & performance, with some problems
text: Installation is easy and performance is solid. There are some nice features, such as the ability to use this router as an access point that can bridge systems operating under the older 802.11b standard (11 mbps), as well as the blazingly fast 802.11g standard. It also comes with the usual features: DHCP server, NAT, and firewall. I also like the fact that the wired ports are based on switching instead of shared as a hub. However, there is one major aspect of this router that you need to consider before purchasing - there is a firmware flaw (as of firmware version 2.02.7) that exposes the remote administration feature to hackers who will attempt to exploit ports 80 and 443. Worse, turning off the remote administration feature does not close the vulnerability of ports 80 and 443. This vulnerability was reported widely by Jason Meserve in the 6/3/04 NW Virus and Bug Patch Alert, and a workaround was provided by Alan W. Rateliff in a 4/31/04 port to the Security Focus bugtrack list. The workaround is to set the port forwarding to send ports 80 and 443 to non-existent hosts. Rateliff further noted that forwarding the ports to any hosts (including listening ones if you are actually running servers) will override the default behavior. He also recommended immediately changing the default admin and password before connecting this router on a live network. There is another issue with Linksys in general, and that is the quality of their technical support, which I consider to be poor-to-mediocre, depending on how lucky you are when dealing with them. Overall, this router has a lot of excellent features and performs very well. The downside is the security issue cited above and my opinion that they have less than great technical support. Therefore, if you are a highly technical user and can perform the workaround described above, and can solve most common problems without needing Linksys' tech support you'll probably like it. However, if you are a non- or quasi-technical user who wants something that works out of the box and does not expose your network to security problems, you may want to look elsewhere.

title: Acceptable Television, but Seriously Outdated
text: Sanyo DS25390 is a simple, no- frills television that offers enough features to get by but is lacking in some of the basics that we now take for granted in television sets, making it a questionable choice of sets for many consumers. Starting with the remote control, this television is very limited and quite out of date. Besides the usual keys (the ten numbered buttons, power button, etc.), you get a set- up key, CC key (closed captioning), Enter, and a Previous Channel selector. The various settings for the television can be accessed from the remote using the menu button, and they include the usual options (changing brightness, color, etc.). There isn't much more to it than that and the control itself has an old- fashioned look to it. Picture quality on Sanyo DS25390 is okay, but nothing exceptional. The sharpness is the greatest weakness of this Sanyo television. Depending on your distance from the screen, the images seem a little fuzzy on the edges. At a good distance, this problem is minimized well enough that most people will not notice but it is definitely not as sharp as the picture on most newer models. Picture quality is most important, but one other important consideration when choosing any television is its features and friendliness to accessories. Sanyo DS25390 has limited abilities and limited features. Chief among these is the limits on input. There are only a few input and output jacks and, because of this, there is no way to directly hook up a DVD player or video camera. This fact alone would probably discourage most people from considering this television. You could probably connect a DVD player or other device by going through a VCR first, but how many among is still own a VCR? I realize this television set was considered acceptable during its day, but it is difficult to overlook things like this. One other problem I have with this television is the sound quality. There is only one small speaker, so the sound delivered is in mono. This certainly reduces the sound quality when watching certain movies or music video. The sound is acceptable for most television shows because the need for stereo quality isn't important. But it isn't very good for certain films and other programs that are greatly enhanced with stereo sound. Overall, Sanyo Model DS25390 is a difficult television set to evaluate. It seems adequate in some ways and it was probably considered a good television set when it was first introduced. But compared to modern televisions, I cannot imagine anyone buying this set. The fact that a DVD player cannot be directly hooked up to this television is reason enough to reject it. Add to that the picture quality and its lack of sharpness and you have a television set that is too flawed to recommend. It's inexpensive, yes. But you most certainly get what you pay for.

title: Stand Holds iPad Securely at Many Comfortable Viewing Angles
text: This BEANPAD FOR iPAD is my absolute favorite iPad 2 stand (and I've tested several). First, it's nearly impossible to knock over or drop. Second, it can be set to almost any comfortable viewing angle. And third, it securely supports your iPad pretty much anywhere you can set it down. To use this stand, you insert your iPad (2 or 3) into the white holder. The holder grips firmly but gently, and easily releases the iPad whenever you like. With the iPad in the holder, the beanbag conforms to any surface (a knee, a table, a desktop, a chair arm), and makes a secure base so that the stand cannot suddenly collapse. The gray beanbag is about the size of a large cantaloupe, and is just heavy enough that it cannot be easily knocked over or knocked off a table. The stand's viewing angle can be adjusted to an almost limitless number of positions. The white holder itself can be rotated so that the iPad is held in a vertical or horizontal position. Then the actual viewing angle can be adjusted by squishing the gray beanbag to find the most comfortable viewing position. I purchased my BEANPAD FOR iPAD from Woot! for the bargain price of $25 (including shipping). However, if something happened to mine, I would replace it, because the BEANPAD FOR iPAD allows me to use my iPad comfortably just about anywhere in my entire home.

title: easy to use, nice clamshell design, but a little bit too flexible.
text: The Samsers Wireless Portable Folding Keyboard is only 1/2" thick when folded, and of course, half that when undfolded. It is quite thin and portable. It caught my attention because it supports pairing with up to 3 bluetooth devices simultaneously and with many of us having multiple devices (phone and tablet is quite normal), these devices are great for someone like me. The clamshell design means that the keys are protected when closed. It also holds itself closed magnetically, and powers on my simply opening it. Pairing is easy: hold the function key and press one of the Q,W, or E keys to select one of the three bluetooth slots, then press the big "connection" button on the left bezel of the keyboard. This all seems pretty slick to me but I did have one concern with this keyboard's design and that is the hinge system. When the keyboard is open, it is quite easy to twist it with just a little bit of pressure. It means that I'm always extra careful when I'm taking it out of my bag. In terms of functionality, the keys are a the same size as the ones on my laptop and has the same amount of travel as well, although they are a little bit louder than my laptop's keys when typing. I'm using this keyboard to type this review right now. What I found is that the keys are a little bit unresponsive. I keep missing letters every few words I type. Just in this last sentence, I had to go back about 5 times to correct missing letters. This defeats the purpose of purchasing a keyboard to type more quickly on a mobile device. Perhaps if you are normally heavy-handed when you type on a keyboard, you wouldn't face this issue. Something that I wish this keyboard had is a touch pad. If this device can be both a keyboard and mouse, it would be much more useful. Overall, the Samsers Wireless Portable Folding Keyboard is a good device but ultimately, it has a couple of shortcomings that keep it from being great.

title: Quality construction and good design for my use
text: I use this to adapt my [[ASIN:B0047T79VS Belkin F8Z492TTP Bluetooth Music Receiver]] to the input jack on my [[ASIN:B0036B8A20 Kustom The Defender 5H Class A Guitar Amp Head]]. This is a power-saving solution I discovered for listening to music through the amp in my music room versus firing up my power-hungry stereo system. I then play music via my bluetooth-enabled phone. It's a kludgy solution that keeps my power bill down. It also shows how this adapter can work in one of an almost limitless number of situations. What I most like about this adapter is it is designed around a block with no dangling wires like, for example, a [[ASIN:B000068O4W Hosa YPR102 TRS Male To Dual RCA Female Y Cable]]. The key advantage is this adapter will not break at the weakest point - the wires. In the past I have had the wires on the Hosa-style adapters go bad or even break due to frequent handling. Every time you insert male RCA jacks into a Hosa-style adapter you are putting stress on the wires, which will eventually break down internally. In some cases the result is a noisy connection with intermittent static, but in others the signal is lost due to an internal break in the cable. The design of this adapter has none of the above mentioned points of failure. However, it does have one disadvantage that the Hosa-style does not: it will not fit into cramped places. Since I am plugging mine directly into the front of an amplifier the design does not matter. If you were to use it as an adapter to an amp or mixer that connects at the rear, you may run into space problems. Also, my amp connection does not have space constraints to the left or right, so, again, the design is perfect for me. For connections that have knobs, or other components close to the 1/4" connection, you may find that only a Hosa-style adapter will meet your needs. One final thing: while the design allows for stereo signal input at the RCA connection end, this adapter sums those signals into mono. That means that your stereo source will not be heard in stereo with this particular adapter. The Hosa model mentioned above will maintain stereo separation of the RCA signals, so if you want to maintain stereo, the Hosa is the way to go.

title: "Heavy Weight" with Solid Sound
text: The product arrived very quickly, within 4 days. When I saw the box at the doorstep and went to pick it up. was shocked at how heavy the relatively small box was. Good sign! At least I knew it wasn't made out of ' Plastic from China'. My previous Desktop 6 CD changer had long stopped working, as it would not 'read' discs as was the case with the previous one . After reading multiple reviews of multiple 'Table Top" multi-CD changers (all- in -ones) It seems that is a recurring theme. I have a few hundred CDs and already started Ripping them to hear on the phone via Bluetooth, but if I could just find a single CD player that would be reliable with Blue Tooth and perhaps even a slot for a USB stick (I could get into that too). This player seemed to be the ticket and it is. The user guide is very large with pictures, though the instructions are not 'the best', but the player really is simple enough. If you can work one in a car, then this is no different. I connected the speakers which didn't take long, the ones from my previous player, and was up and running within 5 minutes. T . The sound quality is far better than my previous player which size wise was much larger. . Actually, from the time I took it out of the box to the time I was listening to the first CD in it, was about 20 minutes (with the Blue tooth connected by then too). I shared some photos with a CD beside the player in this review so you can compare how big it is. The volume for my 2 bedroom condo is great, wouldn't need anything any louder, and it's clear sound (even with the likely very cheap speakers I have right now). So far, it's only Day 1 having it, but no complaints so far. Nicely, had just received an offer for Amazon Prime the same day, so have been using the blue tooth to take advantage of that. If you need a player that won't skip during an earthquake, this seems to be the one. I tried playing a CD recorded using Windows Media Player, a variety of song copied down from YouTube put to CD, and the CD worked fine. Was also able to insert a USB stick with Music copied to it from the computer and with the help of the Very Rapid Response Customer Service was enabled to work through a 'glitch' on my end in regard to how the player reads music stored on the USB device (which happens to be , 'a separate folder for each ripped CD' )

title: Fits my iPad Pro 9.7 (A1674) perfectly ...
text: An attractive case -- I tested the Rose Gold version -- nicely made with no obvious flaws. I've been trying various types of external keyboards as I like to use my iPad on trips and blog as I go. Having a full keyboard makes life a lot easier. This is the first integrated case/keyboard I've tried, and so far, I like it. It's easy to set up -- charge it (a cable is provided), pop in the iPad, turn on, pair, and you're set to go. The backlight is fully adjustable - brightness as well as color. That's a nice touch. Battery life seems to be excellent -- I've used it 2-3 hours every day for the past 10 days, and it's still going strong and it still hasn't indicated I need to recharge it. The keyboard size is a bit smaller than my MacBook Air laptop, but after a few minutes, I got used to the slightly tighter quarters and had no issues at all. Getting it into the case is easy; getting it out of the case takes a little more effort. When I take it along on my trips, I'll leave it in the case for the duration. My only complaint is that it's quite heavy but given the battery capacity, that's probably not too surprising. So the trade-off is a slightly heavier case with LONG battery life, or a keyboard-only solution with much shorter battery life. I think in the end, I'll appreciate the longer battery life and fully integrated case/keyboard. I'll continue to use this over the next few months, and plan to take it on my next couple of trips. If all goes well, it will come along on a 131-day world cruise early next year.

title: This cable is definitely the pits.
text: My use for this cable was to just get video between my laptop and my 40" TV. I use it for Telework (Telecommuting). I always use Blue Jeans Cable and had an 8 foot Series-FE HDMI cable from BJC that served this purpose exceptionally well. I don't do gaming and I have to have this working all the time, without fail. This KabelDirekt 15 foot HDMI cable fell flat on it's face. I am no novice when it comes to computers. I thought with all those 5 stars, I'll spend $12 instead of $43.75 to get a new 15 foot HDMI cable. This KabelDirekt purchase was a complete waste. It would not sync up to the television. I decided to reboot the laptop since this was the cable's first hookup (Note: I NEVER have to reboot my laptop when I use BJC HDMI Cable. If we didn't rearrange the room such that I now needed a 15ft cable, I would have never tried to buy a cable that was not from BJC. It just pained me that I obsoleted my 8 foot BJC Series-FE HDMI cable only because we rearranged the room I use it in). I did everything I could to make the synchronization stable. EVERYTHING. If the computer slept, I lost synchronization. If the screen saver came on, I lost synchronization. I wasted time and money on this. Just to double check I wasn't insane, I temporarily moved my laptop closer to the TV so that the BJC 8 foot Series-FE HDMI cable could reach the TV. The KabelDirekt 15 foot HDMI cable was currently plugged in with the TV not able to synchronize with the laptop. I swapped out the 15 foot KabelDirekt cable with the 8 FOOT BJC cable and within the blink of an eye, the laptop was synchronized with the TV. No reboots, no configuration changes. Merely a swap out of HDMI cables. This KabelDirekt 15 foot cable is on its way to the electric recycling program. I have ordered my BJC 15 Foot Series FE HDMI cable which is now on Amazon and like all the other cables I order from BJC, I know I made the right decision.... I will NEVER stray from BJC again.

title: Great all around machine that draws low power.
text: I love this laptop. It's replacing an HP Envy that isn't even a year old yet. The ergonomic on this laptop are way better.I haven't accidentally bumped the touchpad while typing, and the keys are large with a nice feel. Performance.....well, I'm not gamer. It's doing ok on Photoshop, Lightroom and Chrome, and that's pretty much my big three. The backlit keyboard is something you'll never be able to live without once you've had it. The screen is gorgeous. It could, perhaps, be a touch brighter. The sound from the speakers is ok. No one will confuse it with a surround sound system but it's good for a laptop. The hard drive is quiet. The spinning of the one in the hP drove me mad. The USB ports are towards the back of the machine. I have not been a fan of the recent trend to move them up front as I'm forever bumping them. Great to see. Despite being larger than the HP, the power drain is MUCH lower. I live in a solar powered vehicle so this is important. Right now, with music playing and the screen at max, in high performance battery mode, I'm showing it using 17w. When I first plugged it and it wasn't fully charged yet, I saw a max draw of 29w. The HP drew almost 50w charging and 90 or better while running. I could not be happier with the Toshiba. The tablet mode thing......well, it's nice to be able to adjust the screen to any angle. Using it in tablet mode......I haven't found a lot of reasons to do that yet. It's pretty heavy and unwieldy to carry it around like an ipad at 5 pounds. But I guess it's nice to have the option. That brings me to another small quibble....it's kind of heavy for how thin it is. I was a little taken aback. I guess you can't have everything. Well, you can, but you'll pay two grand and up for it. For the $$ this is a great all around machine. I'm definitely not buying another Envy. Windows 10......boy, I was prepared to hate it. I don't. So far anyway. A lot of headaches can be avoided by setting your wifi connection to metered, then all those background updates don't happen as far as I can tell. PS The stupid stickers they put all over these things came off with little effort and no residue. I hate those things. The Intel one put up a little fight but came off relatively easily.

title: This stuff is weird, but it works- just be careful not to drop it
text: This Splat stuff is really interesting. When I first got the jar, it was filled to the top with gooey shivery bright orange goo that is the same type of thing we used to get at the toy store and throw against the wall and watch it roll down. This glob of goo, however, is supposed to be used to clean crevices in your car- speakers, dials, switches, vents, etc, that you can not easily clean otherwise. I did not use this goo right away, and almost a month later, I was surprised to see that the volume of the Splat goo went down by half. It had shrunk- and no one had taken any out either. It is a bit weird to get used to using at first too, but it quickly becomes easy to manipulate into little places you want to clean. When using it, it really does work. Little switches and indented areas that gather a lot of dirt and dust were cleaned well with no residue. The only problems I had were two: ---Don't drop it on the floor or it will grab every single stone, piece of dirt, or garbage it touches. ---Don't let it touch the windows or it will leave a smudge that you can only see at night when lights are coming at you and it can be very distracting. It does wash off relatively easily- but it takes a bit of work to get it back to the way it was. All in all, however, I like Splat. As long as I don't drop it or let it touch any windows in the car, it does a good job. It is only useable for 50 uses and that is not a whole lot- but it is biodegradable. I have not used it up yet so I can not comment on the biodegradability of Splat.

Nothing abusive, just a gentle knock while it was hanging around his neck. Junk. I've added a star because Amazon's exchange process has been completely painless, and I'm taking the second pair into consideration. The replacement binoculars arrived, and haven't broken yet. They are, however, still pretty crappy. Maybe one step above something you might find in a Happy Meal. The optics are headache inducing just like less pretentious toy binoculars and the diopter adjustment is unstable and out of adjustment (my 20/20 vision has it maxed to the positive side to work). I wasn't expecting Swarovski quality here for $20, but the favorable reviews these got here on Amazon are frankly surprising given the optical mediocrity and poor quality control. Do yourself a favor and save up for a real brand (refurbished?) for your kid's binoculars, even if it costs more (I've been pleased with the $80 Nikon Trailblazers for my older son). It's worth the extra $60 to avoid the headaches and feeling nthat you've purchased something that should have come for free in a Cracker Jack box.

title: Avoid This SSD At All Costs!
text: I installed this SSD in a laptop. When the laptop goes to sleep (or if I put it to sleep), the drive will not wake up. I have to press and hold the power button to shut off the computer. OCZ released a firmware update for the Vertex 3's that might fix this issue (among other issues) fairly quickly after the drive was released, but there is no way to upgrade the firmware on my drive. OCZ blocks firmware upgrading if the Vertex 3 SSD is the Windows Boot Drive (which it is, of course, in my laptop). OCZ also blocks upgrading the firmware if the SSD is connected to a USB to SATA adapter. So, I have no way whatsoever to upgrade the firmware. I do not own a tower where I could connect the drive to a secondary SATA controller. (And honestly, I shouldn't have to do that in the first place.) $300 and no way to update the firmware so I can fix the issues? In 2011? This is astounding, and a complete blunder on the part of OCZ, who could not care less. Avoid these OCZ Vertex 3 drives!

title: Big Thumbs Up!  Lots of power, itty bitty little space
text: I wasn't too sure how well this speaker would work when I first requested it since every portable speaker we've ever gotten has had a sort of tingy sound to it, but this portable speaker rocks! The sound quality is even better than what we get on our home speakers and I love that you can feel the 'thumps' and hear some of the background sounds in the music that really weren't noticable before. My husband's been wanting some way of listening to music out at the barn so we charged this little unit up and then headed out for the afternoon milking. We were easily able to hear the music over all the normal machinery racket - the whole time the hubby's giving me the thumbs up. He especially loves that once it's set up it will automatically connect to his bluetooth (though I could happily live without having to listen to his conversations!). It played the entire 4 hours we were out there and still had more battery life left! UPDATE: This SOLEMATE finally died - and I mean it won't charge, won't pay, nothing... so we've bought another one to replace it and the new one did not come with a standard plug in charger for an outlet. It has to be charged with a computer (not real happy with that). It's still a great speaker but miss the freedom of being able to plug it into the wall and know it's charging without having to leave a computer running. At first I couldn't figure out how to hook up the SOLEMATE to the computer, iPad or the iPod then the hubby pointed out that there's a cord underneath it that attaches to them so I'm good to go. The Bluetooth range is great as well. 33 feet I believe I read but I know my husband was able to walk the entire length of the milking parlor without having any disruption... oh and there's a convenient bag to put the SOLEMATE in so it's safe from sand and water (though I wouldn't recommend getting it wet or sandy on purpose). We had it in the bag and coveredd with paper towels. My only complaint is that the instruction manuals really aren't very thick.. just basic information - and while I don't mind learning by doing it would be much simpler and less frustrating if there was someplace I could find answers - besides reading through other reviews. The SOLEMATE works much better than I ever expected it to and I'm extremely impressed. Will definitely be using it instead of our home stereo system. It certainly sounds better!

title: About what you'd expect for the price
text: When my much more expensive Bluetooth on-ear headphones broke I was a bit frustrated and decided to go back to something cheaper. As expected, this product is less comfortable and clearly uses cheaper materials and construction, but I can't really complain given the price. The sound quality is fine as far as I can tell. The battery life is decent but noticeably shorter than my previous headphones. Also, the low battery warning is a very annoying loud series of beeps that repeats periodically until you charge it. So if you just want to listen for 5 more minutes or get through the last couple of minutes of that video and then charge it, well too bad, because the beeps are going to annoy the **** out of you until you charge it. Also, the pictures in the instructions were not clear to me at all, and it took me a while to find the charging port (you have to tilt one of the earphones to reveal the port which is otherwise hidden behind the headband). Also, unlike my previous headphones, this one does not work well with my tablet's media volume sync (when the headphones are turned on they always revert to a default volume, they don't remember the last volume used), so I had to turn sync off to avoid constantly having to recalibrate the volume of the headset relative to the tablet.

title: Small, bright but does get hot.
text: I ordered the 'SOKANI X21 Pro X21Pro Bi-Color LED Video Light Portable Pocket-Sized 3200-6500K 122 LED Bulbs OLED Screen Build-in 3000mAh Battery for DSLR Sony, Nikon, Canon, iPhone Video Vlogging and Selfies' for review. I have several LED lights similar to this one and this Sokani is the smallest and most compact of them all. With that said, it's not the brightest that I have, nor does it have RGB as some of the others I have which is sort of a con to me since I have experienced the others. However for what this light is, it is a great little LED light. The OLED screen is a great feature that not all LED lights offer. The OLED screen allows you to easily see and adjust the brightness level, color temp (3000k - 6500k), and see the battery level. The included padded soft carry case, cold shoe mount, USB type C (Yay!) charging cable, and plastic color filters all make this little LED even more valuable. The color filters are nice however they don't quite fit into the soft case without bending up the corners. This was a bit of a disappointment as they should have been sized small enough, and maybe with a little plastic case to keep them protected and together when stuffing them into the soft case. (see attached images) They attach to the LED light with the installed magnetic diffuser. This is another great feature however I have other lights that are RGB so there is no need for plastic color filters on those lights. The only cons with this little light, besides the color filters not fitting very well into the soft case, is the fact that this thing gets pretty hot after being on for a while. I don't know if this could eventually cause issues if left on for the full advertised 1.8 hours of battery life, as I usually only run it for 20-30 min at a time. Overall this is a great little light and though I have other lights in my collection, I find myself using this one quite a bit. It really is a great value.

title: performance not better despite high cost
text: I bought this to replace a 750GB WD Black hard drive in my older T61 Thinkpad. That hard drive was fairly fast (2nd place in Toms Hardware tests of HD's). Obviously this is lower capacity so I also bought a Seagate 750 HD for data storage (another fairly fast one). I was hoping that the SSD would provide a performance advantage, faster boots, and faster response times in day-to-day desktop tasks. It does not. If there is any advantage in performance it is only visible in rigorous benchmark testing or atypical massive throughput tests. In everyday desktop tasks, there is just no perceptible advantage. I can see a non-speed-performance related advantage for other uses (tablets and ultrabooks), but for my use this just hasn't proven to be worth it for double the cost of a hard drive that is 3 times greater capacity and for practical purposes just as fast.

text: Highly recommend

title: Good for seeing what’s there but that’s it
text: Using it as you normally would expect to replace the batteries every couple of months. I don’t know who is getting 2 year battery life. The view is okay, not a wide range and not really adjustable. Lasted a while about 3.5 years and then would no longer respond to motion. It did what we needed it to do - tell us who was at the door or when someone was at the garage but any farther away than that or for audio not so much. Audio would have just drained the battery faster. Probably won’t replace with another blink system.

title: The Netgear extender gives me great coverage with no hesitations
text: Fabulous Netgear Extender By Bee 12345 I have a L shaped house with the router in the West end and my TV in the South East end making it difficult to get coverage in that area. The Netgear extender gives me great coverage with no hesitations. Thank you Netgear.
